properties.name: Bennelong
properties.summary: Woollarawarre Bennelong was a Wangal man who became the early colony's foremost go-between with the British, and who remained a senior and respected Eora elder on his own Country.
body: Bennelong was a Wangal elder of the Eora and the early colony's most important go-between with the British. Seized at Manly in 1789, he turned captivity to his own purpose, learned English, and even sailed to England before coming home to the Parramatta River. Far from a man broken between two worlds, he lived out his last years as a respected elder on his own Country, where he died in 1813.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Barangaroo
properties.role: Cammeraygal fisherwoman who refused the colony
properties.place: The western harbour headland now named for her
properties.summary: Barangaroo was a senior Cammeraygal fisherwoman of the Eora, a keeper of women's law who met the British colony at Sydney with open defiance.
body: Barangaroo was a senior Cammeraygal fisherwoman and a keeper of women's law who met the British colony with open defiance. As her husband Bennelong drew closer to the newcomers, she grew into the colony's loudest critic, refusing European clothes and the way the settlers stripped the harbour of its fish. She died in 1791, and the headland renamed for her in 2007 keeps her name on the water she would not surrender.
properties.source: Barangaroo Delivery Authority, naming history; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Francis Greenway
properties.role: The convict who built Macquarie's Sydney
properties.summary: Francis Greenway came to Sydney in chains for forgery and left it the look of a colonial capital, designing the lighthouse, the convict barracks and St James' Church under Governor Macquarie before he fell from favour and died poor.
body: Francis Greenway was an English architect transported to Sydney for forgery, only to become the colony's first civil architect under Governor Macquarie. In a few remarkable years he gave the town its first grandeur, designing the Macquarie lighthouse, the Hyde Park Barracks and St James' Church. He quarrelled his way out of favour and died poor in an unmarked grave, yet his face later went onto the ten dollar note, the only convicted forger to grace a nation's money.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Elizabeth Macquarie
properties.role: The governor's wife who shaped the harbour's edge
properties.summary: The governor's wife who shaped the harbour's edge, laying out the road around the Domain and lending her name to the carved sandstone seat at its point.
body: Elizabeth Macquarie was the governor's wife who shaped the harbour's edge, a Highland woman with a real eye for gardens, farming and building. She planned the road around the Government Domain to the point at its tip, and both the road and the point came to carry her name. Convicts cut a bench into the sandstone there in 1810, the seat still known as Mrs Macquarie's Chair.
properties.image_caption: The sandstone bench carved for her, photographed c. 1870–75
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Museums of History NSW

properties.name: Mary Reibey
properties.summary: Transported from Lancashire at thirteen for stealing a horse, Mary Reibey rebuilt her life into one of the largest trading fortunes in early Sydney. The convict girl became the merchant whose face now looks out from the twenty dollar note.
body: Mary Reibey was transported from Lancashire at thirteen for riding a stolen horse, and turned a convict sentence into one of the largest trading fortunes in early Sydney. With her husband Thomas she built farms, river boats and a George Street warehouse, then ran the whole enterprise herself after he died in 1811, leaving her with seven children. Shrewd, wealthy and generous to churches and schools, she died a legend in 1855 and now looks out from the twenty dollar note.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Caroline Chisholm
properties.summary: Caroline Chisholm met the immigrant ships of the 1840s and found the jobless young women aboard shelter, work and safe passage inland, settling thousands across colonial New South Wales.
body: Caroline Chisholm met the immigrant ships on the Sydney wharves in the 1840s and would not stand by while jobless young women were left with nowhere safe to go. She opened the Female Immigrants' Home with its free employment registry, then loaded newcomers into drays and rode inland to find them work, settling some 11,000 people across the colony. A Catholic in a Protestant town, she was famous across the Empire and later honoured on the five dollar note.
properties.image_caption: Caroline Chisholm in 1852 (State Library of NSW)
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Sir Henry Parkes
properties.role: Five times premier, father of Federation
properties.summary: Five times premier of New South Wales and the man most often called the father of Federation, Henry Parkes rose from a poor English workshop to lead a continent toward nationhood.
body: Henry Parkes arrived in Sydney in 1839 with almost nothing and went on to serve five times as premier of New South Wales, more than any colonial figure. He built the foundations of free state schooling and ran the liberal Empire newspaper, though his ambitions left him bankrupt more than once. His 1889 Tenterfield speech set Federation in motion, and he is widely called the father of the nation he did not live to see.
properties.image_caption: Sir Henry Parkes, photographed by Henry Walter Barnett
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Louisa Lawson
properties.summary: Louisa Lawson founded The Dawn in 1888, the first Australian journal written, set and printed entirely by women, and her Dawn Club helped launch the campaign that won NSW women the vote.
body: Louisa Lawson left a worn-out bush marriage and came to Sydney in 1883 with her children, among them the young Henry Lawson. In 1888 she founded The Dawn, a monthly journal produced from start to finish by women. At its height she employed about ten female staff, printers included, and the journal reached readers across Australia and overseas. The New South Wales Typographical Association, which refused to admit women, pressed her to sack her printers and even leaned on advertisers to boycott her. She kept publishing for 17 years.

Lawson launched the Dawn Club in 1889, and it became a meeting point for the suffrage movement in Sydney. When NSW women won the vote in 1902 she was hailed as the mother of the campaign. As she put it, a woman's opinions are useless to her unless she can express them. The Dawn press also printed Henry's first volume of verse in 1894, so the journal nurtured two reputations at once.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Fanny Durack
properties.role: The fastest woman in the water
properties.summary: Fanny Durack won the 100 metres freestyle at the 1912 Stockholm Olympics, the first Olympic swimming gold by an Australian woman, with her friend and rival Mina Wylie taking silver.
body: Fanny Durack learned her speed in the harbour and ocean baths of Sydney's east, swimming and competing alongside Mina Wylie, whose father Henry had built the baths on the rock platform at Coogee in 1907. When the 1912 Stockholm Games first opened swimming to women, the New South Wales association at first refused to send any, on the view that women should not race where men could watch. Public pressure and fundraising paid the two women's way.

Durack took the gold in the 100 metres freestyle, the first Olympic swimming gold won by an Australian woman, with Wylie close behind for silver. Through the decade that followed she dominated women's freestyle, holding world records across distances from the short sprints to the mile. She was born in 1889 and died in 1956, and remained the only Australian woman to win Olympic swimming gold until Clare Dennis in 1932.
properties.image_caption: Fanny Durack and Mina Wylie, Stockholm 1912
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Wylie's Baths Trust

properties.name: Victor Trumper
properties.summary: Victor Trumper was the most graceful batsman of cricket's golden age, a Sydney hero who died young and was mourned by enormous crowds. He also helped bring rugby league to Australia.
body: Trumper was the most graceful batsman of cricket's golden age. He made runs on pitches that defeated everyone else, and he was just as likely to give his wicket away once the job looked done. The Sydney Cricket Ground was his home stage, and the wet English summer of 1902 his masterpiece, when he piled up centuries on surfaces built for bowlers. The famous photograph of him leaping out to drive is still the textbook picture of attacking batting.

In 1904 he opened a sports store at 108 Market Street with the Test wicketkeeper Hanson Carter. Three years later the shop became a meeting place for footballers unhappy with how rugby union treated them, and Trumper threw his weight behind the breakaway. When the New South Wales Rugby Football League was formed in 1907, he was elected its first treasurer.

His health failed quickly, and he died of Bright's disease in 1915 at the age of 37. Sydney came out in force to farewell him. The funeral procession to Waverley Cemetery drew one of the largest crowds the city had ever seen for a sportsman.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; SCG Trust

properties.name: Arthur Stace
properties.summary: Arthur Stace was a reformed alcoholic who chalked the single word Eternity in flowing copperplate across Sydney's pavements for some 35 years, his identity unknown until 1956.
body: A Surry Hills petty criminal and derelict drinker, Stace walked into a free meal at a church gathering in 1930 and came away a changed man. Two years later, at the Burton Street Baptist Tabernacle in Darlinghurst where he had taken work as a janitor, he heard the evangelist John Ridley cry that he wished he could shout the word Eternity through the streets of Sydney. Stace, who could barely read or write, found that this one word flowed from his chalk in perfect copperplate.

For about 35 years he rose before dawn and wrote Eternity on footpaths and doorsteps across the city, perhaps half a million times. His identity stayed a mystery until a clergyman caught him in the act and a newspaper named him in 1956. The word blazed across the Harbour Bridge as the year 2000 began, and a wrought-metal replica now sits near where one of his originals survived.
properties.image_caption: Stace's copperplate 'Eternity', preserved at Central Railway
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Kate Leigh
properties.summary: Kate Leigh ran a sly-grog and cocaine empire across Surry Hills through the 1920s and 1930s, and her feud with Tilly Devine helped drive the razor-gang wars.
body: When the six o'clock closing of 1916 emptied the legal pubs, Kate Leigh moved in to fill the thirst. She ran more than twenty sly-grog shops across Surry Hills and East Sydney, with cocaine and stolen goods as profitable sidelines, which earned her the nickname the Snow Queen. From her terraces around Devonshire Street she commanded one side of the razor-gang wars of the late 1920s, locked in a long and violent rivalry with Tilly Devine.

Surry Hills also knew a softer Kate, the one who threw Christmas parties for slum children and quietly settled neighbours' debts. She beat charge after charge over the years and was brought down in the end not by the police but by the tax office. She died broke in 1964, and her funeral at St Peter's in Devonshire Street drew several hundred mourners, one of the biggest send-offs the suburb had seen.
properties.image_caption: Kate Leigh's gaol photograph, Long Bay (NSW State Archives)
properties.source: NSW State Archives; Dictionary of Sydney, razorhurst

properties.name: Tilly Devine
properties.summary: Tilly Devine ran a chain of Darlinghurst brothels through the 1920s and 1930s and became, with Kate Leigh, one of the most notorious figures of Sydney's razor years.
body: A bricklayer's daughter from Camberwell in London, Tilly Devine had already worked as a prostitute before she followed her soldier husband to Sydney, arriving on the war bride ship Waimana in January 1920. She soon found the gap in the law that made her fortune. The Police Offences (Amendment) Act of 1908 made it a crime for a man to keep a brothel or live off prostitution, but it said nothing about a woman doing the same. So Tilly put her own name on the door.

By the late 1920s she controlled a string of brothels along Palmer Street, Darlinghurst, in the heart of the area the papers called Razorhurst. She wore diamonds and furs, racked up a long police record, and ran a savage rivalry with the other great Sydney crime boss, Kate Leigh. Their gangs slashed and bled through the razor wars until police forced an uneasy truce in 1936.

In the end it was not the police who brought her down. In October 1955 the tax office hit her with a bill for more than 20,000 pounds in unpaid tax and fines, and she had to start selling up. She sold her last Palmer Street brothel in 1968 and died of cancer in 1970, aged seventy.
properties.source: NSW State Archives 'Specials' photographs; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Jack Mundey
properties.role: The builder who refused to build
properties.summary: Jack Mundey led the builders labourers who downed tools to save Sydney's heritage, turning his union into one of the most unlikely conservation forces the country has known.
body: In June 1971 a group of women from Hunters Hill asked the New South Wales Builders Labourers Federation to help save Kelly's Bush, the last patch of foreshore scrub in their suburb, from a housing development. Mundey, the union's secretary, answered by pulling his members off the job. He called it a green ban, a refusal to build anything that wrecked heritage or community. The idea caught fire, and more than forty bans followed, holding up billions of dollars in projects across the city.

When developers turned on The Rocks, the union's ban and the residents' barricades saved the oldest streets in Australia. The well-known photograph of Mundey being carried away by police was taken only a few steps from the corner that now carries his name. He was arrested again and again, and the federal branch of his own union eventually broke the movement in 1975, but the buildings he fought for are still standing.

The word he coined in 1973 travelled further than he can have imagined. When the German activist Petra Kelly visited Sydney and saw the green bans at work, she carried the idea home, and in 1979 the world's first Greens party took its name from what Mundey and his labourers had done.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Juanita Nielsen
properties.summary: Juanita Nielsen was a Kings Cross publisher and conservationist who fought the developers of Victoria Street, then disappeared in 1975 in a case that has never been solved.
body: Born into the family that controlled the Mark Foy's retail fortune, Nielsen lived at 202 Victoria Street, Potts Point, and ran a local newspaper called NOW from her terrace. She used its pages against the developer Frank Theeman, whose plan to clear the street's low-rent residents for high-rise towers ran into a union green ban that held the line for years.

On 4 July 1975 she went to the Carousel Club in Kings Cross, expecting to talk about advertising for her paper, and was never seen again. Her body has never been found. A coronial inquest in 1983 concluded she had been killed but could not say how or by whom, and pointed to the development fight and to police corruption that crippled the investigation. Two men linked to the Kings Cross figure Abe Saffron were later jailed over a plot to kidnap her.
The surviving terraces along Victoria Street, the ones she refused to give up, are the closest thing she has to a monument.
properties.source: NSW Coroner's findings; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Bea Miles
properties.summary: Bea Miles was Sydney's most famous street eccentric, a sharp, well-read rebel who quoted Shakespeare for coins and waged a forty-year war on taxi drivers and authority.
body: For four decades Beatrice Miles treated the city as her own front room. She leapt into taxis and trams and refused to pay, took on all comers, and racked up a long string of court appearances. On street corners she stood with a sign offering to recite Shakespeare for a few pence to a few shillings, and she knew the plays well enough to make good on it.

She was university educated and a famously fast reader, said to get through a couple of books a day even in her last years. The stories around her grew tall, though the best ones are true. In 1955 she paid a woman to drive her to Perth and back, a round trip of nineteen days, and settled the fare in full. Sydney never quite knew whether to fear her or adore her, and mostly did both.
She spent her final years in a Randwick home run by the Little Sisters of the Poor and died in 1973. Long after, Kate Grenville drew on her life for the heroine of the 1985 novel Lilian's Story.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Dawn Fraser
properties.place: Dawn Fraser Baths, Elkington Park, Balmain
properties.summary: Dawn Fraser, a Balmain wharfie's daughter, learned to swim in the local tidal baths and became the first swimmer to win the same Olympic event three Games running.
body: Dawn Fraser was a Balmain docker's daughter who had chronic asthma as a child and was sent to the water to strengthen her lungs. She learned to swim in the tidal baths at Elkington Park, the salt pool on the harbour edge where she would later make her name. Between 1956 and 1964 she won the 100 metres freestyle at three consecutive Olympics, in Melbourne, Rome and Tokyo, a feat no swimmer had managed before her. She held the world record for that distance for fifteen years.

Officialdom never quite knew what to do with her. After the Tokyo Games the Australian Swimming Union handed her a ten year ban, in part for souveniring a flag near the Emperor's palace and a string of other run-ins with the authorities. The suspension cost her the rest of her competitive career, though it was lifted a few years later.

Balmain made its own verdict clear, putting her name on the baths where she had learned to swim. Opened in 1882, the Dawn Fraser Baths are the oldest surviving public tidal baths in Australia, home to a swimming club founded in 1884 that is the country's oldest. They carry the name of the rebel who came up out of their water.
properties.image_caption: Dawn Fraser at the Rome Olympics, 1960
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography (living person, verify); Inner West Council

properties.name: Pemulwuy
properties.summary: Pemulwuy was a Bidjigal man of the Eora nation who led more than a decade of armed resistance to the British colony around Sydney, from his spearing of Governor Phillip's gamekeeper in 1790 to his death in 1802.
body: Pemulwuy was a Bidjigal man of the Eora who led more than a decade of armed resistance against the British farms spreading around Sydney. From spearing Governor Phillip's gamekeeper in 1790, he raided and burned across Parramatta, the Georges River and the Hawkesbury, and so many believed bullets could not kill him that warriors flocked to follow him. He was shot dead in 1802, and his severed head was sent to London and never returned.
properties.source: National Museum of Australia; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: May Gibbs
properties.summary: May Gibbs taught generations of Australian children to love the bush by turning gumnuts into babies and banksias into bogeymen, and she did most of it from a small house above Neutral Bay.
body: May Gibbs taught generations of Australian children to love the bush, turning gumnuts into babies and banksias into bogeymen. Born in England in 1877 and raised in the West, she settled in Sydney in 1913 and became the country's first professional woman cartoonist, famous for Snugglepot and Cuddlepie. She worked above Neutral Bay for forty-four years, and the home she left behind was saved by her readers and reopened as a museum.
properties.source: May Gibbs' Nutcote; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Mei Quong Tart
properties.role: The mandarin of the tea rooms
properties.place: Ashfield, and his QVB tea rooms
properties.summary: Mei Quong Tart came out from Guangdong as a boy for the goldfields and grew into one of colonial Sydney's best loved merchants, presiding over grand tea rooms in the Queen Victoria Building.
body: Quong Tart reached the New South Wales goldfields around Braidwood as a boy of nine and was taken in by a Scottish storekeeper's circle, picking up a broad Scots burr and a love of Robert Burns along the way. He made money in gold claims, turned to importing tea, and opened a run of tea rooms across Sydney. The grandest sat in the Queen Victoria Building, a luxurious hall that could seat hundreds, where premiers, suffragists and shop girls all came in for scones and a pot.

He used his standing for more than commerce. He campaigned against the opium trade that was wrecking lives in the Chinese community, fed the poor, gave to charity, and held mandarin rank from the Qing court. In August 1902 he was beaten with an iron bar and robbed of a few pounds at his QVB office. He never properly recovered, and he died of pleurisy at his Ashfield home in July 1903. Sydney mourned hard, and crowds turned out to see him off to Rookwood.
properties.source: Society of Australian Genealogists; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Maria Ann 'Granny' Smith
properties.summary: Maria Ann Smith was a Ryde orchardist whose chance green seedling, spotted in 1868, grew into the Granny Smith, one of the most widely grown apples on earth.
body: Maria Ann Smith farmed the fruit country between Ryde and Eastwood, where small orchards ran down to the creeks. In 1868 she noticed a seedling that had sprung up beside her creek, apparently from the remains of French crab-apples she had been cooking with. It bore a firm green apple that cooked beautifully and kept far longer than the other varieties grown in the colony.

Smith died in 1870, well before the apple carried her name around the world. Local growers propagated cuttings and showed the fruit as Granny Smith's seedling, and from there it spread across orchards everywhere. A century on, the same bright green apple became the emblem of the Beatles' Apple record label, a small Ryde discovery turned global shorthand.
properties.image_caption: Maria Ann Smith (State Library of NSW)
properties.source: Ryde District Historical Society; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Bungaree
properties.summary: Bungaree was a Garigal man of Broken Bay who became the first Australian-born person to sail right around the continent, and the canniest broker between the Eora and the newcomers in early colonial Sydney.
body: Bungaree was a Garigal man of Broken Bay who sailed around the continent with Matthew Flinders in 1802 and 1803, the first person born here to circle his own Country. Back in Sydney he became the canny broker between the Eora and the newcomers, greeting every ship and asking the tribute he said was owed for landing. Augustus Earle's portrait of him made his face one of the most familiar in the colony.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Rosaleen Norton
properties.summary: Rosaleen Norton was an artist and occultist known as the Witch of Kings Cross, whose pagan visions drew police raids, obscenity charges and decades of tabloid scandal.
body: Born in New Zealand in 1917 and raised in Sydney, Norton painted pagan and trance visions devoted to the god Pan, working out of Kings Cross cafes and rented rooms. Conservative 1950s Sydney went after her hard, with obscenity charges, confiscated books and police raids on her exhibitions. She gave the Cross much of its bohemian mythology and paid a heavy price for it.

The scandal pulled others down with her. Her circle drew in Sir Eugene Goossens, the conductor who had championed the idea of an opera house on Bennelong Point. In 1956 Goossens was stopped by Customs at Sydney airport carrying erotic material and ritual items, pleaded guilty, was fined and resigned his posts in disgrace. He left the country and never saw the building he had helped set in motion. Norton outlived the outrage and died in Darlinghurst in 1979.
properties.image_caption: Rosaleen Norton in 1943 (State Library of NSW)
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Charles Perkins
properties.summary: Charles Perkins was an Arrernte and Kalkadoon man who led the 1965 Freedom Ride through country New South Wales and went on to become the first Aboriginal head of a federal government department.
body: Born in Alice Springs, Perkins came to Sydney as a professional soccer player before enrolling at the University of Sydney. In February 1965 he led around thirty students, organised as Student Action for Aborigines, out of the campus on the Freedom Ride. Their bus toured country towns and put the everyday segregation of New South Wales, with Aboriginal people barred from pools, pubs and clubs, in front of the nation's cameras and the world's newspapers.

He became one of the first Aboriginal university graduates, and in 1984 the first Aboriginal person to head a federal government department as Secretary of the Department of Aboriginal Affairs. He spent the rest of his life pressing governments on Aboriginal rights. The 1967 referendum campaign drew strength from what the Freedom Ride had made impossible to ignore.
properties.image_caption: Charles Perkins on the Freedom Ride, 1965 (Tribune)
properties.source: National Museum of Australia; University of Sydney

properties.name: Victor Chang
properties.summary: Victor Chang was a cardiac surgeon who built St Vincent's into Australia's leading heart transplant centre, and whose murder in 1991 shocked the nation.
body: Victor Chang trained at St Vincent's Hospital in Darlinghurst and set up Australia's National Heart Transplant Program there in 1984. The first transplant came in February that year, and in April his team operated on 14-year-old Fiona Coote, Australia's youngest heart transplant patient, who went on to survive for decades. Under Chang the program reached survival rates among the best in the world, and he also helped design an artificial heart valve later used widely across Asia.

His murder in 1991, during a botched extortion attempt at Mosman, stunned the country. Australians later voted him Australian of the Century in a public poll. The Victor Chang Cardiac Research Institute, beside St Vincent's, carries on the work in his name.
properties.image_caption: Victor Chang's bronze, by Linda Klarfeld
properties.source: Victor Chang Cardiac Research Institute; St Vincent's Hospital

properties.name: Billy Blue
properties.role: The Old Commodore of the harbour crossing
properties.summary: Billy Blue was a Black convict who became colonial Sydney's first regular harbour ferryman, the loud and beloved 'Old Commodore' of Blues Point.
body: By his own shifting accounts Blue was born in either New York or Jamaica around 1767. He was convicted at Maidstone in Kent in 1796 for stealing raw sugar, served years in a prison hulk, and reached Sydney in 1801. After his sentence he took up boating and by 1807 held a licence to ferry passengers across the harbour from Dawes Point to the north shore, the first regular service of its kind.

Governor Macquarie took a liking to him and dubbed him 'Commodore', made him a harbour watchman, and in 1817 granted him eighty acres on the point that now carries his name. Blue rowed his boats dressed in a blue coat and top hat, did a little quiet rum smuggling, and greeted everyone, the governor included, with cheerful abuse while demanding a salute in return.
By the time he died in 1834 he was about the most recognisable figure on Port Jackson. His grant on the north shore underlies the suburb of McMahons Point, and Blues Point still bears his name.
properties.image_caption: Billy Blue, 'The Old Commodore', lithograph by Charles Rodius, 1834 (State Library of NSW)
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; North Sydney Council heritage

properties.name: Elizabeth Macarthur
properties.summary: Elizabeth Macarthur ran the family's merino flocks at Elizabeth Farm through the long years her husband John was absent, doing much of the quiet work that founded the Australian wool industry.
body: Elizabeth arrived in New South Wales in 1790 as one of the first free, educated women in the colony. The Macarthurs took up land at Parramatta and built Elizabeth Farm, where the merino breeding that would shape the colony's economy took root. For roughly a dozen years across two long absences, while John was caught up in a duel and its aftermath in England and then exiled there from 1809 to 1817, Elizabeth managed the flocks herself.

She rode out to inspect the sheep, weighed up which rams to use, oversaw shearing and shipments, and ran a workforce of convicts, writing detailed reports to John and carrying out his instructions while he drew the credit in London. Historians have come to see the foundation years of Australian wool as substantially her work, a recognition the nation was slow to give.
John died at Camden in 1834, estranged and mentally unwell. Elizabeth outlived him by sixteen years and remained at Elizabeth Farm, the house still standing at Rosehill today.
properties.source: Museums of History NSW, Elizabeth Farm; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Eliza Donnithorne
properties.summary: A Newtown recluse remembered as Australia's own Miss Havisham, though most of her legend cannot be proven.
body: For a century Newtown told one version of Eliza Donnithorne. Jilted at the altar of St Stephen's, she stopped the clocks, left the wedding breakfast to rot on the table and kept the front door ajar for thirty years in case her groom returned. Gossip went further and claimed that Charles Dickens heard the tale and turned her into Miss Havisham.

Historians can verify almost none of this. The recluse of King Street is real enough, but the abandoned wedding is not documented, and the dates point the other way. Great Expectations came out in 1861, and it looks as though Sydney readers later draped Dickens's character over Eliza, lending her details borrowed from the book. Dickens never set foot in Australia.

She was born in Cape Town in 1821 and died at Newtown in 1886. The legend itself is a genuine Victorian artefact, and Newtown has never been willing to let it go. She lies in Camperdown Cemetery beside her father, and her grave is still one of the most visited there.
properties.image_caption: Camperdown Cemetery, where Eliza Donnithorne lies
properties.source: Camperdown Cemetery Trust; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Annette Kellerman
properties.summary: Annette Kellerman was a Marrickville girl who became the most famous swimmer on earth, billed in vaudeville as the Australian Mermaid and starring in Hollywood's first million-dollar picture.
body: Annette Kellerman took up swimming as a child to strengthen legs weakened by illness, and she turned that hard discipline into world fame. She set distance records, drew crowds in vaudeville as the 'Australian Mermaid', and in 1916 starred in A Daughter of the Gods, the first film to cost a million dollars and the first to show a major star nude on screen.

She is best remembered for a bathing costume. Kellerman did not invent the one-piece swimsuit, but she wore the close-fitting version when most women were still buried in skirts and pantaloons, and she made it respectable. The story that she was arrested on a beach near Boston in 1907 for an indecent costume is part of the legend, though historians suspect she helped plant it herself.

Whatever the truth of the arrest, the costume she championed helped close the era of bathing machines and woollen skirts. Every swimmer who later moved freely at Bondi owed her a little. She performed into old age and died at Southport in Queensland in 1975, still urging girls to learn to swim.
properties.source: National Film & Sound Archive; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Walter Burley Griffin
properties.role: The architect who listened to the bush
properties.summary: The American architect who, after Canberra ground him down, built Castlecrag on Middle Harbour as a suburb that bent to the bush instead of bulldozing it.
body: After the politics of Canberra wore him down, the American architect Walter Burley Griffin turned to a sandstone headland on Middle Harbour. From the early 1920s he and Marion Mahony Griffin, his wife and fellow architect, laid out Castlecrag. The houses sat low in the landscape in stone and roughcast. There were no front fences and no red tile roofs. Every street curved to follow the contours, and each was named for a part of a castle.

The Griffins held a simple creed. Australian suburbs should defer to the Australian bush, not flatten it. That idea ran decades ahead of its time. Marion's drawings carried the vision to paper with a clarity Griffin's own hand never matched, and the open-air theatre the couple ran in their bushland amphitheatre helped seed Sydney's experimental stage.

Griffin designed more than forty houses for Castlecrag, though only about fifteen were ever built. He died in 1937 while working in India. The suburb he left behind is the closest thing to a built manifesto for how he thought people might live among the gum trees and rock.
properties.image_caption: Walter Burley Griffin at his drafting table, c.1912 (State Library of NSW)
properties.source: Walter Burley Griffin Society; Willoughby Council heritage

properties.name: Ruth Park
properties.summary: The novelist who put the Surry Hills slums on the page and made the whole country read about them.
body: Ruth Park arrived from Auckland in 1942 with a typewriter and a plan to live by writing. She married the writer D'Arcy Niland and settled into the inner-city poverty of Surry Hills, then turned what she saw straight into fiction. 'The Harp in the South' won a Sydney Morning Herald competition and ran as a daily serial from January 1947 before coming out as a book the following year.

The story of the Darcy family, with its bugs and brothels and fierce love only a couple of miles from Circular Quay, scandalised respectable Sydney. Readers loved it anyway, and they have never let it slip out of print.

Park went on to write more than forty books. One of them, 'Playing Beatie Bow', sends a modern Rocks child time-travelling into the streets of 1873, the same vanished Sydney this map tries to hold. The city she once documented from the bottom now marks her memory with a plaque set into the footpath.
properties.image_caption: Ruth Park, around the time she wrote The Harp in the South
properties.source: State Library of NSW; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Brett Whiteley
properties.role: The painter of the harbour's curve
properties.summary: Brett Whiteley turned Sydney Harbour into his great subject, painting it in sweeping ultramarine from a house above Lavender Bay.
body: From a wedding-cake house above Lavender Bay, Brett Whiteley painted the harbour as nobody had before him. He gave it sweeping ultramarine blues, bursts of jacaranda and a single restless curve, until the bend of the bay seemed to become the curve of everything. He was the youngest living artist to have a work bought by London's Tate, and in 1978 he became the only painter to win the Archibald, Wynne and Sulman prizes in the same year.

Heroin unmade him. He died alone in a motel at Thirroul, on the coast south of Sydney, in 1992. His Surry Hills studio is now a museum kept much as he left it. Below the Lavender Bay house, the garden planted by his former wife Wendy has grown into the harbour's best loved unofficial park, and the ultramarine harbour he painted is permanently his.
properties.image_caption: Brett Whiteley Place, near his Lavender Bay studio
properties.source: Art Gallery of NSW; Brett Whiteley Studio

properties.name: Nancy Bird Walton
properties.role: The youngest woman in the Empire to fly
properties.summary: Trained at Mascot by Kingsford Smith, Nancy Bird became the youngest woman in the British Empire licensed to carry passengers, and flew medical aid across outback New South Wales.
body: Nancy Bird took her first lessons at Charles Kingsford Smith's flying school at Mascot when she was seventeen. The rules made her wait until nineteen for a commercial licence, and when it came in 1934 she was the youngest woman in the British Empire qualified to carry passengers. She put it straight to work, flying medical and aerial clinic services across the dusty distances of western New South Wales. The country called her the Angel of the Outback. She traded her first Gipsy Moth for a sturdier Leopard Moth better suited to the runs.

She founded the Australian Women Pilots' Association in 1950 and remained its patron for the rest of her long life. Decades later Qantas named its first A380 after her. She insisted, in her usual no-nonsense way, that the airline spell out Nancy-Bird Walton in full, so that nobody would forget the aircraft carried the name of a woman.
properties.image_caption: Nancy Bird, photographed by Harold Cazneaux
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Qantas heritage

properties.name: Robert Watson
properties.summary: A quartermaster of the First Fleet flagship Sirius, Robert Watson became one of the colony's first harbour pilots and gave his name to Watsons Bay.
body: Robert Watson sailed with the First Fleet as quartermaster of the flagship Sirius, and stayed with the ship until she was wrecked off Norfolk Island in 1790. In 1801 Governor King granted him land at South Head, on the bay that came to carry his name. He spent his working life on the water at the entrance to the harbour, serving as boatswain at the dockyard and then as one of the colony's first pilots, rowing out through the Heads to bring strangers safely in.

In November 1818 Macquarie made him the first superintendent of the new Macquarie Lighthouse, the first light to shine on the Australian coast, set on the ridge above the same Heads he had piloted for years. He held the post only briefly. He died on 1 November 1819 at his house in The Rocks, his name already fixed to the bay below the light.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Woollahra Council heritage

properties.name: Christina Stead
properties.summary: One of the twentieth century's great novelists, Christina Stead spent her teens in Watsons Bay and made the harbour the living backdrop of her early fiction.
body: Christina Stead was born at Rockdale in 1902, the daughter of the naturalist David Stead. In 1917 the family moved to Watsons Bay, and that harbour soaked into her early work. Seven Poor Men of Sydney, published in 1934, and For Love Alone, in 1944, render this exact water, the ferries and the fishing village and The Gap, in some of the finest prose the city has ever been given.

She left for Europe in 1928 and built a towering body of work abroad, including The Man Who Loved Children, while remaining largely unread at home for decades. In 1967 a committee recommended her for the Britannica Australia Award, only for the chairman to overrule them on the grounds that she was no longer Australian, and the prize was withheld. She came back to Australia at last in 1974, and died in Sydney in 1983.
properties.image_caption: The Stead house at Watsons Bay, the harbour of her fiction
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Lachlan Macquarie
properties.summary: The fifth governor of New South Wales, who set out to rebuild a convict outpost in stone and good order and to give emancipists a real place in colonial life, and who also ordered the 1816 reprisal that became the Appin massacre.
body: Lachlan Macquarie was the fifth governor of New South Wales, who set out to rebuild a convict outpost in stone and good order and to give emancipists a real place in colonial life. Over twelve years from 1810 he laid out towns, founded a bank, and worked with the convict architect Francis Greenway. The same governor also ordered the 1816 reprisal that became the Appin massacre, and his legacy is weighed against both.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: James Barnet
properties.place: Macquarie Lightstation, his rebuilt first light
properties.summary: For a quarter of a century James Barnet was the Colonial Architect of New South Wales, and much of Victorian Sydney still carries his hand.
body: From 1865 to 1890 James Barnet served as Colonial Architect of New South Wales, and the public face of Victorian Sydney is largely his. The General Post Office with its carved frieze, Customs House, the Lands and Colonial Secretary's buildings, and courthouses and post offices scattered across the colony all came from his office. So did the lighthouses, including the faithful 1881 to 1883 rebuild of Greenway's crumbling Macquarie tower, the colony's first light, and the lonely tower at Barrenjoey.

His career ended badly. In 1890 a royal commission censured the shoddy work on the Bare Island fortifications at Botany Bay, and the blame settled on him, a scandal marked elsewhere on this map at La Perouse. The Garden Palace had already brought its own heartbreak. Raised at his direction in a matter of months for the 1879 exhibition, it burned to the ground three years later. Few architects anywhere have stamped a city so completely.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks; NSW State Archives

properties.name: Thomas Sutcliffe Mort
properties.place: Mort's Dock, Woolwich, his great dry dock at Balmain came first
properties.summary: Thomas Sutcliffe Mort was Victorian Sydney's industrial imagination in one man, the wool auctioneer and dock builder who poured a fortune into the dream of shipping frozen meat to Britain.
body: Mort started the colony's wool auction system in the 1840s and went on to almost everything else, from railways and dairying to shipbuilding. His Balmain dry dock, working by 1855, was Australia's first, and its engineering works grew into the largest private employer in the colony, building ships and locomotives and employing thousands. The later Woolwich dock that carried his company's name was for decades the largest in the country.

His true obsession was refrigeration. He backed the engineer Eugene Nicolle for years and sank a fortune into freezing works at Darling Harbour, convinced Australia could feed Britain with chilled meat. He died in May 1878, before the first successful frozen-meat shipment finally proved him right. At the unveiling of his statue in Macquarie Place, hundreds of his workmen gave up a day's pay to attend.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Nikolai Miklouho-Maclay
properties.place: Australia's first marine biological station, Camp Cove
properties.summary: Russian explorer, marine biologist and anthropologist who built Australia's first marine biological station at Camp Cove in 1881, and who spent his life arguing that all peoples were equal.
body: Nikolai Miklouho-Maclay had lived alone among the people of New Guinea's Maclay Coast, learning their languages and earning their trust, and he emerged as one of their fiercest defenders against the blackbirding trade and colonial land grabs. In 1881 he built Australia's first marine biological station at this small point near Camp Cove, a sandstone cottage designed by the Sydney architect John Kirkpatrick. He studied sponges and sharks in the harbour, and in 1884 he married Margaret Robertson, the widowed daughter of the long-serving New South Wales premier Sir John Robertson, very much against her family's wishes.

Long before the language existed for it, Miklouho-Maclay worked as an anti-racist anthropologist. His research helped refute the idea that the different races of humankind were separate species, and his notebooks insisted on the full equality of peoples his contemporaries dismissed as savages. Tolstoy wrote to tell him he was the first to prove by his own experience that, in his words, man is man everywhere. Russia still honours his name, while Sydney largely forgot the little laboratory by the water.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Frederick Ward, Captain Thunderbolt
properties.role: The escape artist of Cockatoo Island
properties.place: Cockatoo Island, and the swim that made him famous
properties.summary: Bushranger who made the only known successful escape from Cockatoo Island by swimming for shore, then spent six years robbing the northern roads as the gentleman bushranger Captain Thunderbolt.
body: Frederick Ward was gaoled on Cockatoo Island for receiving stolen horses, and there he pulled off the island's most famous escape. On 11 September 1863 he and a fellow prisoner, Frederick Britten, slipped away from their work gang, hid for two days, then swam from the northern side of the island towards the Woolwich shore. It is the only escape from Cockatoo Island known to have succeeded by swimming. Tradition credits his Worimi partner Mary Ann Bugg with smuggling supplies to him across the water, though historians point out the records place her in the Dungog district at the time. The legend has outlasted the facts.

Ward spent the next six years as Captain Thunderbolt, the so-called gentleman bushranger of the New England and northern roads. He was courteous to those he robbed, and there is no evidence he ever shot anyone. Constable Alexander Walker caught up with him at Kentucky Creek near Uralla on 25 May 1870 and shot him dead. The harbour swim remains the founding legend of the island he fled.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Mary Gould
properties.role: The girl who lived as Tom
properties.summary: An orphan from the Central West who lived as a boy for eight years on her dying mother's instruction, until a vagrancy arrest sent her, still taken for a boy, to a school ship and then to the Biloela girls' institution on Cockatoo Island.
body: Mary Gould was orphaned young when her mother died at Grenfell in the colony's Central West. On her deathbed her mother made the child promise to live as a boy, reasoning she would be safer and more able to earn a living that way. Mary kept the promise for about eight years, working as a shepherd and labourer under the names James, Jem and Charley, and being arrested as a boy too. After a vagrancy arrest in 1879 she was sent, still believed to be male, to the nautical school ship Vernon. When she refused to undress for the processing bath the truth came out, and she was committed to the Biloela girls' institution on Cockatoo Island, where staff rechristened her Charlotte before a constable confirmed her real name was Mary.

Her file is a rare window into the city's underclass children and the harsh industrial-school system that warehoused them. Biloela was notorious. Inquiries in the early 1870s revealed head-shaving, solitary confinement and the use of straitjackets to subdue the girls. Of Mary herself, little more is known. She entered domestic service after leaving the island, and in 1883 she was convicted of stealing and sentenced to six months' hard labour. After that the record thins out, and the map keeps her name where the system put her.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks; NSW State Archives

properties.name: Sarah Auburn
properties.role: From Biloela to her own shop
properties.summary: A girl raised inside the Biloela Industrial School on Cockatoo Island who left the institution and made an independent life in Sydney as a newsvendor.
body: Sarah Auburn grew up inside the Biloela Industrial School for Girls on Cockatoo Island, the bleak institution that took over the island after the convicts in 1871. Up to 120 girls were held there at a time, some only infants, and inquiries in the early 1870s exposed head-shaving, solitary confinement and the use of straitjackets. It was not a place that encouraged its inmates to imagine a future of their own choosing.

Sarah did imagine one. She left the island and built an independent living in the city as a newsvendor, selling papers on her own account. The Harbour Trust tells her story as a counterweight to Biloela's grim record, a reminder that the girls the colony shut away on its prison island were not what the system assumed them to be.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks

properties.name: Barney Kieran
properties.place: The training ship Sobraon, Cockatoo Island
properties.summary: Barney Kieran learned to swim as a committed boy aboard the training ship Sobraon and held the world's freestyle records from the middle distances to the mile, before dying of appendicitis at nineteen.
body: In March 1900 Bernard Kieran's mother had him committed to the nautical school-ship Sobraon, moored off Cockatoo Island. There the wayward Sydney boy learned to swim in the ship's netted harbour pool, and under his mentor W. Hilton Mitchell he turned out to be extraordinary. Within a few years of leaving he held world freestyle records across the middle distances and up to the mile.

Sydney called him the champion swimmer of the world, and the English press agreed after his 1905 tour, where he set record times in the pools of the north. He raced the best men of his day and beat them. He was barely out of his teens and already the fastest distance swimmer alive.

In December 1905 he travelled to Brisbane and won three Australasian titles, then fell ill on 8 December. He had an operation for appendicitis and died in hospital on 22 December, aged just nineteen. Victor Trumper's funeral a decade later is the one Sydney remembers. Kieran's was nearly as large, and it deserves to be remembered too.
properties.image_caption: 'Champion Swimmer of the World', Barney Kieran, 1886–1905
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Annie Egan
properties.role: The nurse who died at quarantine
properties.summary: Annie Egan was an army nurse who caught the deadly 1918 influenza while caring for returned soldiers at the North Head Quarantine Station, and her dying plea for a priest, refused by the authorities, became a national scandal.
body: When pneumonic influenza arrived on the troopships at the end of 1918, the army sent the newly qualified Annie Egan to the North Head Quarantine Station. She reached the station on 21 November and began nursing soldiers from the troopship Medic. Within a day or two she had the disease herself. She kept working until she collapsed, and on 26 November she was admitted to the quarantine hospital.

Knowing she was dying, the devout young Catholic from Gunnedah asked again and again for a priest. The authorities refused. They cited overcrowding and the danger of carrying the infection back into the city, and a Manly priest who offered to come was turned away. Egan died on 3 December 1918, aged 27, without the last rites she had begged for.

The public would not let it rest. Requiem masses were held for her at St Mary's Cathedral, at St Vincent's Hospital and at St Joseph's in her home town of Gunnedah, and the outcry forced the federal government to back down and admit clergy to the dying at quarantine. She was buried the day after her death, with full military honours, in the Third Quarantine Cemetery on North Head.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks; NSW State Archives

properties.name: The Silva family
properties.role: A century of keeping the light
properties.summary: For close to a century, one family kept the light at Macquarie Lightstation on South Head, beginning with a Portuguese sailor who jumped ship and ending with his grandson.
body: Emanuel Silva was born in 1800 on Faial in the Azores. He reached New South Wales aboard an American whaler around the age of thirty, jumped ship to escape brutal conditions, and in the early 1860s became First Assistant Light Keeper at Macquarie Lightstation. He worked the light until 1888 and died at the station at the remarkable age of 102.

His son Frederick grew up at the lighthouse from the age of two and kept it from seventeen until he retired at fifty-seven. Frederick's son James was born on the headland in 1901, served as a relieving keeper before the First World War, and came back as signal master in 1948. Three generations of Silvas tended the same windswept ridge between the tower and The Gap.

Keeping a light was a whole household's work. Wives logged the weather and passing ships, children carried oil up the stairs and grew up to the moan of the foghorn. Automation eventually closed this way of life down, and the Silvas' long watch at South Head stands for every keeping family who lived by a lonely coast.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks

properties.name: Florence 'Mrs Mac' McKenzie
properties.place: Georges Heights, where her trainees signalled
properties.summary: Florence McKenzie was Australia's first female electrical engineer, and the signalling school she founded sent the first women ever to serve in the Royal Australian Navy.
body: Everyone called her Mrs Mac. Florence McKenzie set up her own electrical contracting business in 1918 and apprenticed herself to it so she could qualify for the Diploma in Electrical Engineering at Sydney Technical College, becoming Australia's first female electrical engineer. In 1922 she was the first Australian woman to hold an amateur radio operator's licence. She ran a wireless shop and a school for women operators long before the country thought women belonged anywhere near a transmitter.

When war loomed she founded and funded the Women's Emergency Signalling Corps in 1939, teaching Morse code and visual signalling. Some twelve thousand servicemen passed through her Sydney classrooms, so the Navy that wanted nothing to do with her women ended up sending its own men to her for training.

Against steady official resistance she pushed fourteen of her trainees into the Navy, and on 28 April 1941 they reported to HMAS Harman in Canberra. They were the first women to serve in the Royal Australian Navy, the beginning of the WRANS. Recognition came slowly. Her OBE was not gazetted until 1950.
properties.image_caption: Mrs Mac in Women's Emergency Signalling Corps uniform
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Ida Leeson
properties.role: The first Mitchell Librarian who was a woman
properties.place: Middle Head, her wartime and ASOPA years
properties.summary: Ida Leeson won the Mitchell Librarianship in 1932 and became the first woman to hold senior management in an Australian library, then built its Pacific collections into one of the world's finest.
body: Ida Leeson was appointed Mitchell Librarian in December 1932 on the strength of her qualifications, and she was the first woman to reach senior management in an Australian library. The trustees did not doubt she was the best candidate. What troubled them was that a woman now stood in line to lead the whole institution, so they quietly reorganised the senior ranks and pared back the standing and salary of her position.

She got on with the work regardless. Leeson developed the Mitchell's Pacific holdings into one of the great collections of their kind anywhere in the world. During the war she served in army intelligence, and afterwards she worked at the Australian School of Pacific Administration on Middle Head.

Her companion of some fifty years was Florence Birch, a New Zealander she had met in 1909, and the two shared a home in Leichhardt. It was a relationship the era expected to stay unspoken. The State Library of New South Wales now names a research fellowship in Leeson's honour.
properties.image_caption: Ida Leeson in 1933, a year into the Mitchell Librarianship that scandalised the trustees
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Camilla Wedgwood
properties.place: Georges Heights, the Australian School of Pacific Administration
properties.summary: Camilla Wedgwood was one of the British Commonwealth's first women anthropologists, and she spent her last years at the Australian School of Pacific Administration arguing that colonial rule had to start from respect for the people it governed.
body: Camilla Wedgwood, born in 1901, was a great-granddaughter of the potter Josiah Wedgwood, but she made her own name in the Pacific. In 1932 she took up an Australian Research Council fellowship for fieldwork on Manam Island off the north coast of what is now Papua New Guinea, and in 1935 she went to Nauru to look at ways of reviving local arts and crafts. She belonged to the first generation of women to work as professional anthropologists in the Commonwealth.

During the war she held the acting rank of lieutenant-colonel in the Australian Army Medical Women's Service and served as a research officer in anthropology in Alf Conlon's Directorate of Research and Civil Affairs, working in Papua and New Guinea in 1944 and 1945.

After her demobilisation in 1946 she became senior lecturer in native administration at the Australian School of Pacific Administration on the harbour. There she trained the patrol officers who would govern Papua New Guinea, and she pressed them, again and again, to see the people of the territory first.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: John Kinsela
properties.place: North Head, where he served and later advocated
properties.summary: A Wiradjuri and Jawoyn man, John Kinsela wrestled for Australia at the 1968 and 1972 Olympics and served in Vietnam, later becoming a leading voice for Indigenous veterans.
body: John Kinsela was born in 1949 at Crown Street Hospital in Surry Hills, a proud descendant of the Wiradjuri through his father and the Jawoyn through his mother. He came to wrestling almost by accident, stepping onto the mat the day his boxing trainer failed to show. The switch paid off. He wrestled for Australia at the 1968 Mexico Olympics and the 1972 Munich Games, where he finished seventh in the 52 kilogram division, and he is remembered as the first Aboriginal Olympic wrestler.

His sporting career was cut across by national service. Called up and sent to jungle training at Canungra, he deployed to Nui Dat in 1970 with 106 Battery, 4th Field Regiment, and spent ten months in Vietnam. The tour cost him a place at the Edinburgh Commonwealth Games. Back in Sydney in 1971, he completed the physical training instructor course at the School of Artillery on North Head, a posting he chose so he could remain close to his family.

Kinsela later returned to the army and served six years with the 1st Commando Regiment, taking out Commando of the Year in 1981. He carried the weight of his service afterwards, working through post-traumatic stress, and by the time of his death in 2020 he had become a respected advocate for Indigenous veterans. His life ties the harbour's military headlands to a longer story of Aboriginal service and the slow fight for recognition.
The Sydney Harbour Federation Trust records his connection to the defence lands of North Head, where he trained and which anchored him to this stretch of coast.
properties.source: Sydney Harbour Federation Trust, Historical People & Landmarks; Wikipedia

properties.name: William Francis King, the Flying Pieman
properties.place: Circular Quay, where he sold his pies
properties.summary: William Francis King, the Flying Pieman, was colonial Sydney's most celebrated eccentric, a pie seller famous for staggering feats of long-distance walking.
body: Born in London in 1807, William Francis King reached New South Wales in 1829 and tried his hand as a schoolmaster at Sutton Forest, then as a tutor and a barman, before settling into the trade that made him famous. By the mid 1830s he was selling pies around Hyde Park and Circular Quay, and he turned the job into a spectacle. He would hawk pies to passengers boarding the Parramatta steamer, then run the thirty or so kilometres overland to meet the same boat at its terminus and offer them the rest.

Through the 1840s his feats grew wilder. He raced the mail coach from Sydney to Windsor on foot and beat it more than once. He walked 1634 miles in 39 days, mostly through rain. He carried a seventy-pound dog from Campbelltown to Sydney through the small hours, and on another occasion lugged an eighty-pound goat a mile and a half in twelve minutes. Crowds gathered wherever he announced a wager.

Dressed in knee breeches, coloured ribbons and a top hat, the Flying Pieman was a street character the colony could not get enough of. The fame did not last and the money never came. He died a pauper at the Liverpool asylum on 10 August 1873 and was buried in the Catholic cemetery there. Much of Sydney's later fondness for its street eccentrics can be traced back to him.
properties.image_caption: William Francis King, the Flying Pieman
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Sir Henry Browne Hayes
properties.role: The knight who fenced out the snakes
properties.summary: Sir Henry Browne Hayes was a knighted Cork rake transported for kidnapping, remembered at Vaucluse for the ring of Irish turf he laid down to keep the snakes out.
body: Born in Cork in 1762 and knighted in 1790 while serving as the city's sheriff, Sir Henry Browne Hayes ruined himself in 1797 by abducting the Quaker heiress Mary Pike and forcing her through a sham marriage at his home. She was rescued within days. He was sentenced to death, had it commuted to transportation for life, and stepped off the Atlas at Sydney in July 1802 with money, a valet and not much humility.

He bought land near South Head and built the first house at Vaucluse, and he clashed sharply with the authorities. Governor King jailed him and exiled him to Van Diemen's Land. During the upheaval that followed the Rum Rebellion, George Johnston packed him off to the Newcastle coal mines for loudly siding with the deposed Governor Bligh.

That loyalty to Bligh eventually told in his favour. A pardon drawn up in 1809 was honoured by Governor Macquarie, and in December 1812 Hayes sailed for Ireland, surviving a shipwreck at the Falkland Islands on the way. He died back in Cork in 1832. His best-remembered mark on Sydney is a legend. Frightened of Australian snakes, he is said to have ringed his house with a moat of turf brought from Ireland, and Vaucluse folklore has long insisted that no snake ever crossed it.
properties.image_caption: Portrait miniature attributed to Adam Buck, said to be Sir Henry Browne Hayes, c.1824 (Wikimedia Commons)
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Museums of History NSW, Vaucluse House

properties.name: Lola Montez
properties.place: The Royal Victoria Theatre, Pitt Street
properties.summary: Lola Montez was an Irish-born dancer and adventuress, once mistress to the King of Bavaria, whose 1855 Australian tour brought scandal to the Sydney stage and her notorious Spider Dance to the goldfields.
body: Born Maria Dolores Eliza Rosanna Gilbert in Grange, County Sligo, in 1821, Lola Montez reinvented herself as a Spanish dancer and made her name across Europe. She became mistress to the ageing Ludwig I of Bavaria, who created her Countess of Landsfeld in 1847. Her sway over the court helped trigger the unrest of 1848, and the king abdicated rather than give her up.

She reached Sydney aboard the Fanny Major in August 1855 and opened at the Royal Victoria Theatre in Pitt Street on the 23rd, playing in a piece called 'Lola Montez in Bavaria'. The performance the papers could neither quite describe nor stop talking about was the Spider Dance, and that scandal broke a little later in Melbourne, where the Argus thundered that it was subversive of all public morality.

On the goldfields her tour turned to outright theatre off the stage. When a sheriff's officer came aboard the Waratah with a debtor's warrant, she undressed in her cabin and dared him to lay hands on her, and he left without her. At Ballarat she answered a hostile editor, Henry Seekamp of the Ballarat Times, by horsewhipping him in public at the United States Hotel. She sailed on into legend and died in Brooklyn in 1861. Sydney's theatre has never seen another tour quite like it.
properties.image_caption: Lola Montez as Sydney saw her, 1855
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of Victoria

properties.name: Captain John Piper
properties.summary: As Naval Officer of the port, John Piper grew rich on a cut of the colony's customs and spent it on the grandest house Sydney had ever seen, until the books gave way beneath him.
body: John Piper held the post of Naval Officer from 1814, which made him responsible for collecting customs duties and harbour dues. In place of a salary he took five per cent of everything that passed through his hands, and the money poured in. He used it to build Henrietta Villa on the point that still carries his name, the most lavish house in the colony. He kept his own band, a uniformed boat crew, and a table that fed everyone worth knowing in 1820s Sydney.

The good times ended in 1827, when officials found the customs accounts badly short. The fault lay in carelessness and easy favours to friends rather than outright theft, but the result was ruin all the same. The most famous story has Piper rowed out onto the harbour and going over the side in full uniform, only for his boat crew to haul him back in.

Forgiven but broke, he retired inland to Bathurst and lived out his years on the land. The point keeps his name, and Henrietta Villa is long gone, buried beneath the mansions of what is now one of the most expensive streets in Australia.
properties.image_caption: Captain John Piper, by Augustus Earle
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Woollahra Council heritage

properties.name: Betty Cuthbert
properties.place: Merrylands, where she grew up in the family nursery
properties.summary: Betty Cuthbert was the gardener's daughter from Merrylands who became Australia's Golden Girl, and remains the only runner to win Olympic gold over 100, 200 and 400 metres.
body: Betty Cuthbert grew up at her family's nursery in Merrylands and ran with her mouth open like a hungry chick, a habit that gave her an unmistakable look on the track. At the 1956 Melbourne Olympics, aged eighteen, she won three gold medals in the 100 metres, the 200 metres and the 4 by 100 metres relay. The press crowned her the Golden Girl, and the title stuck for the rest of her life.

Injury cost her the 1960 Rome Games, and many assumed her best was behind her. Instead she switched to the 400 metres, an event open to women at the Olympics for the first time, and won gold at Tokyo in 1964. That fourth title made her the only runner of any nation to take Olympic gold over 100, 200 and 400 metres.

Multiple sclerosis was diagnosed in 1969 and reshaped the rest of her life. She bore it in public with grace and became a strong advocate for research into the disease. At the Sydney 2000 Olympics she was wheeled around the stadium as a torchbearer, carried through the kind of crowd her own victories had helped bring into being.
properties.image_caption: Betty Cuthbert in the 1950s, by Ted Hood
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Athletics Australia

properties.name: George Howe
properties.summary: George Howe was a transported shoplifter who became the colony's Government Printer and produced Australia's first newspaper, the Sydney Gazette, in 1803.
body: George Howe was born on St Kitts in the West Indies, the son of a government printer, and learned his trade on the newspapers of London before he was transported to New South Wales for shoplifting in 1800. His skill was rare and valuable in a young colony, and he was soon made Government Printer.

On 5 March 1803 he produced the first issue of the Sydney Gazette and New South Wales Advertiser, the first newspaper printed in Australia. He worked with a worn-out press and so little type that he begged readers to return their old copies so the letters could be set again. He had already printed the colony's first book the year before, a volume of standing orders running to well over a hundred pages.

Howe printed almanacs, official orders and the colony's first book of verse, all while steering the Gazette carefully around the censorship of successive governors. He received no salary for the work until 1811, and even then only sixty pounds a year. Australian publishing, journalism and printing all trace back to his hand-press.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Lillian Roxon
properties.place: The Push's pubs, where she sharpened her pen
properties.summary: Lillian Roxon sharpened her wit in the Sydney Push before conquering New York, where her 1969 Rock Encyclopedia made her the mother of rock journalism.
body: Lillian Roxon learned to write and to argue in the back rooms of the Sydney Push during the 1950s, the freewheeling crowd of bohemians and intellectuals who gathered in inner-city pubs. She studied at the University of Sydney from the late 1940s and fell in with the movement there before she set her sights on New York.

From a corner table at Max's Kansas City she chronicled the rise of rock culture as it happened. Her Rock Encyclopedia, published in 1969, was the first serious reference book devoted to rock and roll, written in a fresh and colloquial voice that matched its subject. The work earned her the title mother of rock journalism.

She championed women, mentored musicians who became famous, and traded sharp words with Germaine Greer, a fellow Push veteran. A severe asthma attack killed her in her Manhattan apartment in 1973, at the age of forty-one. Australian music writing claims her as its founding spirit.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; 'Mother of Rock' (2010)

properties.name: Ethel Turner
properties.place: 'Woodlands', Killara, where Judy Woolcot was born
properties.summary: At 23 Ethel Turner wrote Seven Little Australians, the first enduring Australian children's classic, in a quiet garden on the upper north shore.
body: In the garden of 'Inglewood' at Lindfield, then an unsewered and unfashionable corner of the upper north shore, Ethel Turner sketched out Seven Little Australians on her 23rd birthday in January 1893. She wrote much of it perched in a low branch of an apple tree. Published in 1894, it became the first Australian children's classic, and it did the unthinkable for the time by letting a child die in its pages.
Judy Woolcot's death has been breaking readers' hearts for well over a century. The book has never once been out of print, the only Australian children's title that can claim as much.

Turner went on to write dozens more books and kept a diary for sixty-two years, yet she never escaped the shadow of the seven, and perhaps never much wanted to. The house still stands, renamed 'Woodlands' by a later owner and now addressed as Killara. It is a heritage-listed private home, and the garden is still recognisably Judy's.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Banjo Paterson
properties.role: The Sydney solicitor who wrote the bush
properties.summary: Andrew Barton Paterson was a Sydney solicitor and journalist who, as 'The Banjo', gave Australia some of its most enduring verse, including The Man from Snowy River and Waltzing Matilda.
body: Andrew Barton Paterson was a Sydney solicitor and journalist who, as The Banjo, gave Australia some of its best-loved verse. The Man from Snowy River and Clancy of the Overflow made him famous, and in 1895 he set words to a tune at a Queensland station to create Waltzing Matilda. A city man who wrote the bush, he reported wars, served in the First World War, and has appeared on the ten dollar note since 1993. He died in Sydney in 1941.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Gladesville heritage

properties.name: David Scott Mitchell
properties.role: The collector who never left the house
properties.place: The Mitchell Library, built for his bequest
properties.summary: David Scott Mitchell was a reclusive Darlinghurst bachelor who quietly assembled the greatest collection of Australiana ever made, then left the lot to New South Wales.
body: Mitchell was born in Sydney in 1836, the son of a well-off doctor, and trained as a barrister without ever bothering to practise. He had money enough not to work, and he spent it on books. From the 1860s he collected in earnest, hunting down every book, map, manuscript and scrap of paper he could find on Australia and the Pacific. Towards the end he rarely left his house on Darlinghurst Road, buying entire libraries at a time and letting the rooms fill up around him.

By the close of his life he owned roughly 40,000 volumes, along with manuscript diaries and letters, thousands of prints, maps and charts, pictures and coins. In 1898 he offered the whole collection to the public library on two conditions. There had to be a building worthy of it, and students had to be allowed to use it.

He died in 1907, and the Mitchell Library finally opened its doors in 1910, three years too late for him to see it. He also left an endowment of 70,000 pounds so the collection could keep growing. Nearly every historian of Australia since has worked from his shelves, and so have many of the stories on this map.
properties.source: State Library of NSW; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Les Darcy
properties.summary: Les Darcy was a Maitland blacksmith's striker who became the most loved boxer in the country, then died in America at 21, branded a shirker for slipping away during the war.
body: Born near Maitland in 1895, Darcy went into a blacksmith's shop as a young man, and the work left him with a punch to match. From 1915 he ruled the Sydney Stadium at Rushcutters Bay, beating one American middleweight after another, many of them champions dodging the war back home. He packed the tin shed to its rafters and became the most adored athlete Australia had yet produced.

With young men of military age being refused passports, Darcy stowed away aboard an oil tanker out of Newcastle in October 1916, sailing the day before the conscription referendum to chase a world title in the United States. The patriotic press on both shores called him a shirker, and American authorities kept him out of the ring. In April 1917 he collapsed, and on 24 May he died in Memphis of septicaemia and its complications, aged 21.

His embalmed body was brought home, and the crowds that turned out to mourn him dwarfed anything he had ever drawn to a fight. An estimated half a million people paid their respects, a strange and enormous farewell for a young man the newspapers had spent months condemning.
properties.image_caption: Darcy and Eddie McGoorty before their 1916 Stadium bout
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; National Museum of Australia

properties.name: Charmian Clift
properties.role: The essayist Sydney read on Thursdays
properties.place: Mosman, where the columns were written
properties.summary: Charmian Clift spent a decade writing on the Greek islands before returning to Sydney, where her weekly Herald essays taught a suburban city how to look at itself.
body: Clift was born in Kiama on the New South Wales south coast in 1923. In 1954 she and her husband George Johnston, the novelist, took their young family to the Greek island of Kalymnos and later to Hydra, where they tried to live by the pen. They were often broke, frequently brilliant, and at the centre of a bohemian crowd that drew in writers and drifters, among them the young Leonard Cohen.

She came back to Sydney in 1964 and made the newspaper essay her own form. Writing from Mosman, she produced some 240 columns for the Sydney Morning Herald and the Melbourne Herald over the next five years, turning a weekly deadline into clear-eyed, intimate prose about a country settling into its suburbs.

Clift died by her own hand in 1969, on the eve of the publication of Johnston's Clean Straw for Nothing, the novel that drew on their marriage. Her essays have been collected and reissued many times and have never stopped finding readers. The Hydra years became their own literary legend, retold half a century later in books and on screen.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Henry Lawson
properties.role: The people's poet, in the city that broke him
properties.summary: Henry Lawson gave Australia the plainest, truest stories it had ever read about the bush, yet he wrote most of them, and did most of his suffering, in Sydney.
body: Henry Lawson gave Australia its plainest, truest stories about the bush, yet he wrote most of them in Sydney. Born on the Grenfell goldfields in 1867 and nearly deaf by fourteen, he found fame in the Bulletin and traded famous verses with Banjo Paterson over whether the country was hardship or romance. His later years sank into drink, poverty and gaol, and in 1922 he was given the first state funeral New South Wales granted a citizen for distinction alone.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Frank Hurley
properties.role: The mad photographer, born in Glebe
properties.summary: Frank Hurley was a Glebe printer's son who became Australia's most daring photographer, shooting Antarctica with Mawson, Shackleton's doomed Endurance, and the front lines of two world wars.
body: Frank Hurley was born in Derwent Street, Glebe, on 15 October 1885, the son of a Lancashire printer and union man. At thirteen he ran away after a blue with his teacher, jumped a goods train to Lithgow, and took work at the Eskbank ironworks. A box Kodak, bought for a few shillings and paid off at a shilling a week, set the course of his life. By his twenties he was making the photographs others thought impossible, first with Douglas Mawson in Antarctica, then with Ernest Shackleton on the Endurance.
