When the Endurance was crushed by pack ice, Hurley dived into the flooded, freezing hull to save his glass plates. Marooned, he and Shackleton sat on the ice and chose the 120 negatives worth carrying out, then smashed the rest so he could never be tempted to go back for them. The images that survived are among the most famous in polar history.

In Flanders the diggers nicknamed him the mad photographer for standing upright under shellfire at Passchendaele. The official war historian Charles Bean was less admiring and condemned Hurley's composite prints, built by layering several negatives into one image, as fakes. The two clashed over what an honest war photograph should be. Hurley served as an official photographer in both world wars and died at his home on the Collaroy Plateau on 16 January 1962, the Glebe boy who had photographed the ends of the earth.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Glebe Society; Australian War Memorial

properties.name: Lawrence Hargrave
properties.role: The man who gave flight away
properties.place: Wunulla Road, Woollahra Point, his home from 1902
properties.summary: Lawrence Hargrave worked out the box kite and the rotary engine that helped lift the first aeroplanes, then refused to patent any of it and watched the credit drift to others.
body: Lawrence Hargrave was the gentleman inventor who helped lift the first aeroplanes and then gave the credit away. On a beach south of Sydney in 1894 his box kites carried him off the sand, nearly nine years before Kitty Hawk, and his rotary engine would later power wartime fighters. He refused to patent any of it, watched the recognition drift to others, and died in 1915 soon after losing his only son at Gallipoli.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; National Museum of Australia; Trove (SMH 7 July 1915)

properties.name: Patyegarang
properties.place: Tarra, Dawes Point, where the notebooks were written
properties.summary: Patyegarang was a young Eora woman who taught Lieutenant William Dawes her language at his observatory on Tarra, now Dawes Point, in 1790 and 1791. Through his notebooks her words became the fullest surviving record of the language of Sydney.
body: Patyegarang was a young Eora woman who, over the summer of 1790 and 1791, taught Lieutenant William Dawes her language at his observatory on Tarra, now Dawes Point. The words she gave him, captured almost as she spoke them, became the fullest surviving record of the original language of Sydney. After Dawes sailed home she vanishes from the record, surviving in her own voice and now anchoring the modern revival of that language.
properties.source: SOAS MS 41645; Jakelin Troy, The Sydney Language; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: Tarra, Dawes Point in 1821, where the notebooks were written

properties.name: George Cann, the Snake Man
properties.role: Four hundred snakebites and a cup of cold tea
properties.summary: George Cann survived some four hundred snakebites, treated most with a cup of cold tea, and turned the Sunday snake show at the La Perouse tram loop into a family institution that still runs today.
body: The snake pit at the La Perouse tram loop was already a Sunday fixture before George Cann arrived. Its founder, Professor Fred Fox, the self-styled Snake King, died of a krait bite in Calcutta in 1914. Cann made the show a dynasty. A Newtown boy who was gassed in France during the First World War, he took over the Sunday pitch around 1919 and went on to become curator of reptiles at Taronga Zoo. He met Essie Bradley on the Queensland showground circuit and married her. She had handled snakes as Cleopatra since she was thirteen and was never once bitten.

George was not so lucky, or perhaps he was. He reckoned he survived about four hundred bites, including a tiger snake strike that blinded him for three days, and he treated most of them with nothing stronger than a cup of cold tea. He worked the pit for some forty-five years and died in 1965.

His sons carried the show on. John Cann gave the family's last performance on 18 April 2010, and the ground was renamed Cann Park in their honour. The show itself still runs most Sundays in the corrugated-iron ring, beside the La Perouse Aboriginal community whose boomerang makers and shellwork sellers drew the same tram crowds for generations. A century on, it may be the longest-running show in Sydney.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; John Cann, The Last Snake Man (2018)

properties.name: Henry Searle
properties.place: The Searle Monument, off Henley Point on the Parramatta
properties.summary: Henry Searle, the Clarence Comet, was the world champion sculler and a colonial sporting idol who died of typhoid at just twenty-three, only a year after winning the title.
body: When professional sculling was the colony's biggest betting sport, the man they idolised was Henry Searle of the Clarence River, known as the Clarence Comet. On the Parramatta River on 27 October 1888, with crowds said to number thirty thousand lining the banks and packing the boats, he beat the reigning world champion Peter Kemp by about 150 yards. He took the world title and a purse of a thousand pounds, and he was only twenty-two.

A year later, sailing home from racing in England, Searle caught typhoid. He left the ship at Melbourne and died at Williamstown on 10 December 1889, aged just twenty-three. The grief in colonial Sydney was extraordinary, and tens of thousands turned out for his funeral procession.

Two years to the day after his death, a tall obelisk was unveiled in the river off Henley Point, marking the exact finish of his championship course. Built of Melbourne bluestone and topped with a broken column of white marble, it still stands in the water, repaired after a boat ran into it during heavy fog in 1926.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Henry Searle, the Clarence Comet, Vanity Fair, 1889

properties.name: Charles Kingsford Smith
properties.summary: Charles Kingsford Smith, the aviator everyone knew as Smithy, made the first flight across the Pacific in the Southern Cross in 1928, then vanished over the sea off Burma in 1935.
body: Charles Kingsford Smith was born in Brisbane in 1897 and raised in Sydney from the age of ten. In late May 1928, with Charles Ulm and two American crewmen, he flew the Fokker trimotor Southern Cross out of Oakland in California, hopping by way of Hawaii and Fiji to land in Brisbane on 9 June. It was the first crossing of the Pacific by air, around 83 hours in the cockpit over the world's largest ocean. Sydney gave Smithy a hero's welcome, and that same year Longueville renamed its sports ground Kingsford Smith Oval with his blessing.

He was knighted in 1932. On the night of 8 November 1935 he and his co-pilot Tommy Pethybridge disappeared over the Andaman Sea, flying the Lady Southern Cross from India towards Singapore in a bid to break the England to Australia record. Their bodies were never found, though some eighteen months later fishermen recovered a wheel from the aircraft near Aye Island off the Burmese coast.
Smithy was thirty-eight. The original Southern Cross survives, preserved in a memorial at Brisbane airport, while Sydney's airport carries his name.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; National Museum of Australia
properties.image_caption: Kingsford Smith and Ulm at Mascot after the Pacific flight, 1928

properties.name: Jessie Street
properties.summary: Feminist and human-rights campaigner known as 'Red Jessie', who fought for women's suffrage and equal pay and helped shape both the United Nations Charter and the 1967 referendum on Aboriginal rights.
body: Jessie Street was born in India in 1889 and raised on Yulgilbar station near Grafton, the property her mother inherited when Jessie was a child. She became one of Australia's most determined campaigners, working through the 1920s and 1930s for women's suffrage and for equal pay. In 1945 she was the only woman in the Australian delegation to the San Francisco conference that founded the United Nations, where she helped have the word 'sex' written into the Charter's clause on equal rights.

Her husband Kenneth would later become Chief Justice of New South Wales, yet during the Cold War she was branded 'Red Jessie' in a smear campaign over her peace work. She kept going. In 1956 she urged Pearl Gibbs to start the Aboriginal-Australian Fellowship, and the constitutional changes she suggested were carried in the overwhelming 1967 referendum on Aboriginal rights. She died at Paddington in 1970. The Jessie Street National Women's Library and the gardens off Loftus Street carry her name.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Jessie Street National Women's Library

properties.name: Mina Wylie
properties.place: Wylie's Baths, Coogee, built by her father
properties.summary: Mina Wylie was one of the first two women to swim for Australia at the Olympics, taking silver at Stockholm in 1912 behind her friend Fanny Durack.
body: At the 1912 Stockholm Games, the first Olympics to let women race in the pool, Mina Wylie and Fanny Durack became the first Australian women to compete at the Games. Wylie won silver in the 100 metres freestyle, just behind Durack's gold. The pair almost stayed home. Officials argued the money stretched only to male athletes and that women should not race where men could watch, and it took a public outcry and private fundraising to get them to Sweden.

Wylie grew up in the water at Coogee, where her father Henry built the ocean baths on the South Coogee rock platform that still carry the family name. Between 1906 and 1934 she gathered some 115 state and national titles across freestyle, backstroke and breaststroke, and in 1911 she swept every Australian and NSW championship event. She held world records in three strokes and was elected to the International Swimming Hall of Fame in 1975.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Wylie's Baths Trust

properties.name: John Tebbutt
properties.summary: John Tebbutt was a largely self-taught Windsor astronomer who discovered the Great Comets of 1861 and 1881 from his own backyard observatory, and whose face later appeared on the Australian $100 note.
body: John Tebbutt was a farmer's son at Windsor who taught himself astronomy and won an international reputation without ever leaving home. On 13 May 1861 he spotted the Great Comet of that year roughly six weeks before it blazed across Northern Hemisphere skies, with no quick way to telegraph word to the rest of the world. He found a second Great Comet in 1881, and both bear his name. In 1862 he was offered the post of NSW Government Astronomer and turned it down, preferring his independent work at Windsor.

He built his own observatories on the family land, a modest one in 1864 and a larger one in 1879, and in the end designed his own burial vault as well. From 1984 his portrait, his Windsor observatory and a comet were printed on the paper $100 note, where they stayed until 1996. The observatory still stands above the Hawkesbury.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; NSW State Heritage Register

properties.name: Cathy Freeman
properties.summary: On the night of 25 September 2000, Cathy Freeman ran 400 metres around Stadium Australia in a green and silver hooded suit and carried the hopes of a nation across the line.
body: Cathy Freeman, a Kuku Yalanji and Birri Gubba woman born near Mackay in 1973, carried a nation's hopes around Stadium Australia on the night of 25 September 2000. She had won Commonwealth gold at sixteen, then lit the Sydney Olympic cauldron and ten days later took the 400 metres before a crowd of about 112,000. The first Aboriginal Australian to win an individual Olympic title, she ran her victory lap holding both flags.
properties.source: Australian Olympic Committee; National Museum of Australia
properties.image_caption: Cathy Freeman at the Sydney 2000 Olympics

properties.name: Mary MacKillop
properties.place: Mary MacKillop Place, Mount Street, North Sydney, where she died
properties.summary: Mary MacKillop co-founded the Sisters of St Joseph to teach poor children, was briefly excommunicated by an Adelaide bishop, and in 2010 became Australia's first canonised saint.
body: In 1866 Mary MacKillop and Fr Julian Tenison Woods founded the Sisters of St Joseph at Penola in South Australia, beginning in a converted stable, to bring schooling to poor children across Australia and New Zealand. In September 1871 the Bishop of Adelaide, Laurence Sheil, excommunicated her over alleged insubordination. She lived apart from her own order for about five months until Sheil lifted the ban in February 1872, just days before he died and admitted he had been badly advised.

She spent her last years at the Josephite convent on Mount Street, North Sydney, and died there on 8 August 1909. On 17 October 2010 Pope Benedict XVI canonised her in St Peter's Square as Saint Mary of the Cross, the first Australian to be declared a saint. Pilgrims still visit her tomb in the memorial chapel on Mount Street.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Mary MacKillop Place

properties.name: Patrick White
properties.role: The Nobel laureate of Centennial Park
properties.summary: Patrick White is the only Australian to win the Nobel Prize in Literature, awarded in 1973, and he lived out his later decades at his house overlooking Centennial Park.
body: Patrick White remains the only Australian to win the Nobel Prize in Literature, awarded in 1973 for what the judges called an epic and psychological narrative art which had introduced a new continent into literature. Too unwell and unwilling to make the trip, he sent the painter Sidney Nolan to collect it in Stockholm. He had already written Voss and Riders in the Chariot at Dogwoods, the small farm at Castle Hill on the city's edge.

After his mother died in 1963 he and Manoly Lascaris left Castle Hill for a large house called Highbury, at 20 Martin Road overlooking Centennial Park, where White lived for the rest of his life. He had met Lascaris in wartime Egypt in 1941, and the two stayed together for around half a century.

White used his fame as a public weapon. He spoke against war and unchecked development, and argued for Aboriginal rights and a republic. He had accepted a Companion of the Order of Australia among the first group in 1975, then resigned the honour the following year in protest at the dismissal of the Whitlam government. He died at the Centennial Park house in 1990.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: Patrick White in 1973, his Nobel year

properties.name: Fred Hollows
properties.role: The eye surgeon who gave sight back
properties.summary: The New Zealand-born eye surgeon who took world-class ophthalmology out to remote Aboriginal communities, then to the world's poor.
body: Fred Hollows arrived from New Zealand in 1965 and built his career at the University of New South Wales, chairing the ophthalmology teaching across Sydney hospitals, including Prince of Wales at Randwick. He soon turned his attention outward to the bush. Visiting Aboriginal communities in the early 1970s, he found trachoma everywhere, a curable eye disease that was sending people blind. He pushed the Royal Australian College of Ophthalmologists into action and helped run the National Trachoma and Eye Health Program from 1976 to 1978.

The numbers from those years still astonish. Mobile teams reached more than 460 communities, examined around 62,000 Aboriginal people, treated 27,000 for trachoma and carried out a thousand operations. The work roughly halved the rate of blindness among Indigenous Australians.

Hollows then set his mind to the rest of the world. He fought to manufacture intraocular lenses cheaply so that cataract surgery could reach people who could never afford it, and he launched the Fred Hollows Foundation in Sydney on 3 September 1992. He died of cancer the next year. After a state funeral at St Mary's Cathedral, he was buried, by his own wish, far out west at Bourke, the town he had come to love.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; The Fred Hollows Foundation

properties.name: Faith Bandler
properties.place: The Aboriginal-Australian Fellowship, founded in Sydney
properties.summary: An Australian South Sea Islander campaigner who became the voice of the Yes case in the 1967 referendum on Aboriginal rights.
body: Faith Bandler was born in 1918, the daughter of a man blackbirded as a boy from Ambrym Island, in what is now Vanuatu, to the Queensland canefields. That story of forced labour shaped everything she did. She became one of Australia's great civil rights campaigners, working as an ally to Aboriginal people and as a bridge between communities.

In 1956 she and Pearl Gibbs founded the Sydney-based Aboriginal-Australian Fellowship. Its first public meeting packed out the Town Hall on 29 April 1957, with Jessie Street among its patrons. Through the 1960s Bandler was the New South Wales spokeswoman and organiser for the campaign to change the Constitution, and in 1967 the referendum passed with nearly 91 per cent of the national vote.

From 1974 she turned to her own people, helping to form the Australian South Sea Islanders United Council and pressing for their recognition. Her novel Wacvie told her father's story. She declined an MBE in 1976 over the sacking of the Whitlam government, and later accepted Australian honours, becoming a Companion of the Order of Australia in 2009. She died in 2015.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; AIATSIS

properties.name: Dame Joan Sutherland
properties.place: The Sydney Opera House, her Joan Sutherland Theatre
properties.summary: Born at Point Piper, Joan Sutherland became one of the great coloratura sopranos of the twentieth century, the singer Italian audiences called 'La Stupenda'.
body: Joan Sutherland was born at Point Piper in 1926 and worked her way up through years of apprentice roles at Covent Garden. Her breakthrough came in 1959, when she sang the title role in Lucia di Lammermoor in a London production conducted by Tullio Serafin and staged by Franco Zeffirelli. At 32 she went from steady professional to international star almost overnight. The following year, after singing Handel's Alcina at La Fenice in Venice, Italian audiences and critics gave her the name that stuck, 'La Stupenda', the stunning one.

Alongside her husband, the conductor Richard Bonynge, she dominated the bel canto repertoire for three decades and helped revive operas that had fallen out of fashion. Her voice paired enormous agility with real power, a rare combination in that demanding music.

She first sang at the Sydney Opera House in 1974 and returned almost every year after that, giving her Sydney farewell there in 1990 in Les Huguenots. She died in Switzerland in 2010. Two years later the Opera House renamed its main opera theatre in her honour, so the house she had helped fill now carries her name.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Sydney Opera House

properties.name: Jack Lang
properties.place: Auburn, his seat for nearly twenty years
properties.summary: Jack Lang, known as the Big Fella, was twice Labor Premier of New South Wales and the only state premier ever dismissed by a governor, sacked in 1932 during the Depression.
body: Jack Lang was twice Labor Premier of New South Wales, from 1925 to 1927 and again from 1930 to 1932, and he towered over Depression-era politics as both hero and villain. His radical answer to the slump, the Lang Plan, called for suspending interest payments to British bondholders and defying the banks. It split the labour movement between his loyalists, who rallied behind the cry 'Lang is Right', and those who thought him reckless. In May 1932 Governor Sir Philip Game dismissed Lang after he instructed state officials not to hand over money to the Commonwealth, making him the only New South Wales premier ever sacked by a governor.

Earlier in that same charged year came one of Sydney's strangest public moments. At the opening of the Sydney Harbour Bridge, Francis de Groot of the right-wing New Guard rode forward on horseback and slashed the ribbon before Lang could cut it. The premier had the ribbon retied and opened the bridge anyway, unruffled.

Lang represented Auburn until he left state parliament in 1946. In old age he became an unlikely mentor to the young Paul Keating, who visited the octogenarian twice a week to learn the political trade, a thread that ties the Big Fella to a future prime minister.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Abe Saffron
properties.place: Kings Cross, the heart of his empire
properties.summary: Abe Saffron was the Kings Cross nightclub and sly-grog boss known as 'Mr Sin', long suspected of much but convicted only of tax evasion.
body: Abe Saffron was born in Annandale in 1919 and from the 1940s built the sly-grog, strip-club and nightclub empire that earned him the name 'Mr Sin' and the title King of Kings Cross. He ran the business through layers of associates and front companies, keeping his own name off the paperwork. For decades the authorities could pin almost nothing on him. His only major conviction came in November 1987, for federal tax evasion, after which he served just over two years in prison.

Rumour tied Saffron to two of Sydney's most haunting unsolved cases. One was the 1975 disappearance of the anti-development campaigner Juanita Nielsen, last seen at his Carousel nightclub in Kings Cross. The other was the 1979 Luna Park Ghost Train fire, which killed seven people. The Luna Park link was pressed again in a 2021 ABC documentary, but no charge was ever laid in either case and both remain unproven.
He died in Sydney in September 2006. To the public he had become the enduring face of the Cross during its long vice years, a man whose reputation outran anything a court ever managed to prove.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Charles Ulm
properties.role: The organiser of the Southern Cross
properties.summary: Charles Ulm was the planner and co-commander behind the Southern Cross flights, the organiser whose logistics made Kingsford Smith's record crossings possible.
body: Charles Ulm was the man who made the daring add up. As co-commander with Charles Kingsford Smith on the Southern Cross, he handled the planning, the funding and much of the navigation, the unglamorous work that turned bold ideas into real flights. He shared in the 1928 first crossing of the Pacific and, weeks later, the first flight across the Tasman. If Kingsford Smith was the dashing pilot, Ulm was the master organiser at his side. In 1929 the pair founded Australian National Airways together.

When that airline collapsed during the Depression, Ulm pressed on. In 1934 he formed Great Pacific Airways and bought an aircraft, the Stella Australis, hoping to open a service linking Australia, North America and the United States.

On 3 December 1934 Ulm and his two crew, George Littlejohn and Leon Skilling, left Oakland in California bound for Hawaii. The Stella Australis overshot the islands and came down in the sea, and his final messages reported that they were going down. Twenty-three United States naval vessels searched the empty Pacific and found no trace of the aircraft or its crew. In early 1935 Kingsford Smith led a formation of planes out over Sydney Heads to drop floral tributes in farewell.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; National Museum of Australia

properties.name: Eugene Goossens
properties.role: The conductor who dreamed the Opera House
properties.place: The Conservatorium of Music, Macquarie Street
properties.summary: Eugene Goossens was the English conductor who badgered Sydney into wanting an opera house, then lost everything in a 1956 scandal before a single stone was laid.
body: From 1947 Goossens ran both the Sydney Symphony Orchestra and the New South Wales State Conservatorium, holding the two posts at once. He used that platform to argue, loudly and repeatedly, that Sydney deserved a dedicated home for opera and concerts. When the government leaned towards a site at Wynyard, he pushed instead for Bennelong Point, the rocky headland jutting into the harbour. The site he wanted was confirmed in 1957.

By then he was already gone. Knighted in 1955, he was undone the following March when customs officers searched his luggage at Sydney Airport and found around a thousand indecent photographs, films and books. The haul exposed his secret affair with the artist Rosaleen Norton, the so-called Witch of Kings Cross, whose interest in the occult and the erotic he quietly shared.

He pleaded guilty, paid a fine of one hundred pounds and resigned in disgrace, leaving Australia within weeks. He never returned, and he died in England in 1962. He designed nothing of the building. Utzon won that competition years later. But more than anyone, Goossens willed the idea of the Opera House into being, and he left long before anyone broke ground.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Sir Roden Cutler VC
properties.role: The VC who served fifteen years at Government House
properties.summary: Roden Cutler won the Victoria Cross in Syria in 1941 and later became the longest-serving Governor of New South Wales, holding the office for fifteen years.
body: Roden Cutler was born at Manly in 1916. As a young artillery officer in the Syria-Lebanon campaign of 1941, he won the Victoria Cross, not for one dramatic moment but for weeks of cool courage under fire against the Vichy French. He mended telephone lines in the open, helped beat back tank attacks, and pressed on directing the guns even as the fighting closed in. Near Damour he was badly wounded and lay out for some 26 hours before rescue. His right leg could not be saved. He remains the Royal Australian Artillery's only VC of the Second World War.

A diplomatic career followed, including postings in New York and the Netherlands, before he came home in 1966 to become Governor of New South Wales. He held the post until 1981, about fifteen years, the longest any Governor has served in the state, longer even than Macquarie. Through four premiers and a change of government he kept a steady, warm public presence, and few vice-regal figures have been held in such genuine affection by Sydney. He died in 2002.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Australian War Memorial

properties.name: Mum Shirl
properties.place: Redfern, the Aboriginal Medical Service she helped found
properties.summary: Mum Shirl was a Wiradjuri woman who became the heart of Aboriginal Redfern, visiting prisoners, raising children and helping to found the legal, medical and community services that reshaped Aboriginal life in Sydney.
body: Shirley Colleen Smith, known to everyone as Mum Shirl, was a Wiradjuri woman born on the Erambie Mission in Wiradjuri country near Cowra around 1923. She did not go to a regular school, partly because she lived with epilepsy, and she was taught instead by her grandfather. She could barely read or sign her own name. None of that held her back in Redfern, where she became one of the most trusted and tireless figures in Aboriginal Sydney.

For decades she visited Aboriginal people in New South Wales prisons, and she was the only woman granted unrestricted access to those gaols. When officials asked which inmate she was related to, she would answer that she was related to all of them. She brought the same fierce care to families and the sick on the outside, sitting with people in courts, hospitals and police stations whenever they needed someone in their corner.

With others in the Redfern community she helped build the organisations that changed Aboriginal life in the city. These included the Aboriginal Legal Service, the Aboriginal Medical Service founded in 1971, the Aboriginal Children's Services and the Aboriginal Housing Company. She also took part in the Aboriginal Tent Embassy protest in Canberra in 1972.

Across her rented Redfern houses she is said to have raised more than sixty children. She was appointed a Member of the Order of the British Empire in 1977 and a Member of the Order of Australia in 1985, and she was later named a National Living Treasure. To the people she served she remained, simply, Mum.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; AIATSIS

properties.name: Margaret Olley
properties.role: The painter of the Paddington still life
properties.place: Duxford Street, Paddington, her house and studio
properties.summary: Margaret Olley was one of Australia's best loved painters of still life and interiors, who worked from her crowded terrace and studio in Duxford Street, Paddington. She was the subject of two Archibald Prize winning portraits, sixty-three years apart.
body: Margaret Olley was one of Australia's most loved painters of still life and interiors. From 1964 she lived in a terrace house and adjoining former hat factory in Duxford Street, Paddington, packed with thousands of vases, flowers, fruit and lengths of fabric. What looked like chaos was carefully arranged, so that she could paint living still lifes without ever leaving home.

She was an Archibald subject across sixty-three years. She was the luminous young woman in William Dobell's 1948 winner, posed in a borrowed wedding dress made of parachute silk, and the elderly grande dame in Ben Quilty's winner of 2011.

Olley died at her Paddington home on 26 July 2011, aged 88, some months after Quilty's portrait took the prize. A generous patron of the arts who gave many works to public galleries, she has her studio lovingly rebuilt, room by room, at the Tweed Regional Gallery.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Art Gallery of NSW

properties.name: William Bland
properties.role: The convict surgeon who designed an airship
properties.summary: William Bland was a naval surgeon transported for a fatal duel who became one of colonial Sydney's most eminent doctors, and who later dreamed up a steam-powered airship.
body: Bland trained as a naval surgeon, and in 1813 a duel at Bombay changed his life. He shot and killed the ship's purser, Robert Case, was convicted of murder, and was sentenced to seven years' transportation. He reached Sydney in 1814 and was pardoned the following year, partly because the colony was desperate for qualified doctors.

From rooms on Macquarie Street he built a flourishing practice and became one of colonial Sydney's most respected surgeons. In 1832 he carried out a daring operation on a subclavian artery aneurysm, said to be only the seventh of its kind on record, and wrote it up for the Lancet. He was also a leading voice among the emancipists and served in the Legislative Council.

His strangest legacy came later in life. Around 1851 he designed the Atmotic Ship, a hydrogen airship driven by steam and screw propellers, which he claimed could carry passengers from Sydney to London in little more than a week. He published the scheme in 1855, and a model was shown at the Crystal Palace in 1854. The vessel was never built, but the dream of flight from a convict surgeon, decades before powered aircraft, lingers all the same.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Pearl Gibbs
properties.role: The lone woman of Aboriginal politics
properties.place: La Perouse, where she was born
properties.summary: Pearl Gibbs was one of the very few women at the heart of Aboriginal politics in the 1930s and 1940s, and the first Aboriginal woman to speak on Australian radio.
body: Pearl Gibbs was born at La Perouse on 18 July 1901, of Ngemba or Muruwari descent through her grandmother Maria of Brewarrina. She was one of the very few women active in Aboriginal politics in the 1930s. She helped organise the 1938 Day of Mourning and served as secretary of the Aborigines Progressive Association, where she spoke out about the conditions faced by women and children on government reserves.

On 8 June 1941 she became the first Aboriginal woman to speak on radio in Australia, broadcasting from 2GB Sydney on the rights of her people. In 1954 she was elected to the New South Wales Aborigines Welfare Board, the only Aboriginal woman, and the only woman of any background, ever to sit on it.

In 1956 she co-founded the Aboriginal-Australian Fellowship in Sydney with Faith Bandler, and she went on working toward the 1967 referendum. Her ancestral country lay far inland on the Darling around Brewarrina, but it was the headland of La Perouse, where she was born, that she was bound to all her life.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; AIATSIS

properties.name: Kenneth Slessor
properties.summary: The poet who gave Sydney Harbour its greatest elegy, 'Five Bells', and made Kings Cross his patch.
body: Kenneth Slessor wrote the poem that Sydney Harbour will always carry. 'Five Bells', published in 1939, mourns his friend the artist Joe Lynch, who fell from a ferry and drowned in the harbour in May 1927. His body was never found. Legend has it the beer bottles weighing down his overcoat dragged him under. The title comes from the ship's-bell time signal that rings out across the dark water, and the poem turns that small sound into a meditation on memory and loss.
He wrote other poems that lodged just as deeply. 'Beach Burial' grieves the dead of El Alamein, and 'William Street' soaks itself in the neon and rain of his Kings Cross.

Slessor earned his living as a working journalist on the Sun and Smith's Weekly. The Commonwealth appointed him Australia's official war correspondent in 1940, and he reported from North Africa, Greece, Crete and the Pacific before resigning in 1944 in protest at army censorship. Born in Orange, he made the Cross and the harbourside east his own, and that geography runs through everything he wrote about the city.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Edmund Barton
properties.summary: Edmund Barton was a Sydney barrister who led the Federation movement to its conclusion and became Australia's first Prime Minister on the first day of 1901. He left the office after less than three years to help found the High Court of Australia.
body: Edmund Barton was a Glebe-born barrister who became Australia's first Prime Minister on the first day of 1901. After Parkes lit the Federation cause at Tenterfield, Barton took it up and led the conventions that drafted the Constitution. He governed for barely two and a half years before resigning to become one of the three founding justices of the High Court, where he sat until his death in 1920.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; National Archives of Australia

properties.name: Grace Cossington Smith
properties.role: The modernist who painted the Bridge rising
properties.place: 'Cossington', Turramurra, her lifelong home and studio
properties.summary: A pioneering Australian modernist who painted the Sydney Harbour Bridge as its two arms reached out across the water toward a join not yet made.
body: Grace Cossington Smith painted in short rhythmic strokes of bright, unmixed colour, what she called colour within colour, vibrant with light. She was one of the first artists in Australia to work in a modernist way, learning from European post-impressionism and from fellow painters Roland Wakelin and Roy de Maistre. In 1920 her family bought a house at Turramurra on the upper north shore, renamed it Cossington, and she took the name as her own. It stayed her home and studio for the rest of her long life.

As the Harbour Bridge went up she caught something no photograph could. In 'The curve of the bridge' (1928-29) and 'The Bridge in-curve' (1930), the two great half-arches climb out over the water toward a centre that has not yet met. The second painting was actually rejected from exhibition in 1930. Both are now counted among the finest Australian works of the century.

Comfortable suburban relatives may have taken her for a ladylike amateur, and recognition came slowly. In her later years she turned increasingly to the rooms of the Turramurra house, painting doorways, windows and furniture filled with warm light. She is now seen as a major figure in Australian art, honoured equally for the bridge canvases and for those glowing interiors.
properties.source: Art Gallery of NSW; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Don Ritchie
properties.summary: For nearly fifty years Don Ritchie lived across the road from The Gap at Watsons Bay and walked over to talk frightened strangers back from the edge.
body: Don Ritchie lived across the road from The Gap, the high cliff at Watsons Bay, and he made it his quiet work to watch it. He was a navy veteran of the Second World War who had settled into life as an insurance salesman. When he saw someone standing too close to the edge, he would stroll over, offer a smile and a few gentle words, and ask them back to his house for a cup of tea. Time and again that small kindness was enough to turn a person away from the drop.

He described what he did in plain terms. He spoke of the power of a simple smile, a helping hand, a listening ear and a kind word. He kept it up for around half a century, often watching the clifftop from his front window and going out whenever someone looked to be in trouble.

He is officially credited with helping save at least 160 lives, and his family believed the true number was far higher. In 2006 he was awarded the Medal of the Order of Australia for his work, and in 2011 he was named Australia's Local Hero. After his death the council laid out Don Ritchie Grove near The Gap, with his own words cut into a low stone wall, so that the place where he answered despair with a cup of tea now carries his name.
properties.source: Australian of the Year Awards; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Don Bradman
properties.place: The Sydney Cricket Ground, his early stage
properties.summary: Don Bradman remains cricket's most lopsided record, a Test batting average of 99.94 that sits a clear forty runs above anyone who has come close.
body: Don Bradman was the most dominant batsman cricket has known, a country boy from Bowral who built his legend on the Sydney Cricket Ground before South Australia claimed him. He scored an unbeaten 452 there in 1930 and went on to captain the unbeaten 1948 Invincibles. His Test average of 99.94, settled by a final-innings duck, still sits far beyond anyone who has come close.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; SCG Trust

properties.name: Roy Rene 'Mo'
properties.place: The Tivoli, the home of his vaudeville
properties.summary: Roy Rene, known to every Australian as 'Mo', was the bawdy vaudeville comedian who became the country's first national comic star, ruling the Sydney Tivoli stage for two decades.
body: He was born Henry van der Sluys in Adelaide in 1891, the son of a Jewish-Dutch cigar maker. He took the stage name Roy Rene, then created Mo McCackie, a leering rascal in a famous black-and-white make-up whose blue, innuendo-soaked patter was forever being censored and forever adored. From July 1916 he and Nat Phillips packed houses as 'Stiffy and Mo', the local answer to Charlie Chaplin.

From 1926 Mo held the Sydney Tivoli stage, on and off, until he left in 1945. His one feature film, 'Strike Me Lucky' in 1934, flopped, because the rapport with a live crowd was the whole secret of his comedy. Radio suited him far better, and his show 'McCackie Mansion' on 2GB drew huge audiences in the late 1940s.

He died in 1954. His catchphrases worked their way deep into Australian slang, and in 1975 the live-entertainment awards were renamed the Mo Awards in his honour, with a statuette shaped like Mo McCackie. They were handed out until 2021.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Australian Variety Theatre Archive

properties.name: Dorothea Mackellar
properties.place: Point Piper, where she was born
properties.summary: Australia's best-loved patriotic poem, 'My Country', was written by a homesick Sydney harbourside girl while she was far away in England.
body: Dorothea Mackellar was born in 1885 at Dunara, the family home at Point Piper, into a wealthy and well-connected Sydney family. Her father was the physician and politician Sir Charles Mackellar. As a young woman she travelled often, and it was while she was homesick in England that she found the words for the poem that would outlast everything else she wrote.

She drafted 'My Country' in her teens, around the age of nineteen. Its famous lines, 'I love a sunburnt country, a land of sweeping plains', grew out of visits to the family's rural properties near Gunnedah. The poem first appeared not in Australia but in London's Spectator in September 1908, under the title 'Core of My Heart'.

She went on to publish more verse and several novels, yet nothing she did ever eclipsed those few stanzas. Later in life she built a cottage called Tarrangaua above Lovett Bay on Pittwater, a quiet place reachable mostly by water. It was a fitting retreat for a poet who had taken the whole continent as her subject.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Edgeworth David
properties.place: The University of Sydney, his geology department
properties.summary: Welsh-born professor of geology at the University of Sydney who, at fifty, climbed Mount Erebus and trekked to the South Magnetic Pole.
body: Tannatt William Edgeworth David came from Wales and took the chair of geology at the University of Sydney in 1891. His early work was anything but cloistered. He mapped the Hunter Valley coalfields through the 1880s and located the Greta seam, the deep band of coal that would carry much of the colony's mining wealth for decades. Then, at the age of fifty, he sailed south to Antarctica.

On Shackleton's Nimrod expedition he led the first party to climb Mount Erebus, the active volcano on Ross Island, in March 1908. The next summer he set out again, hauling a sledge with Douglas Mawson and Alistair Mackay all the way to the region of the South Magnetic Pole. The round trip ran to roughly two thousand kilometres over about four months, one of the longest journeys of its kind made under human power alone.

When war came he refused to sit it out. In 1915, aged fifty-seven, he proposed that Australia raise a corps of miners and tunnellers, then took a commission as a major and served as a geologist on the Western Front, advising on the mine warfare fought in the clay beneath the trenches. He was knighted in 1920. By the time he died in August 1934 he was one of the best-loved scientists in the country, and the colony he had adopted gave him a state funeral.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; University of Sydney

properties.name: Arthur Phillip
properties.place: Sydney Cove, where he raised the flag
properties.summary: Arthur Phillip led the First Fleet to Port Jackson and founded the colony of New South Wales, holding a starving, ill-equipped settlement together as its first governor.
body: Arthur Phillip led the First Fleet to Port Jackson and raised the flag at Sydney Cove on 26 January 1788. As first Governor of New South Wales he held a starving, ill-supplied settlement together through years when collapse looked certain. He kept to an unusually conciliatory line with the Eora, forbidding any reprisal even after he was speared at Manly. More than anyone, he is the reason Sydney came to exist.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Watkin Tench
properties.place: Sydney Cove and Rose Hill, where he was stationed
properties.summary: A marine officer of the First Fleet whose two books are the warmest and sharpest eyewitness account we have of Sydney's first years.
body: Watkin Tench sailed with the First Fleet as a captain-lieutenant of marines on the transport Charlotte. He left behind the most vivid and humane record of the colony's early years, set down in two books. 'A Narrative of the Expedition to Botany Bay' came out in 1789, and 'A Complete Account of the Settlement at Port Jackson' followed in 1793. The first proved so popular it was reprinted across Europe.

He wrote about the Eora people with curiosity rather than contempt, and he was among the first Europeans to reach the river Governor Phillip named the Nepean. He also recorded the slow creep of famine as supplies ran low, noting that gloom and dejection spread over every countenance.

Stationed at Rose Hill, Tench grumbled with dry wit that the posting had kept him off the list of discoverers of the Hawkesbury. He sailed for home in December 1791. His clear-eyed, compassionate pages remain the closest thing we have to standing in the early streets of Sydney and looking around.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Lloyd Rees
properties.role: The painter of the harbour light
properties.place: Northwood, on the Lane Cove River
properties.summary: Lloyd Rees was one of Australia's most admired landscape painters, who from 1934 made his home at Northwood on the Lane Cove River and painted Sydney Harbour and its light across half a century.
body: Lloyd Rees built an Italianate villa at Northwood, on the Lane Cove River, in 1934, and from there he painted the harbour for the rest of his working life. He chased the harmony of water, headland and sky, and the pursuit won him the Wynne Prize in 1950 for The Harbour from McMahons Point. He took the prize again in 1982 at the age of 87, that time for a Tasmanian scene, Morning on the Derwent.

He and a circle of friends called themselves the Northwood group, heading out to paint around the harbour at places like Ryde and Balls Head. Among them was Roland Wakelin, a friend Rees had known since their days as young commercial artists in Sydney.

As his eyesight failed in old age, his harbour paintings grew more luminous and drifted toward abstraction, with light increasingly swallowing form. It was the culmination of a lifelong search for radiance. Born in Brisbane and died in Hobart, Rees painted Sydney Harbour into the Australian imagination.
properties.source: Art Gallery of NSW; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Norman Lindsay
properties.role: The Bulletin's provocateur, and the Magic Pudding
properties.place: The Bulletin, where he drew for half a century
properties.summary: For more than fifty years Norman Lindsay was the great provocative pen of Sydney's Bulletin, and he wrote and drew the children's classic The Magic Pudding.
body: Norman Lindsay was a painter, etcher, sculptor and cartoonist who joined Sydney's Bulletin as a staff artist in 1901 and stayed with the paper for more than half a century. In 1913 he took over as its Sydney staff cartoonist. His sensuous pen-and-ink nudes and pagan mythologies scandalised conservative Australia, and some of his work was censored and seized.

His lightest project became his most loved. He wrote and drew The Magic Pudding in 1918 on a bet that children prefer food to fairies. The result was Albert, the grumpy walking pudding who is endlessly eaten and endlessly reforms, and the book has never been out of print.

Born in the goldfields town of Creswick in country Victoria in 1879, Lindsay made his life's work in Sydney and at his home and studio at Faulconbridge in the Blue Mountains, now the Norman Lindsay Gallery. His concrete nudes still stand among the gardens there. He died in 1969, having outraged and delighted the country for sixty years.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; National Trust

properties.name: Neville Wran
properties.role: 'Nifty', the Balmain boy who ruled NSW
properties.summary: Neville 'Nifty' Wran was the Balmain boy who became the longest-serving Labor Premier of New South Wales, leading the state from 1976 to 1986.
body: Neville Wran grew up in working-class Balmain and trained as a barrister before politics took hold. He became Labor Premier of New South Wales in 1976 and held the office until 1986, longer than anyone before him. The voters loved him. He swept back into power with a huge majority in 1978, the win the papers dubbed the 'Wranslide', and did it again in 1981. The courtroom polish sat easily beside a Balmain street-fighter's nerve, and for a decade that combination made him the hardest man to beat in state politics.

His government left marks that outlasted him. In 1984 the parliament decriminalised sex between adult men in New South Wales, carried on a conscience vote that Wran championed. In October 1982 his cabinet banned logging in the state's rainforests and protected roughly 120,000 hectares in the north-east. Asked years later what he was proudest of, he answered simply that he had saved the rainforests.

Wran also served as federal president of the Labor Party through the 1980s, steadying a party that was learning how to win again. When he resigned the premiership in 1986 he walked away on his own terms, still the dominant figure in the room.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Parliament of NSW

properties.name: Gough Whitlam
properties.summary: Gough Whitlam, the member for Werriwa from the migrant south-west, became Prime Minister in 1972 and was dismissed by the Governor-General in 1975, the only Australian PM removed that way.
body: Gough Whitlam won the seat of Werriwa at a by-election in 1952 and threw in his lot with Sydney's working-class, migrant south-west. From 1957 he and his wife Margaret lived in a modest house at 32 Albert Street, Cabramatta. Constituents could be seen coming up the drive through the kitchen window, and they were asked inside. He spent years fighting for ordinary things for the area, including getting the streets connected to the sewer.

Sworn in as Prime Minister in December 1972 on the 'It's Time' wave, Whitlam ended 23 years of Labor opposition and set about remaking the country at a sprint. In three years his government brought in Medibank, abolished university fees, ended conscription, and in 1975 at Wave Hill in the Northern Territory he poured a handful of red soil into Vincent Lingiari's hand as a sign of returning land to the Gurindji people.

On 11 November 1975, amid a deadlock over the budget, Governor-General Sir John Kerr dismissed him. No other Australian Prime Minister has been removed from office that way. His Cabramatta home is now a heritage site cared for by the Whitlam Institute, a small house that still tells you a great deal about the man.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Whitlam Institute

properties.name: John Macarthur
properties.role: Wool baron and Rum Rebellion instigator
properties.place: Elizabeth Farm, Parramatta, the Macarthur home
properties.summary: John Macarthur was a NSW Corps officer who helped pioneer Australia's fine-wool industry and was the civilian ringleader of the 1808 Rum Rebellion against Governor Bligh.
body: John Macarthur was at once the most pivotal and the most poisonous man in the early colony. An officer of the New South Wales Corps, he helped found the fine-wool trade that would underwrite Australia's wealth. He carried colonial fleece to England, where in 1802 it was judged the equal of the best Spanish wool, and he came home with grants of land and sheep from the royal flock to breed from. Much of the real work, though, fell to his wife Elizabeth, who ran the flocks at Parramatta and Camden through his two long absences in England.

The second of those absences came because Macarthur was a fugitive. He was the civilian instigator of the 1808 Rum Rebellion, when the Corps deposed Governor William Bligh on the colony's twentieth anniversary. It remains the only successful armed takeover of government in Australian history.

Litigious and vindictive, Macarthur once boasted that he had never failed to ruin a man who had become obnoxious to him. He pushed his Camden Park estate past 60,000 acres and took a seat in the colony's first Legislative Council in 1825. In 1832 he was removed from it, formally pronounced a lunatic, and he died at Camden in 1834.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Lottie Lyell
properties.summary: Lottie Lyell was Australia's first film star and, working behind the camera as well, one of the country's earliest women filmmakers.
body: Charlotte Edith Cox was born in Balmain in 1890 and took the stage name Lottie Lyell. She became the first star of Australian cinema, lifted to fame by her lead in Raymond Longford's The Romantic Story of Margaret Catchpole in 1911. But stardom was only part of what she did. Across a fifteen-year partnership with Longford she acted, wrote, edited and co-directed, and she had a hand in some of the most important films of the silent era.

Audiences knew her best as Doreen in The Sentimental Bloke of 1919, still regarded as the high point of the Longford and Lyell films. In 1921 she wrote, edited and co-directed The Blue Mountains Mystery, work that won real praise. The credits of the day rarely recorded a woman doing any of this, so her full contribution stayed hidden for decades.
Tuberculosis cut her life short. She died in 1925, aged 35. Australia's screen industry now honours her with an award given in her name.
properties.image_caption: A studio portrait of Lottie Lyell
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: H.V. "Doc" Evatt
properties.place: Practised at the NSW Bar, Sydney
properties.summary: The Sydney jurist and Labor leader who presided over the United Nations as it adopted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights.
body: Herbert Vere Evatt, known across the country as Doc, was born at East Maitland in 1894 and made his name as a brilliant Sydney barrister. He took silk young and joined the High Court of Australia in 1930 at just 36, still the youngest appointee in the court's history. In 1940 he left the bench for politics and soon became Labor's wartime Minister for External Affairs.

In September 1948 he was elected President of the United Nations General Assembly, the only Australian ever to hold the post. He was presiding in Paris on 10 December 1948 when the Assembly adopted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, a cause he had pressed Australia to back. He went on to lead federal Labor through the 1950s as Leader of the Opposition, then closed his career as Chief Justice of New South Wales. He died in 1965.
properties.image_caption: H.V. Evatt as President of the UN General Assembly, 1948
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Australian Human Rights Commission

properties.name: Jørn Utzon
properties.place: Designed the Opera House; never saw it built
properties.summary: Jørn Utzon was the young Danish architect who won the Opera House competition in 1957, then resigned in 1966 and never returned to see his sails finished.
body: In January 1957 a little-known Danish architect named Jørn Utzon won the international competition to design the Sydney Opera House. His drawings showed a cluster of soaring, sail-like shells, and engineers would spend years puzzling out how such curves could actually be built. The eventual answer was elegant: every shell was cut from the surface of a single imaginary sphere, so the same geometry could be repeated and cast again and again.

Costs climbed and a bitter fight broke out with the incoming state government over money and control of the work. Utzon resigned on 28 February 1966, with the building still half-built, and flew out of Australia that April. The decision stirred an outcry, and around a thousand people marched on State Parliament demanding he be reinstated, but he was never invited back. He refused to watch others compromise his design, and so he never returned.

When the Opera House opened in 1973, the man who imagined it was absent. Reconciliation came slowly. From 1999 the Opera House Trust re-engaged him to write a set of design principles to guide the building's future, and in 2003 he was awarded the Pritzker Prize, architecture's highest honour. He died in 2008, having known his masterpiece only through photographs.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Sydney Opera House Trust

properties.name: Miles Franklin
properties.place: Lived at Carlton, in Sydney's south
properties.summary: She wrote 'My Brilliant Career' at nineteen, then left her estate to found Australia's foremost literary prize.
body: Stella Maria Sarah Miles Franklin was born at Talbingo in 1879 and wrote 'My Brilliant Career' before she turned twenty. It is a brash, vivid novel about a country girl who refuses marriage for a life of her own. Australian publishers turned it down, so the manuscript reached Henry Lawson, who recognised its worth and helped see it into print in 1901.

Fame embarrassed her, and she spent the better part of two decades abroad, working in the feminist and labour movements in Chicago and London. She returned to Australia in 1927 and settled at Carlton, in Sydney's south. She kept writing, often under the pen-name Brent of Bin Bin, and died in 1954. Her will founded the Miles Franklin Literary Award, first won in 1957 by Patrick White for 'Voss'. It remains the country's most coveted literary prize.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: William Charles Wentworth
properties.summary: Born to a convict mother off Norfolk Island, William Charles Wentworth crossed the Blue Mountains, founded the colony's first free newspaper, and won New South Wales the right to govern itself.
body: Born off Norfolk Island to a convict mother, William Charles Wentworth spent his life confounding the colonial gentry who scorned his origins. He helped find a way over the Blue Mountains in 1813, founded the colony's first free newspaper, and won New South Wales the right to govern itself. The radical of his youth hardened into a conservative landowner with harsh views on Aboriginal people, but he also drove the founding of the University of Sydney and was given the colony's first state funeral.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Maria Lock
properties.summary: Maria Lock was a Darug woman, daughter of Yarramundi, who excelled at school and became one of the colony's earliest Aboriginal landholders.
body: Maria Lock was a Darug (Dharug) woman of the Boorooberongal clan, whose Country lay near the Hawkesbury, or Deerubbin. She was a daughter of Yarramundi, a senior man of the Richmond people, and in December 1814 she became one of the first children admitted to the Native Institution at Parramatta.

She made the record books in 1819. The Sydney Gazette reported that a Darug girl of about fourteen, understood to be Maria, had taken first prize at the annual school examination, ahead of the other Institution children and close to a hundred European pupils. In January 1824 she married Robert Lock, a convict carpenter assigned to work for her. The sources call it the first officially sanctioned union between a convict and an Aboriginal woman.

Maria spent decades holding and defending land. Grants came at Blacktown and Liverpool through the 1830s, and in 1843 she secured the thirty-acre grant of her brother Colebee, a rare assertion of Aboriginal land rights in the colonial system. She died at Windsor in 1878. Many Darug families today trace their descent to her.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Conrad Martens
properties.summary: Conrad Martens was colonial Sydney's finest landscape painter, and he came to the harbour by way of HMS Beagle, where he sketched alongside the young Charles Darwin.
body: Conrad Martens was born in London in 1801 and reached his subject by an unlikely path. In 1833 he joined HMS Beagle as the ship's artist, striking up a friendship with the young naturalist Charles Darwin that lasted the rest of his life. He left the ship at Valparaiso in late 1834, and after a stretch in New Zealand he sailed into Sydney in 1835 and stayed for good.

He soon became the colony's pre-eminent landscape and watercolour painter. Martens turned the harbour, the Heads and the surrounding bush into luminous, carefully observed views that now serve as a primary record of how early Sydney actually looked. His eye was topographer and artist at once.

In 1844 he built a Gothic sandstone house, Rockleigh Grange, at St Leonards on the North Shore, drawn by its sweeping views across the water. He worshipped at the nearby St Thomas' Church and made its font with his own hands. The friendship with Darwin endured. When the Beagle had called at Sydney in 1836, Darwin commissioned work from him, and the two corresponded for decades. Martens painted the harbour until his death in 1878.
properties.image_caption: Conrad Martens, 'View of Sydney from St Leonards' (1851)
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Art Gallery of NSW

properties.name: John Dunmore Lang
properties.summary: John Dunmore Lang was the colony's first Presbyterian minister and a fierce republican who fought convict transportation and ran his own migration schemes.
body: John Dunmore Lang stepped ashore at Sydney Cove in May 1823, the first Presbyterian minister the colony had seen. He founded Scots Church in the city and set about building a Presbyterian school system from almost nothing. The pulpit could never hold him for long.

Over half a century Lang ran newspapers, sat in the Legislative Council, and organised whole shiploads of Protestant migrants to come and settle New South Wales. He campaigned hard against convict transportation and won a seat for Sydney in 1850 by beating the pro-transportation candidate. Years before most colonists dared think it, he argued out loud for an independent Australian republic.

He was combative, litigious and forever landing in trouble, including a spell in gaol. Yet few colonial voices were louder or more original. Lang kept arguing his case, in print and in parliament, right up to his death in 1878.
properties.image_caption: The Reverend Dr John Dunmore Lang
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Albert 'Tibby' Cotter
properties.place: Played for NSW at the SCG
properties.summary: Albert 'Tibby' Cotter was the fastest bowler Australia had in the years before the First World War, and he was killed in the Light Horse charge at Beersheba in 1917.
body: Albert 'Tibby' Cotter bowled at frightening pace for New South Wales and Australia, often on the Sydney Cricket Ground, where his bouncers and shattered stumps drew big crowds. He was not a tall man, but he was about as quick as anyone in the world in his time. Between 1904 and 1912 he played 21 Tests and took 89 wickets.

When war came he joined the First AIF and served with the Light Horse in the Middle East. He was killed on 31 October 1917 at Beersheba, the day of the famous mounted charge that captured the wells. He had been acting as a stretcher-bearer and was dismounting when a Turkish soldier shot him dead at close range. A well-loved story has him calling out 'this is the last over, boys' just before the end, though no biographer has confirmed he ever said it.

A century later the Albert 'Tibby' Cotter pedestrian bridge opened over Anzac Parade at Moore Park in 2015. It carries crowds across to the cricket and football grounds where he made his name, and never came home to play again.
properties.source: Australian War Memorial; ESPNcricinfo

properties.name: Daniel Deniehy
properties.place: Spoke at the Victoria Theatre, Pitt Street
properties.summary: Daniel Deniehy was the native-born orator who mocked a proposed colonial nobility as a 'bunyip aristocracy' and helped laugh the idea out of existence.
body: Daniel Henry Deniehy was born in Sydney in 1828 and grew into the colony's most dazzling public speaker. On 15 August 1853, before a packed protest meeting at the Victoria Theatre on Pitt Street, he tore into W.C. Wentworth's scheme for a hereditary colonial peerage. With one phrase he sank it. He called the proposed nobility a 'bunyip aristocracy', a 'mushroom order' of 'Botany Bay magnificos'. The crowd roared, popular feeling swung hard against the plan, and the idea of a homegrown House of Lords died in the laughter.

Deniehy was a lawyer, journalist and man of letters as much as a politician. He won a seat in the Legislative Assembly for Argyle in 1857 and later for East Macquarie in 1860, around the Bathurst district. His gifts were real, but drink and misfortune wore him down. He died in Bathurst in October 1865, aged only 37, after a fall in the street. His ridicule of inherited rank still echoes whenever Australians bristle at borrowed titles.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Sir Thomas Mitchell
properties.summary: Sir Thomas Mitchell was Surveyor-General of New South Wales for 27 years, led four inland expeditions, and fought the colony's last known duel.
body: Major Sir Thomas Livingstone Mitchell was a Scottish soldier turned surveyor who ran the mapping of New South Wales like a military campaign. He arrived in 1827, became Surveyor-General in 1828, and held the post until he died in 1855. Under him the colony took shape on paper, with road lines, town surveys and the cuttings that carried traffic down from the Blue Mountains.

He led four great expeditions into the interior between 1831 and 1846. He chased a rumoured northern river, traced the Darling down to the Murray, and in 1836 crossed into the lush grasslands of western Victoria, which he named 'Australia Felix'. His final journey in 1845 and 1846 looked without success for an overland route to Port Essington in the tropical north.

Mitchell was a combative man to the end. In 1851 Stuart Donaldson, the future first Premier, criticised over-spending in his department, and Mitchell challenged him to a duel. On 27 September the two men fired three shots each. One tore through Donaldson's hat and another passed close to Mitchell's throat before their seconds called the matter settled. It stands as the last known official duel in the colony. Mitchell died four years later at his Darling Point home, Carthona, still Surveyor-General.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Harry Seidler
properties.summary: The Viennese refugee who, more than any other architect, gave Sydney its modern skyline.
body: Harry Seidler was born in Vienna in 1923, into a Jewish family that scattered when the Nazis took Austria. He reached England as a student, only to be interned there as an enemy alien and shipped to a camp in Canada. Freed in 1941, he studied architecture at the University of Manitoba, then crossed to Harvard to learn from the Bauhaus masters Walter Gropius and Marcel Breuer. He arrived in Sydney in 1948 to build a house for his parents.

That house was Rose Seidler House, finished in 1950 on a bush site at Turramurra, now part of Wahroonga. A glass-and-steel box floating above the gum trees, it won the Sulman Medal for 1951 and scandalised a suburban city that had never seen anything like it. Seidler was twenty-eight.

Over the next half-century he remade the centre of Sydney. Blues Point Tower, the round Australia Square, the MLC Centre, the curving Horizon and Grosvenor Place all carried his stamp. He could be combative and never softened his modernist convictions. The Royal Institute of British Architects gave him its Royal Gold Medal in 1996, and he kept working until his death in 2006.
properties.image_caption: Harry Seidler, photographed by Max Dupain, 1954
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Yabba (Stephen Gascoigne)
properties.summary: Stephen Gascoigne, the door-to-door rabbit seller known as Yabba, became Sydney's most famous cricket barracker, booming his commentary across the SCG from the grassy Hill.
body: Stephen Harold Gascoigne was born at Redfern in 1878 and earned his living as a rabbito, hawking dressed rabbits door to door around Balmain and the inner west. The nickname Yabba came from his boyhood habit of talking, and talk he did. From the open Hill at the Sydney Cricket Ground he held court for the better part of three decades, rarely missing a Test or a Sheffield Shield match.

His real weapon was a voice that carried clear across the ground, paired with a wit faster than most of the bowling on show. The crowd loved it and the players mostly did not. Lines like 'your length's lousy but you bowl a good width' and, to a batsman idling between deliveries, 'whoa there, he's bolted' have been passed down as gospel, though the best of them have surely been polished in the retelling.

Yabba died in 1942, but the Hill never quite forgot him. In 2008 a bronze by Sydney sculptor Cathy Weizmann was unveiled at the ground, showing him seated with a hand cupped to his mouth, mid-bellow, returned to the spot he ruled for a generation.
properties.image_caption: The bronze statue of Yabba at the Sydney Cricket Ground
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; SCG Trust

properties.name: Clive James
properties.summary: Kogarah-born writer and broadcaster whose memoir turned his Sydney boyhood into one of the best-loved stories in Australian letters.
body: Clive James was born in Kogarah in 1939 and grew up there and at Jannali, raised by his mother after his father's death. Albert James had survived a Japanese prisoner-of-war camp, only to be killed in 1945 when the plane carrying him home flew into a typhoon and crashed. That loss sat at the centre of everything his son later wrote.

Decades on, James turned the suburban world of his childhood into 'Unreliable Memoirs' (1980), a comic gem of backyards, billycarts and small catastrophes. It became one of the most cherished opening acts in Australian writing.

He had sailed for England in 1962 and made his name there as a television critic, essayist, poet and broadcaster of rare wit and range. He died in Cambridge in 2019. His Sydney boyhood, sunlit and accident-prone, stayed the subject he wrote about best.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; 'Unreliable Memoirs'

properties.name: Johnny O'Keefe
properties.summary: Johnny O'Keefe, the singer they called the Wild One, was the first Australian rock'n'roller to crack the local charts and the roaring face of a brand new sound.
body: John Michael O'Keefe was born in 1935 and grew up in Sydney's eastern suburbs around Bondi. Billed as the Wild One, he became the first Australian rock'n'roller to reach the national charts. His 1958 anthem 'Wild One', released overseas as 'Real Wild Child', was followed by 'Shout!' and then in early 1960 by his first number one, 'She's My Baby'. As host of television's 'Six O'Clock Rock' from 1959, he beamed the new sound into living rooms right across the country.

A serious car accident on the Pacific Highway near Kempsey in 1960 left him with severe head injuries, and his later years were dogged by ill health and personal trouble. He died in Sydney in 1978, aged 43, mourned as the man who gave Australian pop its first homegrown star.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Gerard Krefft
properties.summary: The German-born scientist who turned the Australian Museum into a serious institution, then was sacked and, by the museum's own account, carried out of the building still sitting in his chair.
body: Johann Ludwig Gerard Krefft was born in Germany in 1830 and became curator of the Australian Museum in 1864. He built it into a serious scientific institution. As a zoologist and palaeontologist he studied the colony's snakes and its extinct megafauna, and he was an early champion of Darwin's new theory. In 1870 he published the first scientific description of the Queensland lungfish, a creature he saw as a living fossil. He named it Ceratodus forsteri, though he first took it for a giant amphibian rather than a fish.

A long and bitter feud with the museum's trustees ended in his dismissal in 1874. By the museum's own retelling, Krefft refused to go, barricaded himself inside, and was finally carried bodily out of the building while still seated in his chair. The museum keeps that chair to this day. The drama may have grown with the telling, but the eviction was real, and so was the injustice he felt.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Australian Museum

properties.name: Lucy Osburn
properties.summary: Trained under Florence Nightingale, Lucy Osburn was sent to reform Sydney's hospital in 1868 and is remembered as a founder of professional nursing in Australia.
body: Lucy Osburn trained under Florence Nightingale at St Thomas' Hospital in London, then arrived in Sydney on 5 March 1868 with five nurses to overhaul the squalid Sydney Infirmary. The mission followed an appeal from Henry Parkes to Nightingale for help. Osburn had barely found her feet when, on 12 March, she was put in charge of nursing Prince Alfred, the Duke of Edinburgh, who had been shot and wounded at a picnic ground at Clontarf.

As Lady Superintendent she introduced Nightingale-style training and discipline, and she is now counted among the founders of professional nursing in Australia. Her sixteen years in the role were far from smooth. She clashed bitterly with the hospital board, and at times even with Nightingale, who came to doubt her.

Osburn resigned in 1884 and returned to England, where she did district nursing among the poor. She died of diabetes at Harrogate in 1891. The hospital she reformed grew into Sydney Hospital, and her name endures through a foundation that still bears it.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Lucy Osburn–Nightingale Foundation

properties.name: Dame Mary Gilmore
properties.summary: A poet and tireless campaigner for the poor and the powerless, Dame Mary Gilmore lived her last years in Kings Cross and now appears on the Australian ten-dollar note.
body: Mary Gilmore was born near Goulburn in 1865 and worked as a teacher before becoming a poet, journalist and lifelong campaigner for workers, the poor and Aboriginal people. In late 1895 she sailed for South America to join Cosme, the utopian colony William Lane had founded in Paraguay after his first 'New Australia' settlement fell apart. She married a fellow colonist there in 1897 and had a son, but the dream soured, and the family came home disillusioned in 1902.

Back in Sydney she edited the women's page of the Australian Worker for more than twenty years and never stopped agitating for the underdog. She was made a Dame in 1937. In her later decades she lived in a flat on Darlinghurst Road in Kings Cross, a small, fierce presence who kept writing and arguing into very old age.

Gilmore died in 1962, aged 97, and was given a state funeral, the first for an Australian writer since Henry Lawson in 1922. Since 1993 her portrait and her wartime poem 'No Foe Shall Gather Our Harvest' have appeared on the reverse of the ten-dollar note.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Rose Scott
properties.summary: From her Woollahra home, suffragist Rose Scott ran a famous weekly salon and helped lead the campaign that won New South Wales women the vote.
body: Rose Scott was raised near Singleton and had settled in Sydney by the 1880s, where she turned her Woollahra home, Lynton, in Jersey Road, into a celebrated weekly salon. Politicians, judges, writers and reformers gathered there, packed in among her books and pictures, and her drawing room became one of the city's quiet engines of reform.

As secretary of the Womanhood Suffrage League of New South Wales, founded in 1891, Scott was a driving force in the long campaign that won NSW women the vote in 1902. She went on to found the Women's Political Education League and served as its first president. Among the causes closest to her was raising the age of consent for girls, lifted from fourteen to sixteen in 1910.

Scott also pressed for reform of women's prisons and urged women to keep their distance from the established party machines. That independence set her apart as the politics of the new century took shape. She died at Lynton in 1925.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Samuel Terry
properties.summary: Samuel Terry was the convict who clawed his way to the top of colonial Sydney, becoming the richest and most powerful moneylender in the colony.
body: A Manchester labourer convicted of stealing stockings in 1800, Samuel Terry stepped off the transport at Sydney in 1801 with a seven-year sentence. He served his time, set up a shop at Parramatta, then moved into the trade that made his name. From a Pitt Street inn he built a fortune as a publican, shopkeeper and, above all, a moneylender. Borrowers who could not pay forfeited their land, and Terry foreclosed his way to vast holdings. Between 1817 and 1820 he held more than a fifth of all the mortgages registered in New South Wales.

His rise scandalised the colonial gentry, who could not abide an ex-convict wielding such power. Terry took it in his stride. He became a director of the Bank of New South Wales, a leading spokesman for the emancipists, and earned the nickname 'the Botany Bay Rothschild'. When he died in 1838 his personal estate was reckoned at around 250,000 pounds, an almost unimaginable sum, with land that defied valuation. His life laid bare the disorienting truth of the early colony, that a transported felon could end up owning much of the town that had punished him.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: James Squire
properties.summary: James Squire was the First Fleet convict who grew the colony's first hops and brewed its first proper beer at Kissing Point.
body: James Squire came out on the First Fleet of 1788 as a convict, and after his emancipation settled at Kissing Point on the Parramatta River, in what is now Ryde. There he took up the trade that made his name. He is generally credited as the colony's first commercially successful brewer of true barley-and-hops beer, and as the first person to cultivate hops successfully in Australia. In March 1806 he carried two of his own hop vines to Government House, heavy with ripe clusters, and Governor King was so taken with them that he ordered a cow given to Squire from the government herd.

His Malting Shovel tavern, licensed in 1798, became a riverside landmark where thirsty Sydneysiders rowed up for a pot of ale. An earlier settler, John Boston, had brewed a rough corn beer first, so Squire's claim rests on brewing the real thing with proper ingredients. By the time he died in 1822 he was one of the colony's most respected emancipists. Nearly two centuries later a popular beer brand revived his name.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: John Verge
properties.summary: John Verge was the architect behind colonial Sydney's finest Regency and Greek Revival villas, designed in a brief and brilliant decade.
body: English-born John Verge arrived at Port Jackson in December 1828 intending to take up farming, but instead became the most fashionable architect of 1830s Sydney.
