In a short, dazzling practice he gave the colony its purest Regency and Greek Revival houses. Among them were Elizabeth Bay House for the colonial secretary Alexander Macleay, Camden Park for the Macarthur family, and the Potts Point villas Tusculum and Rockwall. His interiors at Elizabeth Bay House remain some of the most dramatic of early colonial Australia.

Most of his Sydney work was done between 1830 and 1837. He then withdrew to farming on the Macleay River in the colony's north, where he died at Austral Eden, near Kempsey, in 1861. His elegant, light-filled houses, several of them now museums, stand as the high-water mark of early colonial architecture in Sydney.
properties.image_caption: Tusculum, Potts Point, one of Verge's villas
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: David Jones
properties.summary: David Jones was the Welsh draper whose 1838 George Street shop grew into one of the world's longest-running department stores.
body: David Jones, a draper from near Llandeilo in Wales, reached Sydney aboard the Medway in September 1835. On 24 May 1838 he opened his own store at the corner of George Street and Barrack Lane. The business he founded is, by the company's own careful claim, the oldest department store in the world still trading under its original name.

Jones built a grand emporium that promised the best and most exclusive goods, and he steered it through the colony's booms and busts. He died at his Liverpool Street home in 1873. His name still crowns the flagship store that opened on Elizabeth Street in 1927, a Sydney institution nearly two centuries on.
properties.image_caption: The David Jones flagship store on Elizabeth Street
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Museums of History NSW

properties.name: Dame Eadith Walker
properties.summary: The heiress of Yaralla, the Concord estate on the Parramatta River, who turned a vast fortune to the care of Sydney's sick and wounded.
body: Eadith Walker was born at The Rocks in 1861, the only child of the Scottish merchant Thomas Walker. When she was nine the family moved to Yaralla, their grand estate on the Parramatta River at Concord. Her father died in 1886 and left her one of the largest fortunes in the colony, sworn for probate at close to a million pounds, and she set about spending it on others.

She gave to hospitals, churches and the University of Sydney, and sat on the committees of charities such as the Royal Alexandra Hospital for Children and the Royal Hospital for Women. The First World War drew out her best work. Through the Red Cross and the returned soldiers' league she did a great deal for ill and disabled servicemen, and built cottages at Yaralla, known as The Camp, for men with advanced tuberculosis.

Made a Dame in 1928, she lived on at Yaralla until her death there in 1937, her dog Cobber beside her. The estate later became the Dame Eadith Walker Convalescent Hospital. Her father's own bequest had already built the Thomas Walker Convalescent Hospital on the same riverside land, the building that now houses the Rivendell adolescent unit.
properties.image_caption: Yaralla House, Dame Eadith Walker's Concord estate
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Edmund Blacket
properties.summary: Edmund Blacket was colonial Sydney's master of the Gothic Revival, the architect of St Andrew's Cathedral, the University of Sydney's Great Hall and close to fifty churches.
body: Edmund Blacket reached Sydney from England in November 1842, just as the young city was spreading into new suburbs and towns. He became the colony's leading architect in the Gothic Revival style and the favoured designer of the Church of England in New South Wales. From late 1849 to 1854 he held the post of Colonial Architect, succeeding Mortimer Lewis, before leaving it to take on the buildings of the new University of Sydney.

His hand is everywhere in colonial Sydney. He designed St Andrew's Cathedral, taken on from 1847, and the Great Hall and Quadrangle at the university, usually counted his masterpiece. Around fifty churches carry his name, among them St Mark's at Darling Point, a sandstone Gothic building designed in 1848, and his practice also turned out schools, hospitals, colleges and lighthouses.

Admirers called him the Christopher Wren of Australia, though the label flattered him more than it described him. Blacket worked almost to the end of his life, dying in 1883. He left behind a skyline of spires and a level of craftsmanship that shaped how colonial Sydney pictured itself.
properties.image_caption: St Mark's, Darling Point, a Blacket church
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: John Fairfax
properties.summary: John Fairfax was the bankrupt English printer who bought the Sydney Herald in 1841 and built it into a paper his family would run for five generations.
body: John Fairfax was born in Warwickshire in 1804 into a settled middle-class family, and was shaped by his mother's independent Congregational faith. The cost of defending a libel case at Leamington left him bankrupt, even though he won it, and in 1838 he sailed for Sydney with his family and only a few pounds in his pocket. He found work as a compositor and then as librarian of the Australian Subscription Library.

In February 1841, with the reporter Charles Kemp, he bought the Sydney Herald and printed it from premises in lower George Street. In the early years the two men did almost everything themselves, from reporting and editing to the mechanical work of getting the paper out. They renamed it the Sydney Morning Herald in 1842. Fairfax bought Kemp out in 1853 and soon brought his son into partnership, turning the paper into the colony's journal of record.

A devout man who prized sobriety and independence, Fairfax kept the Herald cautious and respectable and made it an institution of colonial Sydney. He died in 1877 with the masthead firmly established and a family dynasty set to hold it for close to a century and a half.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Maybanke Anderson
properties.summary: Maybanke Anderson was a leading Sydney suffragist and pioneer of free kindergartens, who built her public life under the name Maybanke Wolstenholme.
body: Maybanke Anderson was born Maybanke Selfe at Kingston upon Thames in 1845 and came to Australia as a child. In 1867 she married the timber merchant Edmund Wolstenholme, and the couple built a large house called Maybanke at Marrickville. He deserted her in 1884, and the next year she opened a school in her own Frazer Street home, preparing girls for the University of Sydney entrance examination. It ran for a decade and became known as Maybanke College.

Through the 1890s she grew into a force for reform. She was a foundation vice-president of the Womanhood Suffrage League of New South Wales in 1891 and its president from 1893 to 1896, and in 1894 she began publishing the fortnightly paper Woman's Voice to press the case for the vote.

The hard years of the 1890s depression drew her to the welfare of poor children. In 1895 she helped found the Kindergarten Union of New South Wales, became its first president, and saw it open the first free kindergarten in Australia at Woolloomooloo. She took the name Anderson on marrying the philosopher Francis Anderson in 1899, and stayed an advocate for women and children until her death in 1927.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: William Wardell
properties.summary: William Wardell was the Gothic Revival architect of St Mary's Cathedral in Sydney, who also ran public works in Victoria and designed St Patrick's Cathedral in Melbourne.
body: William Wilkinson Wardell reached Melbourne in September 1858, already an accomplished ecclesiastical architect after years designing Catholic churches in Britain. The colonies gave him a far bigger canvas. He went on to design the two grandest Catholic cathedrals in the country, St Patrick's in Melbourne and St Mary's in Sydney, and in 1861 was made inspector-general of public works in Victoria, in charge of every public building the colony put up. Government House, Melbourne, is among the works attributed to him.

His Sydney connection began early. He prepared plans for St John's College at the University of Sydney in 1858 and was appointed its architect in February 1859, sending the drawings north from Melbourne. A perfectionist who did not bend easily to committees, he fell out with the college council over the next year and resigned in June 1860. The council kept his plans and carried on under the supervision of Edmund Blacket.

Wardell later moved his practice to Sydney, where St Mary's Cathedral became his great unfinished work. Its design followed his vision closely long after his death in 1899, with the building not completed until 1928.
properties.image_caption: St John's College, University of Sydney, a Wardell design
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Gregory Blaxland
properties.summary: Co-leader of the first recorded European crossing of the Blue Mountains in 1813, and a pioneer of Australian wine.
body: In May 1813 Gregory Blaxland set out with William Lawson and William Charles Wentworth to get past the Blue Mountains, the rugged barrier that had penned the colony against the coast for 25 years. Earlier parties had been turned back by the gorges. Blaxland's group kept instead to the ridgelines, edging along the high ground with packhorses and provisions. In about three weeks they reached open country to the west and opened the inland plains to settlement.

Blaxland was a settler and grazier as much as an explorer. From his Brush Farm estate near Eastwood he became one of the first colonists to make wine in any quantity, working with vine stock he believed resistant to disease. Wine he sent to London won a silver medal from the Society of Arts in 1823, the first such honour for an Australian wine, and a gold medal in 1828.

His later years were hard. Money troubles, family losses and ill health wore him down, and he died on 1 January 1853. The crossing remains the achievement attached to his name, though the wine medals mark a quieter first that shaped a whole industry.
properties.image_caption: Brush Farm House, Blaxland's Eastwood estate
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Joseph Maiden
properties.summary: Joseph Maiden ran Sydney's Botanic Gardens for nearly thirty years and became the world's leading authority on the eucalypts. He also helped launch Wattle Day and pushed the golden wattle as a national emblem.
body: Joseph Maiden took charge of Sydney's Botanic Gardens as director and government botanist in May 1896, a post he held until he retired in 1924. He had already spent sixteen years running the city's Technological Museum, but it was on the harbour's edge that he made his name. His great achievement here was the National Herbarium of New South Wales, opened in 1901, with its own museum and library to anchor the study of Australian plants.

Maiden became the world authority on the eucalypts. His Critical Revision of the Genus Eucalyptus ran to eight volumes published between 1903 and 1933, with the last parts appearing after his death. The work stayed a standard reference for more than fifty years. He wrote on the acacias, or wattles, with the same care, and dozens of plant species now carry his name.

He was also a determined champion of Australian plants in public life. In 1909 he helped found the Wattle Day movement to stir national feeling through native flora, and he pressed the case for the golden wattle as an emblem of the country. He worked among the gardens almost to the end, dying in 1925, the year after he stepped down.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: William Bede Dalley
properties.summary: William Bede Dalley was a Sydney-born barrister and famed orator who, while running the colony's government in 1885, sent the first Australian troops to an imperial war.
body: William Bede Dalley was born in Sydney in 1831 and grew into the colony's most celebrated orator, with a courtroom eloquence and a wit at the dinner table that few could match. As a barrister and politician he carried his native-born confidence into public life at a time when many in power still looked first to England.

Early in 1885, with Premier Alexander Stuart laid low by illness, Dalley was effectively running the government as attorney-general and acting colonial secretary. When news arrived that Khartoum had fallen and General Gordon was dead, Dalley offered Britain a New South Wales contingent for the Sudan. The troops sailed from Sydney on 3 March 1885. It was the first time soldiers paid by a self-governing Australian colony marched off to fight in an imperial war.

The gesture thrilled the Empire and divided opinion at home, where some saw imperial showmanship rather than necessity. It made Dalley a celebrated figure abroad, and in 1886 he became the first Australian appointed to the Privy Council. He died in 1888, and his statue still stands in Hyde Park, looking down Macquarie Street.
properties.image_caption: The Dalley statue in Hyde Park
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: William Redfern
properties.summary: The convict surgeon whose 1814 report forced lasting reform of the deadly convict transports, and whose land grant gave the suburb of Redfern its name.
body: William Redfern was a young surgeon's mate aboard HMS Standard when its crew joined the 1797 mutiny at the Nore. He was the most senior man charged, sentenced to death, then reprieved partly on account of his youth. After years in an English gaol he was transported to Sydney, arriving aboard the Canada in December 1801.

Pardoned in the colony, he rose to become one of its most respected doctors. He was the first man to gain a medical qualification in Australia and the first to train students here, and from 1816 he took charge of the General Hospital on Macquarie Street, the so-called Rum Hospital.

In 1814 he was asked to investigate the appalling death tolls aboard three convict ships, the Surry, the General Hewitt and the Three Bees. His report on diet, fresh air and sanitation led to surgeon-superintendents being placed on every transport, a change that saved countless lives at sea and is one reason he is often called a father of Australian medicine.

He resigned in 1819 after being passed over for the post of principal surgeon, and turned to farming and colonial politics. The inner-city suburb of Redfern, on land he was granted around 1817, still carries his name.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.image_caption: William Redfern, the colonial surgeon (Wikimedia Commons)

properties.name: Samuel Marsden
properties.summary: Samuel Marsden was Parramatta's senior chaplain and a magistrate so feared for his sentences that the colony's convicts called him the flogging parson.
body: Samuel Marsden landed in Sydney in 1794 as assistant chaplain and quickly made Parramatta his home, rising to become the colony's senior clergyman. He opened a Sunday school, pushed for a proper church, and saw St John's at Parramatta open its doors in April 1803.

On the magistrate's bench he handed down floggings harsh enough to brand him for life. Irish Catholic convicts in particular remembered him bitterly, and the nickname stuck. The picture is more tangled than the legend suggests. Governor Macquarie aired the charge of cruelty against him in 1817, and historians have repeated it ever since, yet recent research finds that against other magistrates of the day Marsden actually reached for the lash less often.

He was also a busy pastoralist and one of the colony's first wool exporters, and a missionary of real conviction. On Christmas Day 1814 he led the first Christian service on New Zealand soil, at Oihi Bay in the Bay of Islands. He died at Windsor in 1838 and was buried in St John's Cemetery, Parramatta, close to the church he had built.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Sir William Dobell
properties.summary: The portrait painter whose 1943 Archibald win triggered Australian art's most famous court case.
body: From an apartment above a bank on Darlinghurst Road in Kings Cross, William Dobell painted the portraits that made him Australia's most talked-about artist. When his gaunt, elongated study of fellow painter Joshua Smith won the 1943 Archibald Prize, two losing entrants took the gallery trustees to the Supreme Court. They argued the work was a caricature rather than a true portrait.
The 1944 case gripped the nation and forced a courtroom to ask what a portrait actually is. The judge dismissed the challenge, and Dobell's win stood. So did modern portraiture in Australia.

Shaken by the furore, Dobell withdrew in 1945 to Wangi Wangi on the shores of Lake Macquarie, settling into his family's old holiday home. He kept painting, won the Archibald twice more, and was knighted in 1966. He died at Wangi Wangi in 1970, one of the giants of Australian art.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.image_caption: William Dobell, photographed by Max Dupain

properties.name: Clive Churchill
properties.summary: The South Sydney fullback hailed as one of rugby league's greatest, and one of the game's first four Immortals.
body: Clive Churchill, 'the Little Master', was the fearless fullback and captain who steered the South Sydney Rabbitohs through their golden 1950s and led the Australian Kangaroos. He was small and quick, and he read the play better than anyone of his time. Many still rate him the finest fullback the code has produced, and his attacking instinct helped reshape what a fullback could be.

He first pulled on the red and green in 1947 and later coached Souths to more premierships from the sideline. In 1981 the magazine Rugby League Week named him one of the game's first four Immortals, alongside Reg Gasnier, Johnny Raper and Bob Fulton.
Since 1986 the player judged best in the grand final has been awarded the Clive Churchill Medal. Every September his name is read out again at the top of the game he once graced from Redfern Oval.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Margaret Preston
properties.summary: A leading Australian modernist who filled her canvases with waratahs, gum blossom and banksia, and who argued that the country needed an art of its own.
body: Margaret Preston was born in Port Adelaide in 1875 and settled at Mosman after her marriage in 1919. The harbour and the surrounding bush gave her the bold still lifes of native flowers that made her name. Her hand-coloured woodcuts and stencil prints had a flat, vivid energy that broke cleanly from the genteel manner of colonial painting, and her Mosman Bridge scenes are among her best-loved works.

Through the 1930s the Prestons also kept a house at Berowra, on the bushland fringe north of Sydney, where she gardened and left much of the block in its native state. It was here that she pressed her most influential and most contested idea, that a truly Australian art should learn from Aboriginal design. Scholars now read that stance critically, as a settler artist taking up First Nations imagery without the cultural right to it.

She returned to Mosman later in the decade and worked there until her death in 1963. By then she was firmly established as one of the country's most important modern painters, and her flowers and harbour scenes remain instantly recognisable.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Simeon Lord
properties.summary: A convict transported for stealing cloth who became one of colonial Sydney's richest merchants and, controversially, a magistrate.
body: Simeon Lord was convicted at Manchester in 1790 for stealing cloth and reached Sydney as a convict in 1791. Once freed, he threw himself into trade and turned his hand to almost everything the young colony could buy or sell. He worked as a wholesaler, auctioneer, shipowner and sealer, and manufactured hats, cloth and leather. By the early 1800s he had built a large stone house and warehouse beside the Tank Stream bridge, at the spot that became Macquarie Place, a landmark of the booming emancipist economy.

Governor Macquarie believed reformed convicts should be allowed to rise, and in 1810 he made Lord a magistrate. The colony's free-settler 'exclusives' were furious that a former convict should sit in judgement over others. Lord went on regardless. When his buildings were later cleared to lay out Macquarie Place properly, he was compensated for the loss.

He died in 1840 as one of the wealthiest men in New South Wales. His career became a living argument for the emancipist cause, proof that a transported felon could build a fortune and a position in colonial society.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Shane Gould
properties.summary: The Sydney schoolgirl who, at fifteen, dominated the pool at the 1972 Munich Olympics and held every women's freestyle world record at once.
body: In 1971 and 1972 Shane Gould held every women's freestyle world record from 100 to 1500 metres at the same time, a feat no swimmer of either sex has matched. She was a teenager training in Sydney's west, and the swimming world had never seen anything like her range.

At the 1972 Munich Olympics, aged just fifteen, she won three gold medals, each in world-record time, along with a silver and a bronze. The haul made her the first female swimmer to take five individual medals at a single Games, and she was named Australian of the Year.

She had learned to race at the Ryde pool and trained in the famous squad run by Forbes Carlile and his wife Ursula, while a student at Turramurra High. Worn down by the pressure of fame, she retired at sixteen. Her Munich summer is still counted among the greatest individual campaigns in Australian sport.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.image_caption: Ryde Aquatic Leisure Centre, where Gould trained

properties.name: D'Arcy Wentworth
properties.summary: An Irish-born surgeon who arrived with the Second Fleet and rose to become the colony's principal surgeon, its chief police magistrate, and one of its wealthiest men.
body: D'Arcy Wentworth sailed out with the Second Fleet, reaching Port Jackson in 1790, and began his colonial career as a hospital assistant on Norfolk Island. He returned to Sydney and was made an assistant surgeon of the colony in 1796, serving at Sydney, Parramatta and Norfolk Island over the years that followed. It was a steep climb for a man who had himself faced trial in England, on highway robbery charges, before sailing for New South Wales.

Under Governor Macquarie his rise was complete. He became principal surgeon of the colony and, in 1810, superintendent of police and chief police magistrate, holding authority over the very system that might once have judged him. He kept that police role, with a break, for much of the following fifteen years.

Wentworth proved as shrewd in business as in medicine, building up around 22,000 acres with his seat at the Home Bush estate west of Sydney. He died in 1827 one of the richest men in the colony. He is also remembered as the father of William Charles Wentworth, the explorer and statesman.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Alexander Macleay
properties.summary: Colonial Secretary of New South Wales and a devoted entomologist whose vast insect collection became the seed of Australian natural science.
body: Alexander Macleay was appointed Colonial Secretary of New South Wales in 1825 and reached Sydney in January 1826 to take up the colony's senior administrative post. He held it for eleven years, leaving under protest in January 1837 after falling out with Governor Bourke. Long before any of that, he had built up one of the finest private insect collections in the world, with a particular love of butterflies and moths.

That collection outlived him in the best way. Expanded by his sons and nephew, it passed to the University of Sydney and formed the heart of the Macleay Museum, which opened in 1890. Its specimens now sit within the University's Chau Chak Wing Museum, still earning their keep as a research collection.

Macleay also built the grand villa of Elizabeth Bay House, finished in 1837, and laid out botanical gardens around it that drew admirers from across the colony. He died in 1848 after a carriage accident, leaving a dynasty of naturalists and a body of scientific collections that still anchors the city's museums.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Billy Hughes
properties.summary: Billy Hughes rose from the Sydney wharves to lead Australia through the First World War as prime minister, the small, deaf, combative figure the troops called 'the Little Digger'.
body: Billy Hughes cut his teeth in Sydney as a union man, helping revive the Sydney Wharf Labourers' Union in 1899 and building the early Labor movement before he won a seat in parliament. He was combative, hard of hearing and famously quick to anger. In 1915 he became prime minister and steered the country through the rest of the war. Twice he tried to bring in conscription, at the referendums of 1916 and 1917, and twice he lost. The fight tore his party apart and got him expelled from Labor, after which he governed at the head of a new alliance.

He led the Australian delegation to the Versailles peace conference in 1919, where he argued hard for a small nation's interests against far larger powers. His parliamentary career ran on for more than half a century, one of the longest in the country's history. He spent his last decades on Sydney's north shore at Lindfield, and died there in 1952, aged 90.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Ruby Payne-Scott
properties.summary: Ruby Payne-Scott was the world's first woman in radio astronomy, a Sydney physicist who helped found the field and ran headlong into the marriage bar that barred wives from permanent work.
body: Ruby Payne-Scott joined the CSIR Division of Radiophysics, on the grounds of the University of Sydney, in 1941, working on classified radar research through the Second World War. From 1944 she helped invent the new science of radio astronomy, pointing surplus wartime gear at the sun. She is credited with discovering the so-called Type I and Type III solar radio bursts and with gathering data that helped pin down other types, work that places her among the first radio astronomers anywhere and the first woman among them.

She married in secret in 1944 because the Commonwealth public service refused permanent jobs to married women. When the marriage came to light in 1950 she lost her permanent post, and she left in 1951 when she was expecting her first child. For years her name slipped from view. Today she is recognised as one of the giants of Australian science and a quiet early opponent of the marriage bar.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: W. C. Piguenit
properties.summary: W. C. Piguenit was a Tasmanian-born draughtsman who became one of the first Australian-born professional landscape painters, drawn to brooding mountains and vast inland floods.
body: William Charles Piguenit was born in Hobart in 1836 and worked for years as a draughtsman and mapmaker in the Tasmanian survey office, where he learned lithography. Largely self-taught as a painter, he is often called the first Australian-born professional landscape painter. He moved to Sydney in 1880, helped found the Art Society of New South Wales, and spent the rest of his life here painting heavy skies, rugged ranges and swollen rivers.

His most famous work, 'The Flood in the Darling 1890', was painted in 1895 in response to the great floods that drowned western New South Wales. The Art Gallery of NSW bought it the same year, and it still hangs there, a wide sheet of grey floodwater under a stormy sky. Piguenit died in 1914 at his home 'Saintonge' on the Lane Cove River at Hunters Hill, among the sandstone and bush he had so often painted.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: William Cox
properties.summary: William Cox was the Hawkesbury magistrate Governor Macquarie chose to turn a rough mountain track into a road, and he drove it across the Blue Mountains to Bathurst in about six months.
body: After Blaxland, Lawson and Wentworth picked out a way across the Blue Mountains in 1813, Governor Macquarie needed someone to make a proper road of it. He chose William Cox, an English-born former soldier who had become a Hawkesbury magistrate, builder and farmer. Cox set out from Emu Plains in July 1814 with a small party of guards and about thirty convicts, guided in part by two Aboriginal men. By January 1815 they had cut roughly 160 kilometres of carriage road over the ranges to the Macquarie River, near where Bathurst now stands, a remarkable piece of early colonial engineering.

The convicts who built it were promised their freedom for the work, and they got it. Cox himself was rewarded with a large land grant and a sum of money. He farmed at Clarendon near Windsor and served the Hawkesbury district for decades, dying in 1837, with his mountain road having opened the inland to the colony.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: John Oxley
properties.summary: John Oxley was the colony's Surveyor-General who charted the inland rivers and explored Moreton Bay, naming the Brisbane River and marking the site of the future Queensland capital.
body: John Oxley became Surveyor-General of New South Wales in 1812, and he both mapped the settling colony and pushed beyond its edges. His expeditions of 1817 and 1818 traced the Lachlan and Macquarie rivers deep into the interior. In 1823 he explored Moreton Bay and rowed up a river he named the Brisbane, choosing the spot that would become the Queensland capital.

Between expeditions Oxley ran his estate 'Kirkham' near Narellan, south-west of Sydney, where he built stables that still stand and where he died in 1828. His surveys shaped the roads, towns and land grants of the early colony.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Arabanoo
properties.role: Eora man, the first captive go-between
properties.summary: Arabanoo was the Eora man seized at Kayeemy (Manly Cove) on the last day of 1788, taken to teach the colonists his language, who later died of smallpox while caring for his own people.
body: By the end of 1788 Governor Phillip had grown frustrated that the Eora kept their distance, so he ordered that a man be captured to learn the British tongue and open a dialogue. On 31 December, at Kayeemy, the place the colonists called Manly Cove, marines under Lieutenant George Johnston and Lieutenant Henry Ball ambushed two men. One escaped. The other was Arabanoo. A rope was thrown around his neck and he was dragged into the boat.

Held at first in handcuffs at the settlement, Arabanoo was treated with growing regard. He gave Phillip's officers words of his language, learned some English in return, and was often seen taking tea at Government House. After a few months his fetters were struck off, and he chose to stay. The linguist Jakelin Troy has called this forced exchange the first linguistic experiment in the colony.

When smallpox tore through the harbour clans in 1789, Arabanoo helped find and nurse the dying, among them two orphaned children, Boorong and Nanbaree, who survived. He caught the disease himself and died on 18 May 1789. Phillip, who had come to esteem him, buried him in the garden of Government House, a rare colonial honour for a man taken by force.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; City of Sydney, Barani

properties.name: William Balmain
properties.summary: The First Fleet surgeon whose harbourside land grant gave Balmain its name.
body: William Balmain sailed with the First Fleet in 1788 as a surgeon's mate aboard the transport Alexander. He worked his way up to principal surgeon of the colony by August 1796, tending convicts and settlers through fevers, accidents and the hungry early years at Port Jackson.

Balmain had a temper to match his position. In 1796 he sided with a settler against soldiers of the New South Wales Corps and fell into a furious quarrel with the officer John Macarthur, calling him a base rascal and an atrocious liar. The row almost ended in a duel, but both men stepped back and agreed to let the matter rest.

In 1800 Governor Hunter granted him 550 acres on the peninsula west of the town. Balmain never settled there. Within a year he signed the whole grant over to a Scottish surgeon, John Gilchrist, for five shillings to clear a debt. He returned to England and died in London in November 1803, aged 41. The suburb that grew on his land still carries his name.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Henry Kendall
properties.summary: One of colonial Australia's best-loved poets, who wrote the bush in a kindlier light than those before him.
body: Henry Kendall gave colonial Australia some of its most quoted verse. 'Bell-Birds', with its channels of coolness, and the elegiac 'The Last of His Tribe' still turn up in anthologies. He was born near Ulladulla on the south coast in April 1839, and through the 1860s he worked in Sydney's Lands Department and then the Colonial Secretary's office. He married Charlotte Rutter, a doctor's daughter, in 1868, all the while wrestling with poverty and drink.

Where earlier writers had found the Australian bush harsh or alien, Kendall wrote it with affection, in a music that felt local rather than borrowed from Europe. London's Athenaeum praised him, and at home readers took him to heart.

His later years brought some steadiness, including a post as inspector of state forests arranged by his old friend Sir Henry Parkes. Tuberculosis took him in Surry Hills on 1 August 1882, aged forty-three. He was mourned as a national poet, and generations of Australian schoolchildren would learn his lines by heart.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: George Caley
properties.summary: George Caley was Sir Joseph Banks's botanical collector in New South Wales, who scoured Sydney and the mountains for new plants from his base at Parramatta.
body: Sent out by Sir Joseph Banks, George Caley arrived in 1800 and made Parramatta his home for a decade. From a government cottage there, and as keeper of the small botanic garden, he ranged across the Cumberland Plain and into the foothills to collect and describe the colony's strange new flora for his patron in London. Blunt, independent and tireless, he was among the first to study the eucalypts in earnest, and the specimens and seeds he shipped home helped Europe make sense of the Australian bush.

In November 1804 he set out from near Richmond with three convicts and pushed into the Blue Mountains. Beaten back by the deep gorges of the Grose Valley, he named a peak Mount Banks for his patron before turning for home. It was a failed crossing nine years before Blaxland, Lawson and Wentworth found a way through. Caley sailed back to England in 1810, and his Sydney collections remain a foundation of Australian botany.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: William Lawson
properties.summary: William Lawson was the third man on the 1813 Blue Mountains crossing, a surveyor and grazier who built his fortune at Prospect, west of Sydney.
body: A soldier of the New South Wales Veteran Company, William Lawson joined Gregory Blaxland and William Charles Wentworth in May 1813 to make the first recorded European crossing of the Blue Mountains. His training as a surveyor mattered. The careful record of times and distances in his journal still lets the route be retraced today, and it was that route which finally opened the interior to the colony.

In 1819 he was made commandant of the new settlement at Bathurst, holding the post until he retired in 1824. He built his wealth as a grazier and from his estate, Veteran Hall, on the rise at Prospect, where he died in 1850.

The estate was resumed in the 1880s for the great Prospect Reservoir, but the house itself stood empty for decades before it was demolished in 1929. Its foundations and a cairn built from the old bricks still mark the spot, and Prospect Hill remains the country Lawson made his own.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Nanbaree
properties.summary: A Cadigal boy orphaned by the 1789 smallpox who grew up between two worlds and later went to sea along the coast.
body: Nanbaree was a young Cadigal boy carried into the settlement at Sydney Cove in April 1789, gravely ill with the smallpox that had already killed his mother and father and so much of his people. He survived. Surgeon-General John White took him into his household, and Nanbaree grew up fluent in both worlds, a nephew of the leader Colebee and a companion of Bennelong.

As a young man he went to sea. He made voyages to Norfolk Island on HMS Reliance, and in 1802 he sailed with Matthew Flinders and Bungaree aboard the Investigator. He travelled north as far as the Whitsunday Islands before homesickness took hold, and he came home to Sydney on the damaged Lady Nelson.

He died at Kissing Point on 12 August 1821. At his own wish he was buried there in the same grave as Bennelong, who had been laid to rest in James Squire's orchard in 1813. Two Eora men who had lived through the colony's first and most wrenching years now share the one resting place.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; City of Sydney, Barani

properties.name: Allan Cunningham
properties.summary: The King's botanist who gathered the colony's plants for Kew and pushed through the ranges to open new country.
body: Allan Cunningham arrived in Sydney in December 1816 as a collector for the Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew, and he spent the next two decades botanising across eastern Australia. He often rode with the explorers, joining John Oxley on the Lachlan and sailing the coast with Phillip Parker King. Plants were the point, but Cunningham had a sharp eye for country as well.

In 1823 he found Pandora's Pass through to the Liverpool Plains, and in 1828 he located Cunningham's Gap, the route linking the Darling Downs to the sea. Both openings would shape where graziers and settlers pushed next.

He came back to Sydney in 1837 to run the Botanic Garden as Colonial Botanist, taking over from his late brother Richard. The job soured quickly, since he had little patience for growing vegetables for the governor's table, and he resigned within a year. Worn out, he died of consumption in Sydney in 1839, leaving thousands of specimens that became a cornerstone of Australian botany.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Hamilton Hume
properties.summary: Hamilton Hume was the colonial-born bushman who, with William Hovell, led the first overland push from the settled districts towards Port Phillip in 1824.
body: Born near Parramatta in 1797 and raised on his family's grant at Appin, on the southern rim of the Sydney basin, Hamilton Hume was one of the first Australian-born men to make his name as an explorer. He was ranging the bush from his teens, and much of his skill came from the Aboriginal people he travelled with, knowledge that carried him through country where imported habits failed.

In 1824, with William Hovell, he led the great overland expedition south from the settled districts towards Port Phillip, the future Melbourne. The party crossed the swollen Murrumbidgee on a punt improvised from a tarpaulin and a cart, then reached a broad river near present-day Albury that they could not cross. Hume named it the Hume after his father. Charles Sturt renamed it the Murray six years later.
Hume later settled on the Yass Plains, far to the south-west, living at Cooma Cottage until his death in 1873. The Hume Highway between Sydney and Melbourne still carries his name.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Christopher Brennan
properties.summary: A Sydney-born poet and classical scholar who was among the most ambitious figures of early Australian literature, and whose university career ended in scandal and drink.
body: Christopher Brennan was born in Harbour Street, Haymarket, in 1870, the eldest surviving child of Irish Catholic migrants. He studied classics at the University of Sydney and won a travelling scholarship to Berlin. There he fell under the spell of the French Symbolist Stephane Mallarme, an encounter that shaped the dense, allusive poetry he would spend the rest of his life writing.

His major work was a carefully ordered sequence titled 'Poems: 1913', though it actually reached print in 1914. Learned and difficult, it found few ordinary readers. Later critics judged it the first Australian book of its kind, a serious attempt to write within the great European poetic tradition, and poets such as A. D. Hope and Judith Wright treated Brennan as a point of departure.

He taught at the University of Sydney for decades, rising to associate professor and head of German and comparative literature by 1921. In June 1925 the university removed him on the grounds of adultery, a fall that came soon after his companion Violet Singer died in a tram accident. His last years were marked by poverty and heavy drinking. He died of cancer at Lewisham in 1932.
properties.image_caption: Christopher Brennan, photographed by May Moore
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Mortimer Lewis
properties.role: Colonial Architect of New South Wales
properties.summary: Mortimer Lewis was Colonial Architect of New South Wales from 1835 to 1849 and gave early Victorian Sydney some of its finest public buildings, before an inquiry into the first Australian Museum ended his career.
body: English-born and trained, Mortimer Lewis arrived in Sydney in 1830 and rose to become Colonial Architect of New South Wales in 1835. Over the next fourteen years he oversaw an ambitious run of public works, designing churches, schools, police stations, courthouses and gaols. Among them were the Darlinghurst Court House, the colony's first purpose-built courthouse, and Sydney's first purpose-built museum, the building that grew into today's Australian Museum.

That museum proved his undoing. The project ran badly over budget and an official inquiry blamed Lewis, finding he had misused materials meant for the works. He resigned in 1849 under the cloud of the inquiry. His personal finances unravelled in the following years, and he was eventually declared insolvent after losses on property ventures.

For himself he designed Richmond Villa, a picturesque Gothic Revival cottage built behind Macquarie Street and facing the Domain around 1849. He did not get to enjoy it for long. The house stood near the heart of the colony's official quarter and later sat in the way of the growing Parliament.

In the late 1970s, to clear room for extensions to Parliament House, Richmond Villa was carefully taken apart and rebuilt on Kent Street at Millers Point. It now houses the Society of Australian Genealogists and remains a rare surviving example of Lewis's domestic Gothic work.
properties.image_caption: Richmond Villa, Millers Point, designed by Mortimer Lewis
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; NSW State Heritage Register

properties.name: Boorong (Abaroo)
properties.place: Sydney Cove, the Rev. Richard Johnson's household
properties.summary: Boorong was a young Burramattagal woman who survived the smallpox catastrophe of 1789 and lived for a time in the colonial chaplain's household, becoming one of the first Aboriginal people at Sydney Cove to learn English and to carry words and meaning between her people and the British.
body: Boorong was a girl of the Burramattagal, the Eora clan whose Country lay along the Parramatta River, and a daughter of the elder Maugoran. In the autumn of 1789 a smallpox epidemic tore through the peoples of Port Jackson. Boorong caught the disease but survived. Found gravely ill, she was nursed at the settlement by Arabanoo, the Eora man held captive at Government House, who soon died of smallpox himself.

For about eighteen months from 1789 she lived in the household of the colony's chaplain, the Reverend Richard Johnson, and his wife Mary, where she learned English and was taught the Lord's Prayer. Together with the boy Nanbaree, who had also survived the epidemic, she became an early go-between, helping the British and the Eora make sense of one another. Even so, observers at the time noted how little could really pass across that gap.

She returned to live with her own people and visited the Johnsons from time to time. Her later life is recorded only faintly. She is remembered as a wife of Bennelong, and the Dictionary of Sydney names her as his last wife, the woman buried beside him in his grave at Kissing Point. She is thought to have died around 1813. Even her name is unsteady across the First Fleet journals, written as Boorong, Booron, Abaroo and Araboo, a reminder of how partially her story survives in the colonists' records.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Cora Gooseberry
properties.summary: Cora Gooseberry was a senior Eora woman and a widow of Bungaree who, for two decades, was one of the best known Aboriginal figures in colonial Sydney. The colonists gave her engraved brass gorgets naming her Queen of Sydney and Botany.
body: Cora Gooseberry, her Aboriginal name recorded as Caroo or Kaaroo, was born around 1777, a daughter of Moorooboora, a leader of the Murro-ore-dial clan of the eastern Sydney coast. She became a wife of Bungaree, the Broken Bay man who sailed around the continent with Matthew Flinders and acted as a go-between with the British. After the death of Bungaree's first wife, Matora, in the 1820s, Cora became his principal wife.

Bungaree died in 1830, and for some twenty years afterwards Cora remained a familiar presence in the town, instantly recognisable in her blanket and headscarf, smoking a clay pipe. The colonists gave her two engraved brass breastplates styling her Queen of Sydney and Botany. Those were titles imposed from outside and had nothing to do with Aboriginal forms of authority.

She camped around the harbour and the Domain and slept outside city hotels. A sympathetic publican let her stay at his Sydney Arms Hotel in Castlereagh Street, where she was found dead on 30 July 1852. She was buried in the old Devonshire Street cemetery, and her headstone was later moved to the Pioneers Cemetery at Botany.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Julian Ashton
properties.place: Julian Ashton Art School, The Rocks
properties.summary: Julian Ashton was an English-born painter who became colonial Sydney's most influential art teacher. A champion of painting in the open air, he founded the art school that trained much of a generation of Australian artists.
body: Julian Rossi Ashton arrived from England in 1878 to work as an illustrator and became a tireless advocate for Australian art and for painting directly from nature, out of doors. As a trustee of the Art Gallery of New South Wales in the 1890s, he pushed it to buy the work of living local painters, and in 1890 he secured its first purchase of an Arthur Streeton.

In 1890 he founded his own art school in Sydney, first called the Academy Julian and long known as the Sydney Art School, and it trained many of the country's leading artists. It moved over the years before settling in George Street, The Rocks, where it continues today as the Julian Ashton Art School. It is often called the oldest art school of its kind still running in Australia.
Ashton taught and encouraged generations of students until his death in 1942. His insistence on drawing, and on plein-air observation, helped shape the course of Australian painting.
properties.image_caption: The Julian Ashton Art School, George Street, The Rocks
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Art Gallery of NSW

properties.name: Matthew Flinders
properties.place: State Library of NSW, Macquarie Street
properties.summary: Matthew Flinders was the Royal Navy navigator who sailed from Sydney to chart the whole coast of the continent, and who did more than anyone to give it the name Australia. His statue, with that of his cat Trim, stands outside the State Library.
body: As a young officer, Matthew Flinders explored the Sydney coast with the surgeon George Bass in a tiny open boat they called Tom Thumb, poking into Botany Bay and the Georges River in 1795 and Port Hacking the next year.

Between 1801 and 1803 he commanded HMS Investigator on the first complete circumnavigation and charting of the Australian coastline. He departed Port Jackson in July 1802 and returned the following June. In his great chart and book he argued for the name Australia, which Governor Macquarie later helped make official.

Detained for years by the French on Mauritius on his way home, Flinders died in London in 1814, just as his life's work was published. Outside the State Library of New South Wales, his statue looks down Macquarie Street, with a small bronze of his faithful cat Trim perched on a ledge behind him.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Arthur Streeton
properties.summary: Arthur Streeton was one of Australia's greatest landscape painters. In the 1890s he lived at the artists' camp at Little Sirius Cove and turned the blue and gold of Sydney Harbour into some of the best loved pictures in the nation's art.
body: Arthur Streeton made his name in Melbourne as a leader of the Heidelberg School, the painters who took their easels out of doors to capture the Australian light. In the early 1890s he came to Sydney, drawn by the harbour.

Through the 1890s he lived at Curlew Camp, a bohemian tent camp on the bush-clad foreshore of Little Sirius Cove at Mosman, sharing it for a time with Tom Roberts. From there he painted shimmering views of the harbour, works such as From My Camp (Sirius Cove), that hang today in the Art Gallery of New South Wales.
Streeton's sunlit landscapes helped shape how Australians saw their own country. He was knighted for services to art in 1937 and died in 1943.
properties.source: Art Gallery of NSW; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Sir John Robertson
properties.summary: Sir John Robertson was Premier of New South Wales five times and the driving force behind the 1861 Land Acts, which let ordinary settlers select farmland and loosened the squatters' grip on the colony.
body: John Robertson was a blunt and wildly popular figure with a salty tongue and a cleft palate that drew cruel jibes from rivals all his life. None of it slowed him down. Between 1860 and 1886 he led New South Wales as Premier on five separate occasions, dominating colonial politics for a generation.

His lasting legacy is the Robertson Land Acts of 1861. They brought in free selection before survey, which let small farmers pick and buy Crown land ahead of the surveyors and pushed back against the squatters who had locked up the interior. The reform remade rural New South Wales for decades, with results that were celebrated by some and bitterly resented by others.

In his later years Robertson lived at Clovelly, a fine house on the heights of Watsons Bay, where he died in 1891. The house was demolished in 1903, and the grounds were later opened as a public reserve. In 1909 it was renamed Robertson Park in his honour.
properties.image_caption: Sir John Robertson, by Alfred Clint
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Dally Messenger
properties.summary: Herbert 'Dally' Messenger was rugby league's first superstar, and his switch from union in 1907 gave the breakaway code the drawcard it needed when the Sydney competition kicked off in 1908.
body: Herbert Messenger was born at Balmain in 1883 and grew up in the boatsheds of Double Bay, where his father built boats. He picked up football at the local school and went on to become the most brilliant rugby player of his day. The nickname 'Dally' came from the politician W. B. Dalley. The grander title 'The Master' came later, from admiring English crowds.

In 1907 Messenger sensationally crossed from amateur rugby union to the new professional game, playing a paid series against a touring New Zealand team and lending his fame to the rebel code. When the New South Wales Rugby League competition began in 1908, his presence with Eastern Suburbs pulled the crowds that kept the venture alive in its first shaky season.

A centre with a famous side-step and a long, raking kick, he steered Easts to three premierships in a row from 1911 to 1913 before he retired. More than anyone, Dally Messenger is remembered as the man who made rugby league in Australia. The game's highest individual honours, the Dally M Awards, still carry his name.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: George Bass
properties.summary: A young naval surgeon who explored the coast around Sydney in a tiny open boat with his friend Matthew Flinders, proved Van Diemen's Land was an island, and then vanished at sea.
body: George Bass reached Sydney in 1795 as surgeon aboard HMS Reliance and threw himself straight into exploring. With Matthew Flinders he made two voyages in a little open boat he had brought out with him, named the Tom Thumb for its size. They charted the Georges River, and in 1796 a second Tom Thumb carried them south of Botany Bay, where they sheltered at Wattamolla and found Port Hacking at the foot of the Cronulla peninsula.

Over the summer of 1797 to 1798, Bass sailed an open whaleboat far down the coast and came to believe that a strait cut the mainland off from Van Diemen's Land. The following summer he and Flinders sailed right around Tasmania in the sloop Norfolk and settled the question. On their return, Flinders proposed to Governor Hunter that the passage be named Bass Strait, and so it remains.

In 1803 Bass sailed from Sydney in command of the trading brig Venus and was never heard from again. The sea he had charted so boldly kept its secret. A monument to Bass and Flinders now stands on the Cronulla headland, looking out over the waters the two young men once probed in their cockleshell boats.
properties.image_caption: A portrait reputed to be George Bass
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Mary Bryant
properties.summary: A First Fleet convict who in 1791 pulled off one of the most astonishing escapes in the colony's history, slipping out of Sydney Cove by night in an open boat and reaching Timor after thousands of kilometres at sea.
body: Mary Broad, a Cornishwoman transported for highway robbery, came out with the First Fleet and married a fellow convict, William Bryant, at Sydney Cove. As their sentences neared their end with no clear path to real freedom, the couple resolved to flee.

On the night of 28 March 1791 they slipped away in the governor's six-oared cutter, taking their two small children and seven other convicts. The voyage ran some sixty-nine days. They rowed and sailed thousands of kilometres up the coast and across open water to the Dutch port of Kupang on Timor, a feat of navigation and sheer endurance that still astonishes.

At Kupang the party was eventually detained and shipped towards England for trial. William and both children died during the long passage home. Mary reached England in 1792, where her story drew wide public sympathy and the support of the writer James Boswell, who helped win her a pardon in 1793. She returned to Cornwall, and after 1794 she vanishes from the record.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Museums of History NSW

properties.name: Peter Finch
properties.summary: An actor who grew up on Sydney's lower North Shore and learned his trade on its stages and radio before international fame, and who became the first person to win a posthumous Academy Award for acting.
body: Peter Finch was born in London in 1916 and sent out to Sydney in 1926, where he was raised at Greenwich Point in the care of a great-uncle. He left school young and worked his way up through vaudeville, radio serials and repertory theatre across 1930s and 1940s Sydney, appearing in early Australian films and helping found the Mercury Theatre.

A meeting with the visiting Laurence Olivier in 1948 drew him to London, where he built an international career of real range and intensity across films such as 'The Trials of Oscar Wilde' and 'Sunday Bloody Sunday'. He won five BAFTA awards for best actor along the way.

In 1977 he won the Academy Award for Best Actor as the unhinged newsman Howard Beale in 'Network'. He had died of a heart attack in Los Angeles only weeks before the ceremony, making his the first posthumous Oscar ever awarded for acting.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; National Film and Sound Archive

properties.name: Snowy Baker
properties.summary: A Sydney sporting marvel who won Olympic boxing silver and reputedly excelled at dozens of sports, then reinvented himself as a star of Australian silent films and a riding coach to Hollywood.
body: Reginald Baker was born at Surry Hills in 1884 and earned the nickname 'Snowy' as a boy for his very fair hair. He seemed able to turn his hand to any sport, and is said to have competed in around twenty-six of them, from rugby and rowing to swimming, diving, fencing and water polo. At the 1908 London Olympics he won a silver medal in middleweight boxing, and he remains the only Australian to have represented the country in three sports at a single Games.

Back in Sydney he became a leading boxing promoter, buying the Sydney Stadium at Rushcutters Bay in 1913, before the new business of cinema caught his eye. He produced and starred in a run of Australian silent films, performing his own daring stunts on horseback.

In the 1920s he moved to the United States and became a sought-after riding coach to Hollywood stars, teaching the likes of Douglas Fairbanks and Rudolph Valentino. He died in Los Angeles in 1953, remembered as one of the most versatile athletes Australia has produced.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Sport Australia Hall of Fame

properties.name: Sir Stuart Donaldson
properties.role: First Premier of New South Wales
properties.summary: Stuart Donaldson was a Sydney wool and whale-oil merchant who became the first Premier of New South Wales in 1856, when the colony won responsible self-government. Years earlier he had fought the last formal duel in the colony.
body: Stuart Alexander Donaldson was born in London in 1812 and sailed into Sydney aboard the Emma Eugenia in May 1835. He built a fortune as a merchant in wool and whale oil before turning to public life, winning a seat on the old Legislative Council and making a name as a sharp critic of colonial spending.

That sharp tongue nearly cost him. In 1851 he attacked the over-spending of the Surveyor-General's Department, and its chief, Sir Thomas Mitchell, demanded satisfaction. On the morning of 27 September the two men faced each other with pistols. Each fired three times. One shot tore through Donaldson's hat, but both walked away, and their seconds declared honour satisfied. It is remembered as the last known official duel in New South Wales.

When Britain granted the colony responsible self-government, Governor Denison called on Donaldson to form the first ministry. On 6 June 1856 he became the first Premier of New South Wales, also holding the office of Colonial Secretary.

His government did not last. It lost the confidence of the new Legislative Assembly and gave way to Charles Cowper on 25 August 1856, a little under three months in. Donaldson stayed in politics for a time, then returned to England, where he died in 1867. He keeps his place at the head of the long line of New South Wales premiers.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Parliament of NSW

properties.name: Yemmerrawanne
properties.role: Wangal man who voyaged to England with Bennelong
properties.summary: Yemmerrawanne was a young Wangal man of the Eora who sailed from Sydney to England with his kinsman Bennelong in 1792, among the first Aboriginal people known to make the journey. He died there, far from his Country, in 1794.
body: Yemmerrawanne was a young man of the Wangal clan, whose Country lay along the southern shore of the Parramatta River. In the early years of the colony he, like Bennelong, moved between the Eora world and the British settlement at Sydney Cove.

On 10 December 1792, as Governor Arthur Phillip prepared to sail home, Yemmerrawanne and Bennelong embarked with him from Warrane, or Sydney Cove, aboard the Atlantic. The ship reached England the following May, and the two men were presented at court and shown the sights of London.

The English winters were hard on Yemmerrawanne. His health failed through a long illness, and on 18 May 1794 he died at Eltham in Kent, aged only about nineteen. He was buried in the churchyard of St John the Baptist there and never returned to his Country. His grave is one of the earliest memorials to an Aboriginal Australian abroad.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Florence Broadhurst
properties.summary: Florence Broadhurst was a flamboyant designer who, after lives as a touring singer and a London couturier, built a celebrated hand-printed wallpaper business in Sydney before her 1977 murder in her Paddington studio, a crime still unsolved.
body: Florence Broadhurst was born near Mount Perry in rural Queensland in 1899 and spent her life reinventing herself. She toured Asia as a vaudeville singer, ran a fashion house in London and worked as a painter, telling colourful stories about her past that were not always true.

In 1959 she launched a wallpaper business in Sydney, and in 1969 moved it to a studio at Roylston Street, Paddington. There her team hand-printed bold, exuberant patterns that eventually numbered around eight hundred designs. Out of step with taste at first, her work has since won an international following and is still reprinted today.

On 15 October 1977 Florence Broadhurst was found beaten to death in her studio. Despite a long investigation, the killing has never been solved. It was a dark end for one of the most original figures in Australian design.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Powerhouse Museum
properties.image_caption: Florence Broadhurst with her paintings (Wikimedia Commons)

properties.name: Sir Thomas Anderson Stuart
properties.place: Anderson Stuart Building, University of Sydney
properties.summary: Thomas Anderson Stuart was a Scottish-born physiologist who, from 1883, built the University of Sydney's medical school almost from scratch into a great institution. The grand stone building that carries his name is his monument.
body: Thomas Anderson Stuart arrived from Scotland in 1883 to take up the University of Sydney's new chair of anatomy and physiology, and he became the founding dean of its medical school. He inherited a handful of students and next to no facilities.

Sheer drive carried him the rest of the way. He lobbied for money, designed the courses, and pushed through the construction of a grand Gothic medical school building, which was largely finished around 1889. By the time he eased back, enrolments had climbed from six students to more than six hundred. To the undergraduates he was simply 'Andy', a plain and exacting lecturer who took endless care over the diagrams he chalked up on the blackboard.

Knighted in 1914, he died at his Double Bay home in February 1920. In 1960 the university renamed its medical school building the Anderson Stuart Building in his honour, a fitting tribute to the man who made Sydney a place to train doctors.
properties.image_caption: The Anderson Stuart Building, University of Sydney
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; University of Sydney

properties.name: Major George Johnston
properties.role: NSW Corps officer; leader of the Rum Rebellion
properties.summary: On Australia Day in 1808, this army officer marched his soldiers on Government House and arrested Governor Bligh, leading the only successful armed takeover of government in Australian history.
body: George Johnston sailed with the First Fleet as a marine officer aboard the Lady Penrhyn and was reputedly the first man ashore at Sydney Cove in January 1788. He rose through the New South Wales Corps over the next two decades. On 26 January 1808, with the colony's officers and traders locked in a bitter feud with Governor William Bligh, Johnston led his men to Government House and placed Bligh under arrest. The episode is remembered as the Rum Rebellion.

For a time Johnston ran the colony himself. He was eventually sent to England and court-martialled for mutiny in 1811. Found guilty, he suffered the relatively mild penalty of being cashiered, then sailed back to New South Wales, arriving in 1813 to live out his days as a settler. His long-time partner was Esther Abrahams, a convict who had come out on the same ship in 1788. The couple married at Parramatta in 1814.
Johnston named his estate on the inner-western flats Annandale, after the district in Dumfriesshire where he was born. From that farm the whole suburb later took its name.
properties.image_caption: A contemporary cartoon of the arrest of Governor Bligh, 1808
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: George Bennett
properties.summary: George Bennett was an English-born surgeon and naturalist who became an early curator of the Australian Museum and did pioneering work on the platypus. He helped lay the foundations of natural science in colonial Sydney.
body: George Bennett trained in medicine at Plymouth and in London, and first saw Sydney as a ship's surgeon in 1829. He came back to settle in 1836 and set up a successful practice. That same year he took on the new post of secretary and curator at the young Australian Museum, where he compiled its first catalogue and built up its collections. He was not the museum's first keeper, as William Galvin had looked after the specimens before him, but Bennett gave the place its first proper scientific footing.

A keen field naturalist, Bennett made important early studies of Australian animals. He was especially drawn to the platypus, whose strange biology he investigated by digging live animals from their burrows at a time when the creature was known only from stuffed skins. He also described the pearly nautilus, work that won him a gold medal from the Royal College of Surgeons in 1832. His book 'Gatherings of a Naturalist in Australasia', published in 1860, introduced many readers to the colony's wildlife.

The Royal Society of New South Wales awarded him its Clarke Medal in 1890. Bennett remained a leading figure in Sydney science for more than half a century until his death in 1893, and a string of Australian animals still carry his name.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Australian Museum

properties.name: Reg Gasnier
properties.role: Rugby league centre, St George Dragons
properties.summary: Reg Gasnier was one of rugby league's finest centres, the so-called Prince of Centres, who spent his whole career in the Red V of the all-conquering St George Dragons and was named among the game's first four Immortals.
body: Reg Gasnier burst into first grade with St George in 1959, a centre of such pace and brilliance that he was picked for Australia within months. He stayed in the famous Red V at Kogarah until 1967, scoring 127 tries in just 125 club games, a strike rate few have matched.

Those years fell inside St George's record run of eleven straight premierships, and Gasnier played in six of the winning sides. In 1962 he captained Australia for the first time at the age of 23, then the youngest man ever to lead the national side, and crowds loved his swerve and side-step.
In 1981 he was chosen as one of rugby league's first four Immortals, and in 2008 he was picked at centre in the Team of the Century. His home ground at Kogarah keeps his memory alive.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Rugby League Hall of Fame

properties.name: Sali Herman
properties.summary: Sali Herman was a Swiss-born painter who fell for the crowded terrace houses and back lanes of inner Sydney, and his affectionate pictures of The Rocks and Woolloomooloo helped change how the city saw its old quarters.
body: Sali Herman was born in Zurich in 1898 and came to Australia in 1937, settling in Sydney. There he found his great subject in the unfashionable terraces, corner shops and steep lanes of The Rocks, Woolloomooloo, Surry Hills and Paddington. Where others saw slums, he painted warmth, character and light.

He won the Wynne Prize for landscape four times, beginning in 1944 with McElhone Stairs, and took the Sulman Prize twice in the late 1940s. In 1945 he served as an official war artist, recording scenes in Lae, Bougainville and Rabaul during the Pacific campaign.

At a time when these inner-city streets were widely dismissed as slums fit only for clearing, Herman's paintings are credited with helping shift attitudes. That change of heart would later see places like The Rocks treasured rather than demolished. He died in Sydney in 1993.
properties.image_caption: Sali Herman as a war artist, 1945
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Art Gallery of NSW

properties.name: Robert Campbell
properties.role: Merchant; founder of the colony's first private trading house
properties.summary: Robert Campbell was the Scottish merchant who opened Sydney's first great free trading house and built its first private wharf at the cove that still carries his name, taking on the trade the Rum Corps officers had kept to themselves.
body: Robert Campbell sailed into Sydney to build up a trading connection for his family's Calcutta firm, and from 1798 he took up land at Dawes Point on the western side of Sydney Cove. There he raised sandstone storehouses and the colony's first privately owned wharf, complete by about 1801. From these works grew Campbell & Co., the first major trading firm in the colony that answered to neither the government nor the military.

By importing goods directly from India and beyond, Campbell broke into the lucrative trade the officers of the New South Wales Corps had run as their own. He stood firmly behind Governor William Bligh, and when the Corps deposed Bligh in the Rum Rebellion of 1808 Campbell was arrested and stripped of his public offices.

He came back from that to serve as a magistrate, as colonial treasurer, and as a member of the colony's first Legislative Council. His sons later built the handsome bond stores that still line Campbells Cove, a working reminder of the man often called Sydney's first merchant prince.
properties.image_caption: Campbell's Stores at Campbells Cove, The Rocks
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Dulcie Deamer
properties.summary: Dulcie Deamer was the New Zealand born writer and journalist crowned Queen of Bohemia in 1925, a flamboyant fixture of the Kings Cross arts scene best known for the leopard skin she wore to the Artists' Balls.
body: Born in Christchurch, New Zealand, in 1890, Dulcie Deamer was a prolific novelist, poet, playwright and journalist who settled around Kings Cross in the early 1920s and became the dazzling centrepiece of bohemian Sydney. She moved in Norman Lindsay's circle and the vaudeville world, and earned her living by her pen for decades.

She is best remembered for the brief leopard skin she first wore to the Artists' Ball on 27 September 1923, dropping into a backbend that became one of the era's defining images. Her friends crowned her Queen of Bohemia in July 1925, a title she carried for the rest of her life.

Beneath the showmanship she worked hard and widely, turning out fiction and journalism and even reporting on boxing, a rare beat for a woman of her day. She died in Sydney in 1972, the city's roaring twenties bohemia made flesh.
properties.image_caption: Dulcie Deamer in her leopard-skin costume, 1923
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Eccleston Du Faur
properties.role: Public servant, arts patron and conservationist
properties.summary: Frederick Eccleston Du Faur was a Lands Department draftsman whose enthusiasms shaped both the culture and the wild country of Sydney, helping build the Art Gallery of New South Wales and driving the creation of Ku-ring-gai Chase.
body: Eccleston Du Faur came to Sydney from London in 1853 and rose through the survey and lands offices to become a senior draftsman. Outside the office he threw himself into the colony's cultural life. He was an original member of the New South Wales Academy of Art in 1871, and when its work passed to the new National Art Gallery in 1876 he became one of its five trustees, later serving as president from 1892 until his death. He was also a founder of the Geographical Society of Australasia and funded an expedition to settle the fate of the lost explorer Ludwig Leichhardt.

His most lasting work lay in the bush on the northern edge of Sydney. After moving to Turramurra in 1888 he campaigned for years to protect the rugged country around Cowan Creek, and in 1894 some 13,500 hectares were dedicated as Ku-ring-gai Chase, among the earliest national parks anywhere. As managing trustee he opened the road down to Bobbin Head.
Du Faur died at Turramurra in 1915, having given Sydney both a great public art collection and one of its best loved wild places.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Esther Abrahams
properties.role: First Fleet convict; mistress of Annandale
properties.summary: Esther Abrahams was a young Jewish Londoner transported on the First Fleet who became the partner and later the wife of Major George Johnston, and for many years the capable mistress of his Annandale estate.
body: Esther Abrahams was a Jewish woman of about twenty, convicted at the Old Bailey in 1786 of stealing lace and sentenced to seven years' transportation. She was put aboard the First Fleet ship Lady Penrhyn with her infant daughter, born in gaol, and reached Sydney Cove in January 1788.

In the colony she became the partner of the marine officer George Johnston and bore him seven children. She showed a real head for business, running his Annandale and Georges Hall estates during his long absence in London after he led the Rum Rebellion against Governor Bligh in 1808. The couple finally married in 1814.

Johnston left her Annandale for her lifetime when he died in 1823. The arrangement did not hold. In 1829 her son Robert had her declared incapable of managing her own affairs, and she spent her final years with her son David at Georges Hall, dying in 1846. Her life ran from a London dock to the heart of a colonial landholding family.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Henry Chamberlain Russell
properties.summary: Henry Chamberlain Russell was the long-serving Government Astronomer who, from Sydney Observatory, observed the southern skies and built the network of weather stations that gave Australia its first systematic forecasts.
body: Born in Maitland in 1836, Russell graduated from the University of Sydney and joined the staff of Sydney Observatory. In 1870 he became the NSW Government Astronomer, a post he held until 1905.

He organised and equipped four Australian parties to observe the 1874 transit of Venus, designed instruments, and committed the observatory to the great international project to photograph the whole sky. His most everyday legacy was meteorology. He built a web of weather and rainfall stations across the colony and from 1877 issued daily weather maps among the first made in Australia.
A tireless organiser of colonial science, Russell was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 1886 and served almost until his death in 1907.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Museums of History NSW

properties.name: Yarramundi
properties.place: Yarramundi, on the Hawkesbury near Richmond
properties.summary: A Boorooberongal elder and clever-man of the Darug, who met Governor Phillip's party on the Hawkesbury in 1791, and whose name still marks his Country near Richmond.
body: Yarramundi, written down by the British as Yellomundee, was a senior man of the Boorooberongal clan of the Darug, the people of the Hawkesbury and Nepean country west of Sydney. He was a garadyi, a healer and clever-man held in deep respect by his people.

On 14 April 1791 he and his father Gombeeree met Governor Arthur Phillip's expedition as it explored the Hawkesbury near present-day Richmond. It was one of the few early meetings between the Darug and the British to be written down, in this case by the officer Watkin Tench.

He was the father of two figures who appear elsewhere in the colony's story. His son Colebee shared in the first land grant ever made to Aboriginal people, 30 acres near Blacktown given with Nurragingy in 1816 and finalised in 1819. His daughter Maria Lock became a noted student at the Native Institution and a matriarch of Hawkesbury Darug families. Yarramundi's own dates are uncertain, but his name endures in the locality of Yarramundi, on the river that was his Country.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Hugh D. McIntosh
properties.summary: Hugh D. McIntosh, the Sydney showman they called 'Huge Deal', built the Sydney Stadium at Rushcutters Bay and staged the 1908 world title fight where Jack Johnson became the first Black heavyweight champion.
body: Hugh Donald McIntosh was born in Surry Hills in 1876 and worked his way up from selling pies and labouring to become one of the great showmen of his age. In 1908 he built the open-air Sydney Stadium at Rushcutters Bay to stage a world heavyweight title fight on Boxing Day between the Canadian champion Tommy Burns and the American challenger Jack Johnson. McIntosh refereed it himself, and Johnson's win made him the first Black man to hold the world heavyweight crown.

From the ring he moved into theatre, taking over the Tivoli vaudeville circuit. He owned newspapers, sat in the New South Wales parliament, and built a reputation for deals that were as spectacular as his debts.

By the 1930s the deals had collapsed and McIntosh was bankrupt. He died in London in 1942. The boxing nights and variety shows he put on had already woven themselves into the fabric of popular Sydney.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Jimmy Little
