properties.summary: Jimmy Little was a Yorta Yorta singer of gentle charm and one of the first Aboriginal Australians to become a mainstream recording star, whose 1963 gospel hit 'Royal Telephone' reached number one in Sydney.
body: James Oswald Little was born on the Cummeragunja mission, on the New South Wales bank of the Murray, in 1937. He began performing professionally around 1951 and built his career in Sydney through the late 1950s, living for a time at Granville. In October 1963 his gospel-pop single 'Royal Telephone' sold tens of thousands of copies and topped the Sydney charts, carrying him into the front rank of Australian popular music.

His career ran across six decades. He recorded steadily, won audiences across the country, and in 1999 was inducted into the ARIA Hall of Fame and took out the ARIA Award for Best Adult Contemporary Album. From 1985 he gave his time back to younger Aboriginal performers, teaching at the Eora Centre in Redfern and lecturing at the University of Sydney.
In his later years Little campaigned on Indigenous kidney health through the Jimmy Little Foundation. He died in 2012, remembered as a pioneer and a gentleman of Australian music.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; National Film and Sound Archive

properties.name: Archibald Liversidge
properties.role: Chemist; founder of science at the University of Sydney
properties.summary: Archibald Liversidge was the English chemist who built science at the University of Sydney almost from scratch in the 1870s, founding its Faculty of Science and helping to organise scientific life across the colonies.
body: Liversidge arrived in 1872 at a university that taught barely any laboratory science. Appointed professor of geology and mineralogy in 1874, he pressed the senate hard for proper laboratories and a broad scientific curriculum, and his persistence paid off.

The Faculty of Science was established in 1882, with Liversidge as its founding dean, and a school of mines followed in 1892. He took the chair of chemistry in 1891. Beyond the campus he was the engine of the Royal Society of New South Wales for years, and he helped bring the Australasian Association for the Advancement of Science into being, with its first meeting held in 1888. The body later became ANZAAS, the great gathering of Australasian science.
A meticulous researcher into minerals and the chemistry of seawater and gold, Liversidge retired in 1907 and died in 1927. He left behind a university that could properly call itself scientific.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; University of Sydney

properties.name: Sir James Martin
properties.role: Three-times Premier and Chief Justice of NSW
properties.summary: Sir James Martin arrived in Sydney as an infant, the son of a Government House groom, and rose to serve three terms as Premier of New South Wales before becoming the colony's Chief Justice. Martin Place carries his name.
body: James Martin was born near Cork in 1820 and reached Sydney late the following year, the son of a groom at Government House. By sheer ability he qualified as a solicitor and then a barrister, working also as a journalist, and he grew into one of the sharpest advocates and orators the colony had seen.

He served three terms as Premier of New South Wales between 1863 and 1872. In November 1873 he was sworn in as Chief Justice, the head of the colony's courts, and he held that office until his death in 1886.

Martin lived at 'Clarens', a villa famous for its gardens on the heights of Potts Point. In 1892 the city named Martin Place after him, the grand civic spine that runs back from the General Post Office through the heart of Sydney.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Supreme Court of NSW

properties.name: Fred Spofforth
properties.summary: Fred Spofforth, 'the Demon Bowler', was the first great Australian fast bowler. His ferocious spell at the Oval in 1882 sealed the win that gave birth to cricket's Ashes.
body: Frederick Spofforth was born in Balmain in 1853 and grew into the most feared bowler of his age. Tall, hostile and cunning, he earned the name 'the Demon' after tearing through an MCC side at Lord's in 1878.

Playing first for New South Wales and then for Australia, he took the first hat-trick in Test cricket. In the famous Oval Test of August 1882 he captured 14 wickets, seven in each innings, to bowl Australia to a seven-run win over England.

That defeat prompted a mock obituary for English cricket in the press, and from it the tradition of the Ashes was born, with Spofforth its unlikely midwife. He later settled in England, where he died in 1926.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Wikipedia

properties.name: Sir Saul Samuel
properties.role: NSW's first Jewish legislator and minister
properties.place: near the Great Synagogue, Elizabeth Street
properties.summary: Saul Samuel was a merchant who became the first Jew elected to a New South Wales legislature and the first to hold ministerial office in the colony. He was also a leading figure in Sydney's Jewish community.
body: Saul Samuel was born in London in 1820 and brought to New South Wales as a boy in 1832. He prospered in trade on the goldfields and in Sydney before entering public life, and in 1854 he won a seat in the colony's Legislative Council, the first Jew elected to a New South Wales legislature.

In 1859 he became colonial treasurer, the first Jew to hold ministerial office in the colony. He served as treasurer again and as postmaster-general, then represented New South Wales as its Agent-General in London for close to two decades.

Samuel was a pillar of Sydney's Jewish community. He laid the foundation stone of the Great Synagogue in Elizabeth Street in 1875 and later became its president. Knighted for his service, he died in London in 1900.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Isaac Nichols
properties.summary: Isaac Nichols was a convict who, once freed, built a fortune as a shipbuilder and auctioneer. In 1809 he became the colony's first Postmaster, running Australia's first post office from his George Street home.
body: Isaac Nichols was transported to Sydney as a convict in 1791. He completed his sentence and rose to become one of the colony's most successful emancipists, a shipbuilder, farmer and auctioneer with land and standing.

In April 1809 the military administration that had toppled Governor Bligh appointed him the colony's first Postmaster, charged with bringing order to the chaotic handling of mail off the ships. He ran Australia's first post office from his own house in lower George Street, advertising in the press the names of those with letters to collect.
He held the post until his death in 1819, a former convict entrusted with one of the young colony's first public services.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; National Museum of Australia

properties.name: Barnett Levey
properties.summary: Barnett Levey is generally remembered as the colony's first free Jewish settler and the father of the Australian stage, the merchant who built Sydney's first licensed theatre behind his George Street hotel against official disapproval.
body: Barnett Levey arrived in Sydney in December 1821, the first free Jewish settler in the colony. He set up as a merchant and auctioneer on George Street, where he also ran the Royal Hotel.

Theatre was his real passion. The authorities were wary, fearing that playhouses would breed disorder among convicts, but Levey kept at it. In December 1832 he secured the colony's first theatre licence and opened the Theatre Royal beside his hotel, the first licensed theatre in Australia.

He died in 1837, only a few years later, leaving a widow and young children in poverty, and his theatre was lost to fire in 1840. Yet he had planted professional theatre in Sydney, and he is fondly remembered as the father of the Australian stage.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Monty Noble
properties.summary: Monty Noble was one of cricket's finest all-rounders and a much-admired Australian captain who earned his living as a Sydney dentist. The Noble Stand at the Sydney Cricket Ground carries his name.
body: Montague Alfred Noble was a mainstay of Australian cricket in the years around 1900. He was a shrewd batsman and a clever bowler, and across 42 Tests he captained the side in 15 of them, winning eight. Players and crowds alike rated him among the most respected leaders the game had produced. Away from the field he had registered as a dentist in 1901 and ran a practice in Macquarie Street.

Noble kept a long connection with the Paddington Cricket Club, where he had first made his name in grade cricket and later returned to coach. His feel for the game lasted well beyond his playing days. He served as a trustee of the Sydney Cricket Ground and went on to chair the trust, helping drive the building work that reshaped the ground.

That work left his lasting memorial. The new northern stand, raised in the mid 1930s, was named the M.A. Noble Stand in his honour. Admired for his sportsmanship as much as his skill, Noble stayed a familiar and well-loved figure in Sydney cricket until his death in 1940.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Wikipedia

properties.name: Sir George Reid
properties.role: NSW Premier and 4th Prime Minister of Australia
properties.summary: George Reid was the witty, rotund free-trade Premier of New South Wales and the fourth Prime Minister of Australia. A famously hedged speech on the federal constitution earned him the nickname 'Yes-No Reid'.
body: George Reid led New South Wales as its free-trade Premier from 1894 to 1899. He pushed for low tariffs, direct taxation and tidier public finances, and built a coherent liberal program that did more in five years than many of his predecessors managed in far longer. He went on to become the fourth Prime Minister of Australia in 1904 and 1905.

Reid was a renowned debater and a natural humorist, and he is best remembered for a Sydney Town Hall speech in 1898. He picked apart the faults of the proposed federal constitution from a free-trade view while still insisting he supported it, and the crowd could not tell which way he leaned. The press dubbed him 'Yes-No Reid'. He claimed to be rather pleased, saying that after a long political life no one could find anything worse to say about him.

He later served as Australia's first High Commissioner in London, holding the post from 1910 to 1916. He stayed on in the city after that and died there in 1918, two months before the end of the First World War.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; National Archives of Australia

properties.name: Thomas Holt
properties.role: Wool merchant, pastoralist and land developer
properties.summary: Thomas Holt was one of colonial Sydney's richest men, a wool magnate and the colony's first Treasurer under self-government. He developed huge stretches of the Sutherland Shire and raised a Gothic fantasy of a mansion called 'The Warren' at Marrickville.
body: Thomas Holt came to Sydney from Yorkshire in 1842 and made an immense fortune as a wool broker and pastoralist, at his peak controlling some three million acres of grazing country. When responsible government arrived in 1856 he served briefly as the colony's first Treasurer, holding the office for only a few months.

Holt poured his wealth into land south of the city. He bought up vast tracts of what became the Sutherland Shire, running from Botany Bay to Port Hacking, and pioneered oyster farming on the Georges River. At Marrickville, in 1864, he built 'The Warren', a turreted Gothic mansion with its own art gallery, set in landscaped grounds stocked with deer and rabbits. It was one of the great sights of Victorian Sydney.
The mansion is long gone, and its name survives only in the streets that now cover its grounds. Holt returned to England, where he died in 1888.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Sir Charles Nicholson
properties.role: First Chancellor of the University of Sydney
properties.summary: Sir Charles Nicholson was a pastoralist and politician who became the founding Chancellor of the University of Sydney. His magnificent collection of Egyptian and classical antiquities grew into the university's Nicholson Museum.
body: Charles Nicholson made his fortune in pastoral New South Wales and entered colonial politics, serving as Speaker of the Legislative Council from 1846 until responsible government arrived in 1856. His lasting monument, though, is the University of Sydney. He sat on its founding senate, helped shape the design of Australia's first university, and was its first Provost, the office later called Chancellor, from 1854 to 1862.

A passionate traveller and collector, Nicholson gathered Egyptian mummies, Greek vases and Roman sculpture during the 1840s and 1850s and presented them to the university in 1857. They became the Nicholson Museum, for many years the largest collection of antiquities in the southern hemisphere.

Knighted for his services, Nicholson moved to England in 1862 but kept a keen interest in the university until his death in 1903. His antiquities now form a centrepiece of the university's Chau Chak Wing Museum.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; University of Sydney

properties.name: William Bligh
properties.role: Bounty captain; fourth Governor of NSW
properties.summary: William Bligh was the naval captain of 'mutiny on the Bounty' fame who, as Governor of New South Wales, was himself overthrown. His own soldiers deposed him in the Rum Rebellion of 1808.
body: William Bligh was already notorious before he reached Sydney. In 1789 his crew on HMS Bounty mutinied and cast him adrift in an open boat, which he navigated some 6,700 kilometres to safety. It was a remarkable feat of seamanship, though it did nothing to soften his abrasive reputation.

Appointed the fourth Governor of New South Wales in 1806, Bligh set out to curb the trading power of the New South Wales Corps and the wealthy John Macarthur. On 26 January 1808 the Corps, led by Major George Johnston, marched on Government House and arrested him. This was the Rum Rebellion, the only armed overthrow of government in Australian history.

Bligh was held and then sent home, where London vindicated him and he rose to vice-admiral. His Sydney estate, a grant of 240 acres made in 1806, he named 'Camperdown' after the naval battle he had fought off the Dutch coast in 1797. The name lives on in the suburb.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Biddy Giles
properties.summary: Biddy Giles was a Gweagal and Dharawal woman who spent her whole life on her own Country around the Georges River and Botany Bay, earning her living as a guide and fisherwoman and holding on to her language through the hardest years of the colony.
body: Biddy Giles was born about 1810 and given the name Bi-yar-rung. She belonged to the Gweagal and held connections that reached across Dharawal Country, from the Illawarra down south to Gamay, which the British called Botany Bay. Unlike so many of her generation, she managed to live out her life on the lands and waters her people had always known, and she remained one of the last fluent speakers of the local Dharawal tongue.

Around 1860 she settled at Mill Creek on the western bank of the Georges River, a place the Dharawal called Gurugurang, with her third husband, an Englishman named Billy Giles. Together they ran fishing and shooting trips for settlers who wanted to see the river country. Travellers wrote home amazed at her skill. No matter the hour, she could put a party onto the fish, work her dogs, read the bush, and turn local food into a fine meal. Her trips ranged from Mill Creek out to the ocean and as far south as the Shoalhaven.

In her later years she was a respected elder known as Granny Giles. She shared her knowledge of language and custom with the recorder Mary Everitt and passed it on to her daughter Ellen Anderson. In 1888 she was one of several people rescued from a boat caught in a storm on Botany Bay. She fell ill not long after, died at Royal Prince Alfred Hospital in Camperdown, and was buried at Rookwood.
In 2020 a park at Kirrawee, in the Country she had lived in and loved, was named Biddy Giles Park in her honour.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography (Indigenous Australia); Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Slim Dusty
properties.summary: Slim Dusty was the king of Australian country music, the voice of 'A Pub with No Beer' and more than a hundred albums, who lived much of his long life from a home on Sydney's North Shore.
body: Born David Gordon Kirkpatrick on a farm at Nulla Nulla Creek, west of Kempsey, in 1927, he took the stage name Slim Dusty as a boy and never let it go. In 1957 he recorded 'A Pub with No Beer' in a single take at EMI's Castlereagh Street studio in Sydney. The song, adapted from a poem by Dan Sheahan and credited to Gordon Parsons, became the biggest-selling record by an Australian to that time and the first Australian single to go gold.

With his wife and lifelong collaborator Joy McKean, whom he married in 1951, he toured the country for half a century and released more than a hundred albums, an output no other Australian musician has matched. He sang the bush and the open road back to the nation, from 'Lights on the Hill' to 'Duncan'.

His roots and his songs were rural, but Sydney was his base for much of his life. He died at his home in the northern suburb of St Ives on 19 September 2003, aged 76. A state funeral followed at St Andrew's Cathedral, where thousands gathered to farewell him.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Wikipedia
properties.image_caption: Slim Dusty with a Golden Guitar award

properties.name: James Ruse
properties.role: First Fleet convict; the colony's first self-sufficient farmer
properties.summary: A Cornish convict who arrived with the First Fleet and became the first European to feed himself from the soil of New South Wales. In 1791 he received the colony's first land grant.
body: James Ruse was a Cornish farmer transported for burglary, who reached Sydney Cove with the First Fleet aboard the Scarborough in January 1788. With the young colony close to starvation, Governor Phillip set him a test. He gave Ruse a cleared allotment at Rose Hill, near Parramatta, to see whether a single settler could grow enough to feed himself.

Ruse went at it properly. He had no plough and no animals for manure, so he burned the felled timber and dug the potash-rich ash back into the ground, then hoed it thoroughly by hand. By early 1791 he was raising wheat and maize and told the authorities he no longer needed government rations. He was the first European in New South Wales to live off the land he worked. In reward Phillip granted him thirty acres he named Experiment Farm, the first land grant made in the colony, with formal title following in 1792.

Ruse sold the farm to Surgeon John Harris in 1793 and moved on, taking up land on the Hawkesbury and later at Bankstown and Riverstone. He died at Minto, near Campbelltown, in 1837. The Georgian cottage standing on the site today was built by Harris around 1835, but the ground itself is where European farming in Australia first took root.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.image_caption: Experiment Farm Cottage, built c.1835 on the site of Ruse's grant

properties.name: Des Renford
properties.role: Marathon swimmer; 'King of the English Channel'
properties.summary: Des Renford was the Maroubra swimmer who crossed the English Channel nineteen times from nineteen attempts and reigned for years as the 'King of the Channel', a sport he only took up at thirty-nine.
body: Des Renford came late to the swimming that made his name. He grew interested in long distances at thirty-nine, and after a successful crossing in 1970 he set his sights on the Channel. Over the following years the Maroubra man crossed the English Channel nineteen times from nineteen attempts, an extraordinary record for an eastern-suburbs swimmer who began so late.

That tally won him the title of 'King of the Channel', held by whoever has the most crossings. Renford wore the crown from 1975 to 1979 and again for a time in 1980. He was tough and stubborn in the water, broke the record for the swim around Alcatraz Island in San Francisco in 1977, and stayed the most prolific Australian Channel swimmer of his era. He was appointed MBE in 1976 and later honoured in the International Marathon Swimming Hall of Fame and the Sport Australia Hall of Fame.

Renford remained a Maroubra identity to the end and died in 1999. The year after, the aquatic centre at Heffron Park, where generations of eastern-suburbs swimmers have trained, was renamed the Des Renford Leisure Centre in his honour.
properties.source: Wikipedia; International Marathon Swimming Hall of Fame

properties.name: Mahroot
properties.role: Kameygal man of Botany Bay; sailor and fisherman
properties.summary: Mahroot was a Kameygal man of Botany Bay who went to sea for decades, then returned to his own Country. In 1845 he was the only Aboriginal person to give evidence to a colonial inquiry, telling it that his people were all but gone.
body: Mahroot, known to the colonists as Boatswain Maroot, was a Kameygal man born around 1793 on the northern shore of Kamay, or Botany Bay, near the mouth of the Cooks River. His father, Maroot the elder, was a senior man of the clan. As a young man Mahroot went to sea, working for close to thirty years on sealing and whaling vessels before coming home to fish the waters of his own Country and hold a small leasehold at Botany.

In September 1845 he became the only Aboriginal person to give evidence to the New South Wales Legislative Council's select committee on the condition of the colony's original inhabitants. He told it that the clan he had known as a child had numbered around 400 people, and that by 1845 only four who spoke his language remained, three women and himself as the last man. His words have come down to us in a starker form as well: 'All gone! Only me left to walk about.'

He died at Botany in January 1850. His testimony stands as one of the most direct Aboriginal accounts we have of the colony's first sixty years, a single voice describing the destruction of a whole community within one lifetime.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Nellie Stewart
properties.summary: Nellie Stewart was the most adored Australian stage performer of her generation, a singer and actress remembered above all as Sweet Nell of Old Drury. She lived and died on the heights of Mosman.
body: Eleanor Towzey Stewart, known to everyone as Nellie, was born at Woolloomooloo in 1858 into a theatrical family that traced its line back to the Drury Lane players of old London. She was on the stage as a small child and grew into the most beloved Australian performer of her age, a singer and actress whose name could fill a theatre for half a century.

Her defining role arrived on 15 February 1902, when she first played Nell Gwynne in 'Sweet Nell of Old Drury'. She returned to the part again and again over the years, and the name Sweet Nell stuck to her for life. In a long professional and personal partnership with the entrepreneur George Musgrove she held sway over the musical and dramatic stage of the J.C. Williamson era. She also made a single film in 1911, reprising Sweet Nell for the director Raymond Longford.

In her later years she lived at Den o' Gwynne on the high ground of Mosman, where she died in 1931. A memorial was later raised to her in the Royal Botanic Garden, and her name lived on as a kind of shorthand for the golden age of Australian theatre.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Ludwig Leichhardt
properties.summary: Ludwig Leichhardt was a Prussian naturalist who made Sydney his base and led an epic overland trek to the far north in 1844 and 1845. On a later attempt to cross the continent he vanished without trace.
body: Ludwig Leichhardt was born in Prussia in 1813 and reached Sydney in February 1842. He threw himself into the natural history of his new country, studying the geology and plants of the Sydney district and spending time at the Botanic Garden, whose directorship he hoped one day to hold.

In 1844 he set out from Sydney on the journey that made his name, an overland expedition of close to five thousand kilometres to Port Essington on the northern coast, near the future site of Darwin. He left the farthest station on the Darling Downs late that year, and after losing one of his party he and the survivors reached Port Essington in December 1845. He came back to a hero's welcome.

Restless for more, in 1848 he set out to cross the continent from east to west. He and his companions left the Darling Downs in the autumn and were never seen again, leaving one of the enduring mysteries of Australian exploration. The inner-western suburb of Leichhardt carries his name to this day.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Isaac Nathan
properties.summary: Isaac Nathan set Byron's verse to music in London, then in Sydney composed the first opera made in Australia. He died beneath a horse-drawn tram in 1864, the city's first such fatality.
body: Isaac Nathan, born around 1792, first made his name in London, where in 1815 he set Lord Byron's 'Hebrew Melodies' to music in a famous collaboration with the poet. He emigrated to Sydney in 1841 and quickly became the leading figure in the young colony's musical life, working as composer, teacher and choirmaster at St Mary's.

In May 1847 his ballad opera 'Don John of Austria' was staged at the Royal Victoria Theatre, the first opera both written and produced in Australia, which is one reason he is often called the father of Australian music. He composed, taught and championed music in the colony for more than twenty years.

His death was as singular as his career. In January 1864 he fell beneath a horse-drawn tram at the corner of Pitt and Goulburn Streets and was killed, Sydney's first tram fatality, on a line so unpopular that it was pulled up not long after. He was buried in Camperdown Cemetery.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: William Holman
properties.summary: The brilliant Labor orator who became Premier, then split his party over conscription in 1916 and governed on against his old comrades.
body: William Arthur Holman was born in London and raised in Sydney, and he rose through the early Labor movement as one of its finest orators and sharpest legal minds. In 1913 he became Labor Premier of New South Wales.

In 1916, as the Great War ground on, Holman backed conscription against most of his party. Labor expelled him, but he refused to give up office. He formed a new Nationalist government with his former opponents and led it until 1920, in one of the great schisms of Australian political history.
Holman lost his own seat at the 1920 election. He returned to Parliament in 1931 as the federal member for Martin, and he died in 1934 at his home in Gordon, on the North Shore.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Sir Joseph Carruthers
properties.summary: Joseph Carruthers was the reforming Premier of New South Wales from 1904 to 1907 who founded the forerunner of the state Liberal Party. He made his home at Sans Souci, in the St George district he represented in Parliament.
body: Joseph Carruthers was born at Kiama in 1857 and became one of the architects of conservative politics in New South Wales. In 1902 he founded the Liberal and Reform Association, the ancestor of today's Liberal Party, and as Premier from 1904 to 1907 he put the colony's finances back in order and overhauled local government. His Local Government Act of 1906 set up the comprehensive system of councils that still runs much of the state.

Carruthers long represented the St George district in Parliament and lived at 'Ellesmere' on the Georges River at Sans Souci, in the southern suburbs he knew well. He kept a close eye on the area's growth and championed the people he had grown up among.

He pushed hard for closer land settlement, opening nearly six million acres to small farmers, and backed the great Burrinjuck irrigation scheme on the Murrumbidgee. Knighted in 1908, he stayed a force in the Legislative Council until his death in 1932.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Sir Richard Bourke
properties.place: Sydney (his statue stands on Macquarie Street)
properties.summary: Sir Richard Bourke was the reforming governor of the 1830s who eased the convict lash, brought in trial by civil jury and granted equal aid to the churches. The colonists honoured him with the first public statue raised in Australia.
body: Sir Richard Bourke was an Irish soldier of liberal mind who governed New South Wales from 1831 to 1837 and set the colony on a more open course. He extended trial by civil jury, reined in the savage flogging of convicts by magistrates, and opened public life to emancipists who had served their sentences.

His Church Act of 1836 ended the privileged standing of the Anglican Church and placed each denomination on an equal footing before the law, with government aid shared among the Anglican, Catholic and Presbyterian churches. It became a foundation of religious pluralism in Australia.

So admired was Bourke that in 1842 the colonists unveiled a bronze statue of him, the first public statue erected in Australia. Cast in London by Edward Hodges Baily and paid for by public subscription, it looked out over the Domain for decades before being moved in 1925 to its place on Macquarie Street, in front of the State Library, where it stands today.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW
properties.image_caption: The Governor Bourke statue, in its original setting in the Domain

properties.name: John Bede Polding
properties.place: St Mary's Cathedral (which he founded; he lies in its crypt)
properties.summary: John Bede Polding was an English Benedictine monk who became Australia's first Catholic bishop and then its first archbishop, leading the colony's largely Irish Catholic flock and founding St Mary's Cathedral.
body: John Bede Polding was an English Benedictine monk who reached Sydney in September 1835 as Vicar Apostolic, charged with leading a Catholic population that was largely Irish, poor and often convict. In a colony run by Protestants, his people were widely treated as second class, and he set out to give them churches, schools and a sense of standing.

Rome approved an Australian church hierarchy in 1842, and Polding became the first Archbishop of Sydney, returning with the title in 1843. He founded religious orders, including the Sisters of the Good Samaritan, built the framework of Catholic education, and laid the foundations of St Mary's Cathedral as the mother church of Australian Catholicism. By the time he died, his original mission had grown into a dozen dioceses staffed by scores of priests.

Polding worked for his flock for more than forty years. He died in Sydney in March 1877 and was buried at Petersham. His remains were later moved into the crypt of St Mary's Cathedral, the building he had begun, where they rest today.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Sir Norman Gregg
properties.place: Royal Alexandra Hospital for Children (former site), Camperdown
properties.summary: Sir Norman Gregg was the Sydney eye surgeon who in 1941 showed that German measles in early pregnancy could damage the unborn child, the first known environmental cause of congenital disability.
body: Norman Gregg was born at Burwood in 1892 and trained in medicine at the University of Sydney. After service in the First World War he returned to build an ophthalmic practice in Macquarie Street and a long association with the Royal Alexandra Hospital for Children at Camperdown.

In 1941 he noticed an unusual wave of babies arriving with cataracts, at rates well above what heredity could explain. Listening to the mothers and combing through case records, he traced the pattern to an epidemic of rubella, or German measles, that had passed through them in early pregnancy. In October that year he read his paper, 'Congenital Cataract following German Measles in the Mother', to the Ophthalmological Society of Australia. It was the first work to show that an infection during pregnancy could harm an unborn child, and it opened the modern understanding of congenital rubella syndrome.
Epidemiologists later confirmed and broadened his finding, which reshaped antenatal care around the world and built the case for rubella vaccination. Gregg was knighted in 1953.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Millicent Preston-Stanley
properties.summary: Millicent Preston-Stanley was the first woman elected to the New South Wales Parliament, in 1925, and she used her single term to campaign against maternal deaths and for the rights of women and children.
body: A leading figure in the Feminist Club of New South Wales, Preston-Stanley won a place in the multi-member Eastern Suburbs electorate at the 1925 state election as a Nationalist candidate. She was the first woman to enter the NSW Legislative Assembly, and when she rose to speak in August 1925 she became the first woman to address the chamber as an elected member.

In her brief term she pressed hard on the causes that defined her. She fought to reduce the appalling toll of maternal mortality, argued for equal guardianship rights for mothers, and called for better obstetric training and child welfare. When the Eastern Suburbs seat was abolished in the 1927 redistribution, she stood for the new single-member seat of Bondi and lost, but she kept campaigning in public life for years afterwards.
She was the second woman elected to any Australian parliament, after Edith Cowan in Western Australia. In 1934 she married Crawford Vaughan, a former premier of South Australia.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; NSW Parliament

properties.name: Lillian Armfield
properties.summary: Lillian Armfield was one of the first two women appointed to the New South Wales Police, in 1915, and she spent more than three decades policing the inner-city underworld through the razor-gang years.
body: A former nurse from Mittagong, Armfield was taken on as a probationary special constable on 1 July 1915 alongside Maude Rhodes, the first two women appointed to the NSW Police. Working from the Criminal Investigation Branch, they had unusual duties and, at the start, limited formal powers. They were given no uniform, no overtime and no right to compensation or a pension, yet Armfield came to exercise the same powers of arrest as the men beside her.

Through the 1920s and 1930s she worked the streets of Surry Hills and Darlinghurst at the height of the razor wars. She dealt again and again with the rival vice queens Tilly Devine and Kate Leigh, and with the sly-grog and cocaine trades that flourished around them.

Armfield rose to lead the NSW Women's Police and retired in 1949 after some thirty-four years of service. Popular accounts often call her 'Australia's first policewoman'. She is more accurately remembered as one of the first two, and among the longest serving.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; NSW Police

properties.name: Adelaide Ironside
properties.place: St Paul's College, University of Sydney (holds her major work)
properties.summary: A Sydney-born painter of the colonial republican circle, among the first Australian-born artists to train in Europe, whose great canvas 'The Marriage at Cana of Galilee' now hangs in the Art Gallery of New South Wales.
body: Born in Sydney in 1831 and raised by her widowed mother, Ironside moved in the radical, nationalist circle of Daniel Deniehy and the Reverend John Dunmore Lang. She wrote pro-republican verse for colonial magazines before she turned seriously to painting.

Encouraged by Lang, she sailed for Europe with her mother in 1855, among the first Australian-born artists to go abroad for training. She settled in Rome and threw herself into fresco technique, working punishing hours. A staunch Protestant, she still won a private audience with Pope Pius IX in 1861 and his permission to copy works in the papal collections.

Her most ambitious canvas was 'The Marriage at Cana of Galilee', for which she modelled the head of Christ on the Italian patriot Giuseppe Garibaldi. She died of tuberculosis in Rome in 1867, aged just thirty-five. The painting was brought home to Sydney and held for decades by St Paul's College at the University of Sydney, until the College presented it to the Art Gallery of New South Wales in 1992.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Design & Art Australia Online

properties.name: Leonard Keysor VC
properties.summary: A young Londoner barely settled in Sydney when war broke out, who enlisted at Randwick and won the Victoria Cross at Lone Pine for catching and hurling back live Turkish grenades.
body: Leonard Keysor was born in London in 1885 and had spent ten years in Canada before landing in Sydney, where he found work as a clerk only about three months before the First World War began. On 18 August 1914 he enlisted in the AIF, joining the 1st Battalion, and within weeks was bound for Egypt.

He came ashore at Gallipoli on 25 April 1915. In the savage fighting at Lone Pine in early August, Keysor proved a master of the bomb. As Turkish grenades dropped into the trenches he smothered them with sandbags or his coat, and when there was time he caught them in the air and threw them straight back, as though fielding at cricket. Twice wounded, he kept it up for some fifty hours and held the position. For this he was awarded the Victoria Cross.
He survived the war and returned to live in London, where he died in 1951. His Victoria Cross is held by the Australian War Memorial in Canberra.
properties.source: Australian War Memorial; Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Sir Charles Rosenthal
properties.place: Holy Trinity Church, Dulwich Hill (his design)
properties.summary: A Sydney architect who became a First World War general, designing churches and city buildings before rising to command an Australian division on the Western Front.
body: Charles Rosenthal qualified as an architect in Victoria, then built a Sydney practice from about 1900. His love of church music drew him to ecclesiastical work, and his buildings include Holy Trinity at Dulwich Hill and, in 1929, Scots Church on York Street in the city. He was twice president of the Institute of Architects of NSW and served for a time as a Sydney alderman.

A part-time soldier before the war, he rose through the AIF to Major-General. In May 1918 he took command of the 2nd Australian Division and led it through the hard-won victories of Hamel, Peronne and Mont St Quentin.

Back home he rebuilt his practice and went into politics, sitting first in the NSW Legislative Assembly and later the Legislative Council. From 1937 to 1945 he administered Norfolk Island. Architect, soldier, musician and politician, he was one of the more remarkable Sydney figures of his generation.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Australian War Memorial

properties.name: Sir Leslie Morshead
properties.place: Morshead Memorial Fountain, Royal Botanic Garden
properties.summary: Leslie Morshead was the Australian general who held Tobruk against Rommel, commanding the men known as the Rats of Tobruk, and who in civilian life ran a Sydney shipping line. A fountain at the Royal Botanic Garden remembers him.
body: Leslie Morshead trained as a schoolteacher before the First World War took him into the army. Between the wars he built a business career in Sydney with the Orient shipping line, and he eventually became its general manager in Australia.

In 1941 he commanded the 9th Australian Division through the siege of Tobruk. His men, the Rats of Tobruk, held the Libyan port against Rommel from April until Morshead handed over and left in October. The following year he led the division to the decisive victory at El Alamein.

After the war he returned to Sydney business life as chairman of David Jones and president of the Bank of New South Wales. He died of cancer at St Vincent's Hospital, Darlinghurst, in September 1959. The Morshead Memorial Fountain at the Macquarie Street entrance to the Royal Botanic Garden honours him.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Australian War Memorial

properties.name: Sir Iven Mackay
properties.place: University of Sydney (studied and lectured here)
properties.summary: Iven Mackay was a Sydney University physics man who became a soldier, commanding the 6th Division in its first hard victories of the Second World War at Bardia, Tobruk and in Greece.
body: Iven Mackay took his degree at the University of Sydney and went on to teach science at Shore before returning to the university as a physics lecturer. When war came in 1914 he joined the 4th Battalion, and in 1916 he led it in the savage fighting at Pozieres on the Western Front. His troops later gave him the nickname Mr Chips, a nod to the schoolmaster's manner he never quite shed.

In April 1940 he was chosen to command the 6th Australian Division. Early in 1941 it stormed the Italian fortress of Bardia and took Tobruk, the first big Australian victories of the new war. Then came Greece, where the division was thrown into a losing campaign and fought a hard withdrawal.

Promoted lieutenant-general, Mackay went on to lead Australia's Home Forces and then served as the country's first High Commissioner to India. He died at his East Lindfield home in 1966, a scholar who had spent his middle years as a general.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Australian War Memorial

properties.name: Gordon Bennett
properties.place: Sydney CBD (worked here as an accountant between the wars)
properties.summary: The general who slipped away on the night Singapore fell, and who, in peacetime Sydney, worked as a city accountant and once helped govern the city itself.
body: Gordon Bennett led two lives in Sydney. From 1919 he worked as a city accountant and company director, and in 1928 he was one of three commissioners appointed to run the City of Sydney.

His fame, and his infamy, came from soldiering. He was a daring brigade commander in the First World War. In 1942 he led the 8th Australian Division through the disastrous Malayan campaign to the fall of Singapore.

On the night the garrison surrendered, Bennett handed his command to Brigadier Cecil Callaghan and escaped by sampan across the Strait of Malacca, convinced he had to bring home the hard lessons of fighting the Japanese. His men went into captivity. A 1945 royal commission found the escape was not justified, and the argument followed him to the end of his life.
After the war he turned to growing fruit at Glenorie, on Sydney's rural fringe. He never held a field command again.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Australian War Memorial

properties.name: Sir Charles Bickerton Blackburn
properties.summary: Charles Bickerton Blackburn was a Royal Prince Alfred physician and Macquarie Street consultant who later served twenty-three years as Chancellor of the University of Sydney.
body: Charles Bickerton Blackburn graduated in medicine from the University of Sydney in 1899, topping every year of his course, and began a lifelong tie to Royal Prince Alfred Hospital. From a practice that started in College Street and moved to Macquarie Street, he became one of the most sought-after consulting physicians in the city. He was a founder of the Royal Australasian College of Physicians and its first president.

In 1941, already in his sixties, he was elected Chancellor of the University of Sydney. He held the post for twenty-three years, through the upheaval of the war and the great post-war wave of new students, and is said to have conferred more than thirty thousand degrees before he retired in 1964 at the age of ninety.
Knighted for his services to medicine and learning, he is remembered in the Blackburn Building, the old medical school named in his honour. He died in Sydney in 1972, aged 98.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography

properties.name: Sir John Northcott
properties.summary: John Northcott was wounded at the Gallipoli landing on the first Anzac Day, rose to head the army during the Second World War, and in 1946 became the first Australian-born Governor of New South Wales.
body: John Northcott was a regular officer of the small pre-war army when the Great War broke out. He landed at Gallipoli on the first Anzac Day in 1915, was wounded, and was invalided home, taking no further part in the fighting. He stayed in uniform between the wars and rose steadily through the ranks.

In 1942 he was appointed Chief of the General Staff, the officer responsible for administering and training the wartime army. After the war he commanded the British Commonwealth occupation force in Japan, the first Australian to lead a multinational force of that kind.

In 1946 he was sworn in as Governor of New South Wales, the first person born in Australia to hold the office. He lived here at Government House for eleven years, among the longest-serving of the state's governors, and twice acted as Administrator of the Commonwealth in the Governor-General's absence.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.image_caption: Sir John Northcott, Governor of NSW

properties.name: Sir Frank Berryman
properties.summary: Frank Berryman was one of the ablest Australian soldiers of the Second World War, a gifted planner who commanded in New Guinea and later ran Eastern Command from Victoria Barracks at Paddington.
body: Frank Berryman built his reputation as a staff officer and planner of rare skill. He served in the desert campaigns in North Africa and Syria, and as a lieutenant-general directed the corps that fought the long, grinding New Guinea battles from Finschhafen to Sio. He was present in Tokyo Bay for the Japanese surrender in 1945.

After the war he was given command of Eastern Command, run from Victoria Barracks at Paddington, a post he held across two periods in the late 1940s and early 1950s. He went on to direct the Commonwealth jubilee celebrations and the 1954 royal tour of Queen Elizabeth II.
On leaving the army he became chief executive of the Royal Agricultural Society of New South Wales, a role he kept until 1961. He died at Rose Bay in 1981.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Australian War Memorial

properties.name: George Barrington
properties.summary: George Barrington was a celebrated Irish pickpocket transported to New South Wales in 1791, who reformed to become chief constable of Parramatta, the thief turned thief-taker.
body: George Barrington was born near Dublin around 1755 and became the most famous pickpocket of his day, a silver-tongued operator who worked the theatres and drawing rooms of England in the guise of a gentleman. In 1790 he was caught lifting a watch and chain at a racecourse and sentenced to transportation. He reached Sydney in 1791 and was set to work at Toongabbie.

His good conduct soon earned him a place guarding the government stores, then a conditional pardon, and in 1796 Governor John Hunter made him chief constable at Parramatta. The notorious thief had become a thief-taker. He also took up land, farming grants at Parramatta and on the Hawkesbury.

Failing health led him to resign the constable's post in 1800, and he died at Parramatta in 1804. The famous couplet often put in his mouth, 'We left our country for our country's good', was almost certainly never his, but it bound his name forever to the convict story.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Britannica

properties.name: Charles E. W. Bean
properties.summary: Charles Bean was the official war correspondent and historian who, more than anyone, shaped how Australians remember the First World War, and who first dreamed up the Australian War Memorial.
body: Charles Bean was the quiet, dogged journalist who shaped how Australians remember war. Chosen as the nation's official correspondent in 1914, he landed at Gallipoli on the morning of the first day and was the only correspondent to stay for the whole campaign, from April until the evacuation in December. He kept reporting even after a bullet caught him in the leg.

After the war he gave more than twenty years to the Official History of Australia in the War of 1914 to 1918. He edited all twelve volumes and wrote six of them himself, nearly four million words in all, much of it at his home Clifton, here at Lindfield. He insisted the history be truthful and unromantic, and that it keep the ordinary soldier at its centre.

Above all it was Bean who dreamed of a great memorial to the fallen. He saw the Australian War Memorial open in 1941 and later chaired its board. He died in 1968 at Concord, the keeper of the Anzac story to the end.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Australian War Memorial

properties.name: Jack Donohoe
properties.summary: Jack Donohoe was a young Dublin-born convict turned bushranger, shot dead near Bringelly in 1830 and remembered ever after as the model for the folk song The Wild Colonial Boy.
body: Jack Donohoe was born in Dublin around 1806 and sent out to New South Wales as a convict. He took to the bush in the late 1820s and led a gang that held up travellers and settlers over a huge stretch of country, from the Bathurst district in the west to the Illawarra in the east and north towards Wollombi.

On the afternoon of 1 September 1830 a party of soldiers and police caught up with the gang in the Bringelly scrub. In the fight that followed Donohoe was shot dead by Trooper Muggleston, aged only about twenty-four, while companions got away.

In death his fame outgrew his short life. Ballads about Bold Jack Donohoe passed from singer to singer, and the most popular of them seems to have shaped The Wild Colonial Boy, whose defiant young hero still carries Donohoe's initials. For decades his name was as well known as Ben Hall's or Ned Kelly's.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.image_caption: Jack Donohoe, sketched after his death, 1830

properties.name: Louisa Collins
properties.summary: Louisa Collins was the last woman executed in New South Wales, hanged at Darlinghurst Gaol in 1889. Her case became a rallying point against capital punishment and for the right of women to sit on juries.
body: Louisa Collins was the last woman hanged in New South Wales, executed at Darlinghurst Gaol on 8 January 1889. She had lived at Botany, where her first husband, Charles Andrews, and then her second, Michael Collins, both died within about eighteen months. The Crown was certain that both men had been poisoned with arsenic.

Convicting her took four trials through 1888. The first three juries could not agree, which troubled much of the colony's legal community, and the case turned on an opened box of 'Rough on Rats', a powder that was almost pure arsenic, and on the testimony of her young daughter, who said she had found the poison in the house before each death. A fourth jury found her guilty of murdering Michael Collins, and she went to the gallows protesting her innocence.

Her execution unsettled even those who believed she was guilty. It became a rallying point in the colony's arguments against the death penalty, and in the campaign to let women sit on juries, since no woman had had any say in judging her. She was buried at Rookwood.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; State Library of NSW

properties.name: Sir Charles 'Fighting Charlie' Cox
properties.role: Light Horse general; railwayman and senator
properties.summary: A Pennant Hills railwayman and citizen soldier who became one of Australia's best-known Light Horse commanders, leading mounted troops from the Boer War through to the long campaign across Palestine.
body: Charles Cox, known to everyone as 'Fighting Charlie', was a Pennant Hills railwayman and weekend soldier. He won his nickname leading mounted riflemen in the Boer War, where two years in the field earned him a name as a spirited and dependable commander.

When the First World War broke out he left the railways and raised the 6th Light Horse Regiment. He took it to Gallipoli, where the light horsemen fought dismounted in the trenches as part of the 2nd Light Horse Brigade. Later, across the Sinai and Palestine, he was given command of the 1st Light Horse Brigade and led it to the end of the war. At Magdhaba in December 1916, caught in the open under Turkish fire, he moved his brigade with a quickness that spared many lives.

Home again, Cox was elected to the Senate as a Nationalist in 1920 and held the seat for New South Wales until 1938, some eighteen years. He died at Croydon in 1944 and was buried with full military honours at Carlingford, in the Hills district where he had been born.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Australian War Memorial

properties.name: Joseph Pawsey
properties.summary: Joseph Pawsey turned wartime radar science toward the sky and led the Sydney group whose cliff-top experiments at Dover Heights helped found radio astronomy, the field he named.
body: Joseph Pawsey led the radio astronomy group inside the CSIR Division of Radiophysics in Sydney after the Second World War. He took the secret radar work of the war years and pointed it at the heavens, helping to open up an entirely new branch of science that he gave its name, radio astronomy.

From a field station on the clifftop here at Dover Heights, south of Sydney Heads, Pawsey and his colleagues aimed war-surplus aerials out over the sea. Early in 1946 he ran his sea interferometer, which used the ocean surface as a mirror. An aerial on the cliff caught the radio waves coming straight from the Sun and the same waves bouncing off the water below, and the two signals combined into a pattern that revealed the size and position of the source. The work tied solar radio bursts to sunspots.

This sea-cliff method was probably Pawsey's most important contribution, and the discoveries that flowed from it put Australia among the leaders of the young science. The ground he broke at Dover Heights led on to the great dishes that followed, including the Parkes radio telescope. He stayed at Radiophysics until his death in 1962.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; CSIRO

properties.name: Robert 'Nosey Bob' Howard
properties.summary: Robert Rice Howard, known as Nosey Bob, was the public executioner of New South Wales for nearly thirty years, an outcast who carried out the colony's hangings and lived apart in a cottage at then-remote North Bondi.
body: From 1876 until his retirement in 1904, Robert Rice Howard was the public executioner of New South Wales, the man who carried out the colony's hangings, most of them at Darlinghurst Gaol. His twenty-eight years on the job made him the longest-serving hangman the colony ever had. He had once been a successful Sydney cabman, but a kick from one of his horses cost him his nose, and the disfigurement, paired with his grim trade, turned him into an outcast.

The society that needed his work would not be seen near him. Howard lived apart in a small four-roomed weatherboard cottage at North Bondi, then a lonely edge of the city, and travelled out to gaols across the colony to do his duty.
He retired in 1904 and died at Bondi in 1906. In him you can see both the cold machinery of capital punishment and the stigma carried by the people who worked it.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Dagmar Berne
properties.place: University of Sydney (first woman to study medicine here)
properties.summary: Dagmar Berne was the first woman to study medicine at the University of Sydney, and after a hostile faculty stopped her finishing there she trained abroad, returned to practise, and opened the door for the women who followed.
body: In 1885 Dagmar Berne enrolled at the University of Sydney and became the first woman to study medicine there. She ran straight into the opposition of the Dean of Medicine, Thomas Anderson Stuart, and others who did not want women in the faculty at all.

Unable to finish her degree in Sydney, Berne completed her training in Britain, studying in London, Edinburgh and Dublin. She came home and in 1895 became the second woman to register with the Medical Board of New South Wales, taking rooms on Macquarie Street.

Her career was painfully short. Tuberculosis took hold, and she moved to the dry inland town of Trundle hoping the climate would help, dying there in 1900 aged only thirty-three. By then she had already forced open the door of Australian medicine for the women who came after her, and the University of Sydney still awards a Dagmar Berne Prize in her name.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography; Dictionary of Sydney

properties.name: Sir Alfred Stephen
properties.role: Chief Justice of NSW; law reformer
properties.place: Supreme Court, King Street (his judicial seat)
properties.summary: Sir Alfred Stephen was Chief Justice of New South Wales for nearly thirty years and a dogged law reformer who narrowed the death penalty and, late in life, finally won reform of the colony's harsh divorce laws.
body: Sir Alfred Stephen presided over the law of colonial New South Wales for almost three decades as Chief Justice, from 1844 to 1873. He remained a force long after he stepped down, sitting in the Legislative Council and serving more than once as the colony's acting Governor.

Cautious but persistent, he worked to narrow the reach of the death penalty and to modernise the courts. His most stubborn campaign was for the reform of divorce, which under colonial law could be ended only on the narrowest of grounds.

Stephen wrote pamphlet after pamphlet defending change, including a reply to the British statesman William Gladstone, and pushed bill after bill through a conservative Council that kept blocking him. His Divorce Amendment and Extension Act finally received royal assent on 6 August 1892, widening the grounds on which an unhappy marriage could be ended. He died in Sydney in 1894.
properties.source: Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.image_caption: Sir Alfred Stephen, Chief Justice of NSW

properties.role: Navigator who charted the east coast
properties.summary: The navigator whose 1770 landfall at Kurnell on Botany Bay first brought the east coast under British eyes.
body: On 29 April 1770 the Endeavour anchored in a wide bay on a coast no British ship had charted, and Lieutenant James Cook stepped ashore at Kurnell. It was the first documented British landing on the east coast of Australia. Two Gweagal men stood on the sand to bar the way. Cook fired a warning shot, then small shot that wounded one of them, and for eight days the strangers filled their boats with water, wood and plants while the local people kept their distance.

Cook first jotted the place down as Sting-Ray Harbour, then settled on Botany Bay for the rich haul of specimens his naturalists gathered there. He charted the coast he claimed as New South Wales and sailed north. Eighteen years later a fleet of convict ships would follow his charts back to this same bay.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; State Library of NSW
properties.name: Captain James Cook
properties.image_caption: Captain James Cook, by Nathaniel Dance, 1775

properties.role: Botanist of the Endeavour; advocate of the colony
properties.summary: Joseph Banks was the wealthy naturalist whose plant collecting gave Botany Bay its name, and whose later advocacy made him the most influential backer of turning that shore into a convict settlement.
body: Joseph Banks sailed with Cook on the Endeavour as the voyage's gentleman naturalist. Over a week at Botany Bay in 1770, he and Daniel Solander gathered so many plants unknown to European science that Cook named the bay for their work. The banksia still carries his name.

Back in London, Banks became the most powerful patron of science in the empire and served as president of the Royal Society for more than four decades. When Parliament weighed where to send its convicts, his evidence and his enthusiasm helped settle the choice on Botany Bay.

His judgement of the place proved shaky. The bay was open and its soils poor, and the First Fleet soon moved north to Sydney Cove. Even so, Banks remained a constant friend to the young colony from afar, fielding requests for seeds, advice and patronage for years.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney
properties.name: Sir Joseph Banks

properties.summary: The French navigator who sailed into Botany Bay just days after the First Fleet, then disappeared without trace on the far side of the Pacific.
body: On 26 January 1788, while the First Fleet was shifting from Botany Bay round to Sydney Cove, two French ships under the Comte de Lapérouse arrived on the bay's northern shore. For roughly six weeks the French made camp on the headland that now bears his name, the suburb of La Perouse, and swapped courtesies with the astonished British. For a moment the two great rivals of the age were neighbours on the same patch of water.

Lapérouse sailed out in March 1788 and was never seen again. His two frigates struck the reefs of Vanikoro, far to the north, and the wrecks were not identified for another forty years. The headland, a monument and his name on the map are all that mark how close Sydney came to being a French story rather than a British one.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney
properties.name: Jean-François de Lapérouse

properties.role: Second Governor of New South Wales
properties.summary: The second governor of New South Wales, a careful chartmaker who mapped the harbour and sent the first platypus home to a disbelieving Britain.
body: John Hunter came out with the First Fleet as a senior officer aboard HMS Sirius, and he had a surveyor's eye. His charts of Port Jackson and the coast were among the first careful maps Europeans made of the place. He went home, then returned in 1795 to take up the colony's top job as its second governor.

He inherited a hard situation. While Arthur Phillip was away, the officers of the New South Wales Corps had taken a firm grip on trade, especially in rum and grain, with men like John Macarthur at the centre of it. Hunter spent much of his term trying to break that hold, and he largely lost the fight.

He was also a genuine naturalist who recorded the colony's puzzling animals. In 1798 he sent a platypus pelt and a sketch back to Britain, where scholars studied the duck-billed creature and suspected someone had stitched together a hoax. Hunter lived to 1821 and rose to vice-admiral in the Royal Navy.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.name: Vice-Admiral John Hunter

properties.role: Third Governor of New South Wales
properties.summary: Sydney's third governor, Philip Gidley King founded the Norfolk Island settlement in 1788 and later backed the colony's first industries and its first newspaper.
body: Philip Gidley King set up the settlement on Norfolk Island in 1788, landing there in March after a hard voyage from Sydney Cove. Twelve years later, in September 1800, he came back to take charge of the whole colony as its third governor.

King tried to rein in the rum trade that ran through the colony like a second currency, cutting spirit imports sharply. He encouraged whaling and sealing, started a small manufactory for canvas, sacking and rope, and in 1803 let the printer George Howe launch the Sydney Gazette, the colony's first newspaper.

The powerful officers of the New South Wales Corps fought him at every turn and slowly wore him down. In August 1806 he handed over to William Bligh, leaving behind a colony whose simmering quarrels would soon boil over into revolt.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.name: Philip Gidley King
properties.image_caption: Governor Philip Gidley King (State Library of NSW)

properties.summary: The reforming governor who tried to rein in the squatters' rush across the interior and who saw the law applied after the Myall Creek massacre.
body: George Gipps governed New South Wales through the turbulent 1840s, when squatters pushed their sheep across the interior far beyond the official limits of settlement. His attempts to make them pay for the Crown land they occupied set off a bitter fight with the pastoral elite, who never forgave him.

Gipps held that the law applied to everyone. The Myall Creek massacre of 1838, in which stockmen murdered a group of unarmed Aboriginal people, led to a trial under his administration. Seven of the killers were hanged. It was the first time white colonists were executed for the murder of Aboriginal people, though it did little to halt the violence on the frontier.
Worn down by the office and by years of conflict over land, money and policy, Gipps left the colony in 1846 and died the following year.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.name: Sir George Gipps

properties.role: Judge-Advocate & chronicler of the First Fleet
properties.summary: David Collins was the First Fleet's judge-advocate, whose two-volume journal remains the fullest account of the colony's founding years.
body: David Collins arrived at Sydney Cove with the First Fleet in 1788 as the colony's first judge-advocate, a job he took on without any legal training. He read out the royal commission that installed Arthur Phillip as governor, presided over the colony's first courts, and doubled as Phillip's secretary. For eight years he watched the settlement scrape through hunger and uncertainty, and he wrote much of it down.

His two-volume Account of the English Colony in New South Wales covers the years from the voyage in 1787 to Governor Hunter's departure, and it stays the essential eyewitness record of the founding. Collins paid close attention to the Eora, and his pages preserve customs and encounters that would otherwise be lost.

He sailed for England in 1796. Years later he returned to the colonies as lieutenant-governor and, after a failed start at Port Phillip, founded Hobart Town on the Derwent in 1804. He died suddenly there in 1810.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney
properties.name: David Collins
properties.image_caption: David Collins, judge-advocate of the First Fleet

properties.summary: Tom Roberts was a leading Australian Impressionist who painted Sydney Harbour from the artists' camp at Mosman, and later put the opening of the first Federal Parliament on canvas.
body: From 1891 Tom Roberts and his friend Arthur Streeton painted in the open air from Curlew Camp, a cluster of tents above Little Sirius Cove in Mosman. They lived rough beneath canvas among a transient, Bohemian crowd of artists, catching Sydney Harbour in shimmering light and helping to found a distinctly Australian school of painting. Roberts stayed at the camp until his marriage in 1896, then moved across the water to Balmain.

In 1901 he was commissioned to paint the opening of the first Federal Parliament, held that May at the Royal Exhibition Building in Melbourne. The vast canvas, known ever after as 'The Big Picture', crowds in 269 individual portraits and took about two and a half years to finish, with sittings sought in Melbourne, Sydney, London and beyond. It remains one of the great records of the new nation's first day.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Art Gallery of NSW
properties.name: Tom Roberts

properties.summary: Sir Russell Drysdale was the Sydney-based painter who gave the drought-stricken Australian outback its haunting modern image.
body: Born in England in 1912, Russell Drysdale grew up in Australia and worked from Sydney from the 1940s. He turned away from soft green pastoral scenes to paint the red and empty interior, with its lonely figures and dead trees. The pictures changed how many Australians pictured their own country.

In 1944 the Sydney Morning Herald sent him through the drought-ravaged west of New South Wales, and his stark drawings of soil erosion brought the disaster home to city readers. Out of that journey came 'The Drover's Wife' in 1945, now held by the National Gallery of Australia, one of the most recognised images in Australian art.

His later paintings, such as 'Sofala' of 1947 in the Art Gallery of New South Wales, confirmed him as one of the most influential Australian artists of the century. He was knighted in 1969 and died in 1981.
properties.source: Art Gallery of NSW; Dictionary of Sydney
properties.name: Sir Russell Drysdale
properties.image_caption: Russell Drysdale, photographed by Max Dupain, 1945

properties.summary: Novelist whose 1941 book 'The Timeless Land' reimagined the founding of Sydney from both shores of the beach.
body: Eleanor Dark was born at Croydon in 1901, the daughter of the writer Dowell O'Reilly, and grew up among books and ideas. As an infant she lived briefly at Burwood before the family moved north. She went on to become one of Australia's finest novelists.

Her 1941 book 'The Timeless Land' retold the first years of the colony through Eora eyes as much as British ones. Bennelong, Arabanoo and Governor Phillip were each given a depth that few earlier writers had attempted, and the story moved between the two camps watching each other across the water.

She wrote it from her home at Katoomba in the Blue Mountains, the house called Varuna, where she lived for most of her adult life. The novel and its two sequels sold well at home and in America, and they shaped how generations of Australians pictured 1788.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.name: Eleanor Dark

properties.summary: A conductor raised in Sydney and trained at the Conservatorium, who left for Europe in 1947 and became one of the world's great opera and orchestral maestros.
body: Charles Mackerras was born in New York in 1925 to Australian parents, who carried him home to Sydney when he was two. He grew up here and trained at the State Conservatorium, where he became professor of oboe at nineteen and principal oboe of the Sydney Symphony Orchestra. In February 1947 he sailed for England to learn his real trade, which was conducting.

From the orchestra pit at Sadler's Wells he worked his way to the front of the world's great opera houses and concert platforms. He is remembered above all for his Janacek and his Mozart, music he played with a scholar's care and a showman's spark, and his Decca recordings of the Czech operas made his name.

He came back often to the city that formed him, including a stint as chief conductor of the Sydney Symphony in the early 1980s. He stayed a living thread between the Sydney Con and the international stage until his death in 2010.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Sydney Symphony Orchestra
properties.name: Sir Charles Mackerras

properties.summary: A Wiradjuri girl from Barellan who learned tennis against a wall and grew into one of the world's finest players, winning Wimbledon twice.
body: Evonne Goolagong was born in 1951 at Barellan in the Riverina, the third of eight children in a Wiradjuri family. She practised by hitting a ball against a wall with a paddle cut from a fruit crate. In 1965 the Sydney coach Vic Edwards heard about her, drove out to see her play, and persuaded her parents to let her move to the city. She boarded with the Edwards family and went to Willoughby Girls High School while she trained.

On the grass of White City and the great courts of the world she became a player of rare grace. She won Wimbledon in 1971, then took it again in 1980, the first mother to win the title in 66 years. She added four Australian Opens and the French, seven singles majors in all.

For Aboriginal Australians and for athletes everywhere she became a figure of quiet pride. In later life she returned again and again to coaching and mentoring young Indigenous players, carrying the game back to the communities she came from.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney
properties.name: Evonne Goolagong Cawley

properties.summary: Ken Rosewall, the Sydney boy they called 'Muscles', built one of the longest great careers in tennis. His major titles spanned nineteen years, from a teenage win in 1953 to the oldest open-era major victory in 1972.
body: Ken Rosewall was born in Sydney in 1934, and his mates handed him the ironic nickname 'Muscles' because of his slight build. He sharpened his famous backhand on the courts of the city's west and at White City in Rushcutters Bay. In 1953, still only eighteen, he won his first Australian singles title.

When he turned professional in 1957 he was shut out of the major championships, which were closed to professionals for years. That ban cost him many titles in his prime, but it did not slow him down on the pro circuit.

Once the open era arrived and the majors reopened, Rosewall came back and kept winning. He took the Australian title again in 1971 and 1972, and at 37 he became the oldest man to win a singles major in the open era, a record that still stands. The span between his first major win and his last reached nineteen years, one of the longest in the game.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney
properties.name: Ken Rosewall

properties.summary: Marion Mahony Griffin was an American-born architect whose luminous drawings helped win the competition to design Canberra, and who with her husband Walter Burley Griffin shaped the bush suburb of Castlecrag on Middle Harbour.
body: Born in Chicago in 1871, Marion Mahony was the second woman to graduate in architecture from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology and the first woman registered to practise architecture in Illinois. She made her name as a draughtswoman of rare skill, and her rendered perspectives of the Griffins' 1911 plan for Canberra are credited with helping the entry take first prize. Those drawings now sit on the UNESCO Memory of the World Register.

She and Walter Burley Griffin came to Australia to work on the new capital, but the Canberra years were bruising and full of official friction. By 1920 they had turned their energy to Sydney, buying around 650 acres of bushland on the shores of Middle Harbour through a development company. They named the estate Castlecrag after a sandstone outcrop the locals called Edinburgh Castle.

At Castlecrag the Griffins built low stone houses that sat among the trees, with shared internal reserves and walking tracks instead of fences. Marion ran the local office, oversaw the drawings and led the cultural life of the place. It was an early experiment in living lightly on the land, and many of those ideas still shape the suburb today.

After Walter died in 1937 she eventually returned to Chicago, where she wrote a sprawling memoir called The Magic of America. She died there in 1961, aged 90, in difficult financial circumstances. Recognition of her own contribution, long folded into her husband's reputation, has grown steadily since.
properties.source: Dictionary of Sydney; Australian Dictionary of Biography
properties.name: Marion Mahony Griffin
